name: Port of Tacoma v. Sacks
holding: Out-of-town travel time for non-exempt employees is compensable door-to-door under Washington law — not just hours within a normal work shift, as federal law allows.
facts: Sacks, a non-exempt employee of the Port of Tacoma, was sent on out-of-town business travel. The Port paid him only for travel time that fell within his normal work hours, following the federal FLSA rule. Sacks sued, arguing that Washington's hours-worked regulation required pay for all travel time, regardless of when it occurred. WA L&I had issued Administrative Policy ES.C.2 in July 2021 taking the same position.
reasoning: The court applied WAC 296-126-002(8), Washington's definition of 'hours worked,' which requires three factors: (1) the employee is authorized or required by the employer, (2) to be on duty, (3) on the employer's premises or at a prescribed workplace. The court found all three met when the Port chose the travel mode, purchased the plane tickets, and required the trip. The court deferred to L&I's interpretation of its own regulation, noting Washington's 'long and proud history of being a pioneer in the protection of employee rights.' The court explicitly rejected the federal rule that limits compensable travel to normal working hours.
application: For Ivy Medical, this is the single most important case for any WA-bound travel. When a travel nurse flies, drives, or rides to a Washington assignment, EVERY hour from their front door to the worksite is compensable — including airport waiting time, layovers (if not bona fide meal periods), and post-flight ground transit. The federal rule (compensable only within normal work hours) does NOT apply when work is performed in WA. This is why the $20/hr travel rate exists: paying clinical rate for 12+ hours of door-to-door transit would be punishing. The travel rate has to clear WA's $17.13 minimum wage (or higher local rates), but otherwise the structure is legal.
dos: Track every hour of WA-bound travel from the employee's front door to the worksite
Pay travel hours at the agreed travel rate (currently $20/hr) per the written wage agreement
Calculate overtime using the weighted-average method when the workweek crosses 40 hours
Apply WA rules to the entire trip when work is performed in WA — even portions through ID or MT
donts: Don't apply the federal rule (within normal work hours only) to any WA work assignment
Don't exclude airport waiting time, transfer time, or 'time between' segments
Don't treat WA travel like Idaho or Montana travel — the rules are fundamentally different
Don't rely on the employee being 'on personal time' during travel — the trip itself is hours worked

name: Idaho Code § 44-1202 (Hours Worked Statute)
holding: Time spent traveling to and from the workplace is not 'hours worked.' Idaho otherwise mirrors the FLSA for travel time and hours-worked questions.
facts: Idaho enacted I.C. § 44-1202 to define 'hours worked' under state wage law. The statute explicitly excludes commute time and certain pre-/post-shift activities (changing clothes, showering, retrieving tools) from hours worked when industry practice has not treated them as paid time. Idaho has not enacted any state-specific rule expanding travel-time compensability beyond the federal FLSA baseline.
reasoning: The Idaho legislature took the federal Portal-to-Portal Act framework and codified it in state law. The deliberate choice not to expand on FLSA leaves Idaho as one of the most employer-friendly states for travel-time analysis. The statute does not address out-of-town travel directly, so the FLSA rules govern — meaning travel as a passenger outside normal work hours is generally not compensable.
application: For Ivy Medical Idaho assignments, the federal rule applies. A travel nurse driving from Boise to an Idaho clinic does NOT have to be paid for the commute. For overnight out-of-town travel within Idaho or to another non-WA state, only travel hours that fall within the employee's normal working hours are compensable when they're a passenger. Time spent driving themselves is always compensable. This is dramatically less expensive than the Washington rule and is the baseline for most of Ivy Medical's domestic-state travel.
dos: Apply the FLSA out-of-town rule for travel within Idaho: paid only within normal work hours when employee is a passenger
Always pay for time the employee spends driving themselves
Pay for travel between worksites during the workday
Document the employee's normal work hours so the analysis is verifiable
donts: Don't pay door-to-door for Idaho travel — that's the Washington rule
Don't assume Idaho law mirrors Washington just because they're neighbors
Don't forget that self-driving is always paid, regardless of the hour

name: 29 CFR § 778.318 (Two-Rate Travel Pay)
holding: Employers may pay non-exempt employees a different (lower) rate for travel time and other 'non-productive' work time, as long as the lower rate is at least minimum wage and the arrangement is agreed to in writing in advance.
facts: The Department of Labor's interpretive regulation under the FLSA addresses how employers can structure pay for time that is compensable but not productive work — including travel time, training time, and waiting time. The regulation allows a separate, lower rate for such time, provided certain procedural requirements are met.
reasoning: DOL recognized that requiring full-rate pay for time spent in transit creates a disincentive to assign travel and inflates labor costs for non-productive hours. The regulation balances that against worker protection by requiring (1) a written agreement signed in advance of the work, (2) a rate that meets or exceeds the applicable minimum wage, (3) proper overtime calculation using the weighted-average method, and (4) accurate separate tracking of the hours at each rate.
application: This regulation is the legal foundation for Ivy Medical's $20/hr travel rate structure. Every travel nurse who is being paid the dual-rate arrangement needs a signed written wage agreement BEFORE any travel takes place. The agreement should state the clinical rate, the $20 travel rate, what qualifies as 'travel time,' and reference 29 CFR 778.318(b). Without that written agreement, the lower rate doesn't hold up in a wage claim. Paylocity needs a separate earn code (e.g., 'TRVL') so travel hours show up separately on pay stubs.
dos: Use a written wage agreement signed by the employee before the first travel assignment
Set the travel rate at or above the applicable state/city minimum wage
Track clinical and travel hours separately in Paylocity with distinct earn codes
Reference 29 CFR 778.318(b) in the wage agreement to make the legal basis clear
donts: Don't implement the dual rate without a written agreement — verbal agreements don't satisfy the regulation
Don't pay below state/local minimum wage for travel hours (WA cities can be $19-22)
Don't lump travel hours into the regular hours total — they must be separately tracked

name: 29 CFR § 778.115 (Weighted-Average OT)
holding: When a non-exempt employee works at two or more different hourly rates in the same workweek, overtime must be calculated using a weighted-average regular rate: total straight-time earnings divided by total hours worked.
facts: DOL's interpretive regulation under FLSA addresses the math problem created by multi-rate pay structures. If an employee earns $50/hr for some hours and $20/hr for others in a week with overtime, the law has to decide which rate (or what blend) determines the overtime premium.
reasoning: The regulation prevents employers from gaming the system by assigning all overtime hours to the lower rate. The weighted-average method ensures the overtime premium reflects the actual mix of work the employee did that week. The math: add all straight-time earnings, divide by total hours worked, that's the regular rate. Overtime premium = 0.5 × regular rate × OT hours. Pay = (straight-time total) + (overtime premium).
application: This is the most common trap in Ivy Medical's dual-rate structure. Cannot pay overtime at 1.5 × $20 for travel hours, even if all the overtime happened to fall during travel. Example: 36 clinical hours at $50 + 14 travel hours at $20 = $2,080 straight-time, ÷ 50 hours = $41.60 regular rate. OT premium = 0.5 × $41.60 × 10 OT hours = $208. Total weekly pay = $2,288. Paying $20 × 1.5 × 10 = $300 instead would underpay by $158 and create wage-claim exposure (potentially doubled under WA, trebled under Idaho). Paylocity must be configured to use the weighted-average method when both earn codes are present in the same week. Verify this in setup, then spot-check pay stubs.
dos: Configure Paylocity to use weighted-average overtime when two earn codes appear in the same week
Audit pay stubs periodically to confirm OT was calculated correctly for mixed-rate weeks
Document the math on at least one example trip per quarter as a sanity check
Train whoever runs payroll review on what weighted-average OT should look like
donts: Don't pay OT at 1.5 × $20 for travel hours when clinical work also occurred that week
Don't assume Paylocity defaults are correct — verify the setting is on
Don't use an 'alternative' OT method (like 1.5x rate-in-effect) without a separate written agreement
Don't ignore mixed-rate weeks because they're 'rare' — those are exactly the audit targets

name: Androckitis v. Virginia Mason Medical Center
holding: Healthcare employers can be liable for both the time worked through a meal break AND an additional 30-minute penalty payment per violation. Doubled if willful. Statute of limitations is 3 years.
facts: Virginia Mason Medical Center had a policy that allowed nurses to clock back in to record interrupted meal breaks but did not automatically compensate them for the missed break itself. The plaintiff and a class of nurses argued they should be paid both for the time they worked during the break AND a 30-minute penalty for the missed break.
reasoning: The WA Court of Appeals construed RCW 49.12.480 (the Healthcare Workplace Improvements Act) strictly. The statute requires uninterrupted meal breaks for direct-patient-care workers and provides two separate remedies: (1) pay for time actually worked during the missed break, AND (2) pay for the 30-minute period itself as a penalty. The court rejected the hospital's argument that paying for the worked time alone satisfied the law. The court also affirmed that willful violations trigger doubling under RCW 49.52.070.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA-based clinical staff, this case sets the exposure ceiling. One missed meal break = up to 60 minutes of pay (30 worked + 30 penalty), doubled to 120 minutes if willful. Over a 3-year lookback for a class of nurses, this adds up fast. If Ivy Medical operates direct-patient-care services in WA, the practical answer is: ensure breaks happen, document any that don't, and pay both the worked time and the penalty automatically. Don't make employees fight for the penalty — it's an exposure multiplier. Coordinate with Paylocity to set up an earn code for missed-break penalty pay.
dos: Track every meal break taken and missed in real time, not after the fact
Auto-pay the missed-break penalty when a break is missed — don't wait for a complaint
Document the unforeseeable emergent or clinical circumstance when a break is interrupted
Train managers that 'we're too busy' is NOT a lawful reason to skip breaks
donts: Don't have a 'breaks are voluntary' or 'employee chose to skip' policy — courts read these as willful
Don't pay only for worked time when a break is missed — that's the Virginia Mason mistake
Don't assume small numbers — 3 years of violations across a clinical team is six-figure exposure
Don't ignore the 'willful' doubling provision — it's the difference between manageable and catastrophic

name: WA HB 1155 of 2019 — Healthcare Workplace Improvements Act
holding: Direct-patient-care healthcare workers in WA are entitled to uninterrupted meal and rest breaks. Breaks may only be interrupted for (1) unforeseeable emergent circumstances or (2) unforeseeable clinical circumstances with significant adverse effect on a patient.
facts: The WA Legislature passed HB 1155 in 2019 to address the well-documented pattern of healthcare workers — particularly nurses — being denied breaks due to chronic understaffing. The bill expanded mandatory overtime protections (RCW 49.28) and added strict meal/rest break requirements for direct-patient-care workers (RCW 49.12.480).
reasoning: The legislature found that habitual interruption of nurse breaks was harming both patient safety and worker retention. The statute defines narrow exceptions — only TRUE emergencies justify interrupting a break, and the employer bears the burden of documenting that an exception applies. Routine 'busy day' staffing pressure is explicitly NOT an emergent circumstance.
application: If Ivy Medical operates direct-patient-care services in WA (clinic, urgent care, hospital department, home health), this statute applies. Every break has to be uninterrupted unless an exception is documented. If Ivy Medical operates only administrative or non-direct-care WA services, this statute does not apply — but WA's general break law (RCW 49.12.187) still requires breaks for all workers. NOTE: The 2019 HB 1155 is different from the 2026 HB 1155 (noncompete ban) — same bill number, totally different topic. Easy source of confusion.
dos: Apply the uninterrupted-break rule to anyone in direct patient care in WA
Document the specific emergent or clinical reason for any interrupted break
Train clinical leads on what does and does not qualify as an exception
Cross-reference with the mandatory-OT ban — same statute family, both apply
donts: Don't treat 'we were short-staffed' as an emergent circumstance — it's a planning failure, not an emergency
Don't apply this rule to non-direct-care WA workers — the general break rule covers them instead
Don't confuse this 2019 HB 1155 with the 2026 HB 1155 (noncompete) — same number, very different law

name: WA L&I Policy HLS.A.2 (Healthcare Labor Standards)
holding: L&I administrative policy implementing the healthcare break rules. Provides the agency's interpretation of when breaks may be interrupted, what counts as documentation, and how penalties calculate.
facts: L&I issued HLS.A.2 as the operational guidance for enforcing the 2019 healthcare break law (RCW 49.12.480) and the 2023 expansion (SB 5236). The policy was updated effective Jan 29, 2026, ahead of the July 1, 2026 deadline when L&I gains authority to issue penalties to hospitals exceeding the missed-break threshold.
reasoning: L&I's policy fills gaps left by the statute: what documentation is needed, how to track threshold compliance, what the missed-break penalty calculation actually looks like, and how staffing plan compliance ties to break enforcement. It also clarifies that when a nurse works through a break, BOTH the worked time AND a 30-minute penalty are owed (codifying the Androckitis holding administratively).
application: If Ivy Medical operates WA healthcare services, this is the practical playbook. Hospital operations (acute care, psychiatric, critical access) face active L&I enforcement starting July 1, 2026. Clinic and non-hospital operations are not subject to L&I hospital enforcement but the RCW 49.12.480 break rules still apply. Keep a copy of HLS.A.2 with the employee handbook; train clinical leads on the documentation standard.
dos: Read the current version of HLS.A.2 — L&I updates it periodically
Use L&I's specified documentation format for interrupted breaks
Build the missed-break penalty into payroll as an automatic earn code
Track the missed-break rate by unit so threshold violations are visible early
donts: Don't rely on outdated versions of the policy — pull the latest from lni.wa.gov
Don't ignore the July 2026 enforcement deadline — penalties can stack quickly
Don't treat L&I guidance as optional — it carries weight in audits and litigation

name: Idaho — No Meal/Rest Break Requirement
holding: Idaho has no state law requiring meal or rest breaks for adult employees. Federal FLSA only requires that any break under 20 minutes be paid; meal breaks of 30+ minutes can be unpaid if the employee is fully relieved of duties.
facts: Unlike Washington, Idaho has chosen not to enact a state-level break requirement. The federal FLSA does not mandate breaks at all for adult workers — it only governs whether time spent on breaks counts as compensable.
reasoning: Idaho's labor framework relies on the FLSA baseline and market practice. The legislature has historically declined to add break requirements, preferring to let employers and employees negotiate.
application: For Idaho clinical staff, Ivy Medical is not legally required to provide breaks at all. As a practical matter, breaks are necessary for clinical safety and retention — but the legal exposure for a missed break in Idaho is essentially zero (no statute to violate, no Androckitis-style remedies). Ivy Medical can set its own break policy in the handbook for ID employees. Just remember: if a break IS provided, time under 20 minutes is paid and time 30+ minutes can be unpaid if the employee is fully relieved.
dos: Set a clear written break policy for ID employees in the handbook
Pay any break under 20 minutes as working time
Keep meal periods of 30+ minutes truly duty-free if treating them as unpaid
Provide breaks as a practical matter even though not legally required
donts: Don't assume Idaho rules apply when sending an Idaho nurse to a WA assignment — WA's rules govern there
Don't make 30-min meal periods unpaid while still requiring on-call response — that's compensable
Don't confuse Idaho's no-break-law with permission to skip federal child labor break rules for minors

name: Montana — No Meal/Rest Break Requirement
holding: Montana has no state law requiring meal or rest breaks for adult employees. Same FLSA-only framework as Idaho.
facts: Montana, like Idaho, has not enacted state break requirements. Federal FLSA governs whether breaks count as compensable time.
reasoning: Same policy choice as Idaho: rely on market and federal baseline.
application: Same as Idaho — Ivy Medical's Montana clinical staff have no statutory break protection. Internal policy governs. The bigger Montana issues are the WDEA termination rules and the wage-claim cure period, not breaks.
dos: Apply your handbook's break policy uniformly across Montana staff
Pay short breaks (under 20 min) as working time per FLSA
Keep meal periods duty-free if treating them as unpaid
donts: Don't assume MT and WA break rules are similar — WA is dramatically stricter
Don't skip breaks entirely for non-clinical safety reasons just because they're not required

name: WA Mandatory OT Ban for Healthcare (RCW 49.28.130-150)
holding: Hospital nurses, LPNs, CNAs, surgical/radiologic/respiratory techs in WA cannot be required to work overtime. Acceptance of overtime must be voluntary. Refusal cannot be grounds for discrimination, dismissal, or any adverse employment action.
facts: WA enacted the original mandatory-OT ban in 2002 for RNs and LPNs in acute care and psychiatric hospitals. The 2019 expansion (HB 1155) added CNAs, surgical techs, radiologic techs, and respiratory care technicians. The 2019 law also added the 8-hour uninterrupted rest requirement after shifts over 12 consecutive hours.
reasoning: The legislature found that mandatory overtime in healthcare correlates with patient safety incidents, medication errors, and worker burnout. The ban incentivizes proactive staffing rather than relying on forced extension of shifts. Exceptions are narrow: unforeseeable emergent circumstances, pre-scheduled on-call time, or when the employer can document reasonable efforts to obtain staffing.
application: Ivy Medical's WA hospital-based staff cannot be required to work past their scheduled shift. Penalties under RCW 49.28.140: civil infractions up to $1,000 each for first three violations; $2,500 for the fourth; $5,000 for each after. For a multi-nurse pattern, this scales to five figures fast. Critical: 'we have a chronic staffing shortage' is NOT a defense — King County paid $10,000 for relying on this exact argument. If Ivy Medical doesn't operate hospitals in WA, this statute doesn't apply directly, but the policy considerations still inform good practice.
dos: Treat all OT acceptance as voluntary for WA hospital clinical staff
Document 'reasonable efforts to obtain staffing' before invoking that exception
Provide 8 consecutive hours of uninterrupted off-duty time after any shift over 12 hours
Track refusals separately so there's never any appearance of retaliation
donts: Don't use chronic understaffing as the reason for required OT — it's the opposite of an emergency
Don't retaliate, discipline, or even subtly disadvantage an employee who refuses OT
Don't apply this to non-hospital WA settings — it's hospital-specific (acute care, psychiatric, critical access)
Don't assume Idaho/Montana rules apply when scheduling WA staff

name: Idaho — Mandatory OT for Nurses Allowed
holding: Idaho has no statute prohibiting mandatory overtime for nurses or other healthcare workers. The FLSA does not prohibit mandatory OT; it only requires time-and-a-half for hours over 40. Employers may require OT subject to that pay obligation.
facts: Idaho is among the 32 states that have not enacted nurse mandatory-OT bans. The 18 states with such bans include WA, OR, CA, and others, but not Idaho.
reasoning: Same employer-friendly framework as Idaho's break-law absence. The legislature has not seen sufficient need to restrict employer staffing decisions.
application: For Idaho clinical staff, Ivy Medical can require overtime — provided OT is paid at time-and-a-half. There's no legal cap on consecutive hours either. As a practical matter, working a nurse past 16 hours raises malpractice and safety concerns even if it's legal. Internal policies should still set reasonable limits even where state law doesn't.
dos: Pay OT at 1.5x the regular rate for hours over 40/week (FLSA minimum)
Set internal shift-length caps even though Idaho law doesn't require them
Document OT consent where possible to head off disputes
donts: Don't apply WA mandatory-OT-ban thinking to Idaho assignments
Don't push consecutive shifts to dangerous lengths just because they're legal
Don't forget that OT must be paid even if not pre-approved

name: Montana — Mandatory OT for Nurses Allowed
holding: Montana has no nurse mandatory-OT ban. Same federal-only framework as Idaho.
facts: Montana follows the FLSA baseline. No state-specific restrictions on healthcare overtime.
reasoning: Same as Idaho.
application: Same operating rule as Idaho: OT can be required, must be paid at time-and-a-half over 40 hours/week. Combined with Montana's good-cause termination rule (WDEA), be careful using OT refusals as a basis for any adverse action — could trigger wrongful discharge exposure if past probation.
dos: Pay OT at 1.5x regular rate for hours over 40/week
Document any disciplinary actions related to OT performance very carefully
Coordinate OT policies with the WDEA good-cause framework after probation
donts: Don't discipline a post-probation MT employee for OT refusal without strong good-cause documentation
Don't push unsafe consecutive shifts
Don't assume MT and WA rules align — they're fundamentally different

name: E.M.D. Sales, Inc. v. Carrera
holding: Employers must prove FLSA exemptions by preponderance of the evidence, not the higher 'clear and convincing' standard. Unanimous decision.
facts: Three sales representatives sued E.M.D. Sales claiming unpaid overtime. The employer defended on the outside-sales exemption. The district court applied the 4th Circuit's 'clear and convincing' standard and ruled for the employees. The 4th Circuit affirmed. SCOTUS took the case to resolve a circuit split — every other circuit had used preponderance.
reasoning: Justice Kavanaugh, writing for a unanimous Court, held that nothing in the FLSA's text designates a heightened standard, the FLSA is not a constitutional rights case requiring heightened proof, and the preponderance standard is the longstanding default for civil litigation. The Court rejected policy arguments that the FLSA's importance to workers required a stricter burden on employers.
application: For Ivy Medical, this lowers the litigation risk when defending an exemption classification. If a nurse-supervisor or administrator is classified as exempt and that classification is challenged, the burden is now preponderance everywhere. Still requires solid documentation of the duties test, salary basis, and salary level — the burden is lower but not eliminated. Practical impact: easier to win, but classification decisions still need to be made carefully.
dos: Document the duties test, salary basis, and salary level for every exempt employee
Review exempt classifications annually as duties evolve
Use written job descriptions that accurately reflect day-to-day work
donts: Don't read this case as a license to be sloppy on classification — burden is lower, not gone
Don't assume the WA salary threshold ($80,168.40 in 2026) matches the federal level — WA is higher
Don't classify clinical staff as exempt based on job title alone — duties test still controls

name: Helix Energy Solutions Group v. Hewitt
holding: Even employees earning over $200,000/year are non-exempt if paid on a daily rate basis rather than a true salary basis. The salary basis test is strict.
facts: Michael Hewitt worked as an offshore tool pusher on an oil rig, earning over $200,000 annually. He was paid a daily rate of $963/day for each day worked, with no guaranteed weekly amount. Helix classified him as exempt under the highly-compensated employee (HCE) exemption. Hewitt sued for unpaid overtime.
reasoning: Justice Kagan, for a 6-3 majority, held that the salary basis test requires a 'guaranteed weekly amount' that doesn't fluctuate based on quantity of work performed. A daily rate, no matter how high, doesn't satisfy this — even when total annual compensation is far above any threshold. The Court read 29 CFR 541.602(a) literally: 'on a weekly, or less frequent basis, a predetermined amount.' Daily-rate pay is by definition not 'weekly or less frequent.'
application: Major implications if Ivy Medical ever uses per-diem stipends, per-shift rates, or daily-rate pay for any worker classified as exempt. The salary basis test requires a fixed weekly amount that doesn't vary with hours, days, or output. If a 'travel nurse premium' or 'per-shift bonus' creates a daily-rate structure for an otherwise-exempt employee, the exemption fails — even if the person earns $200K+. Practical answer: keep salary-basis pay structures truly salary-based; use bonuses and add-ons that supplement, not replace, the weekly guarantee.
dos: Use a true weekly salary for all exempt employees — guaranteed, regardless of days worked
Pay bonuses and per-diems on top of the salary, not in lieu of it
Audit any 'per-diem' or 'shift premium' arrangements for exempt-status compliance
donts: Don't structure pay as a daily rate and call the worker exempt — Helix says no
Don't rely on high total earnings to save a classification — structure matters more than amount
Don't assume a 'per-diem' label is harmless — it can blow up exempt status

name: Encino Motorcars v. Navarro
holding: FLSA exemptions should be given a 'fair reading,' not a narrow one. Overturned decades of precedent (back to A.H. Phillips v. Walling, 1945) requiring narrow construction of exemptions.
facts: Service advisors at a car dealership sued for overtime, arguing they weren't covered by the FLSA's exemption for 'salesmen, partsmen, or mechanics primarily engaged in selling or servicing automobiles.' The 9th Circuit had narrowly construed the exemption to exclude them.
reasoning: Justice Thomas, for the majority, rejected the narrow-construction principle that had governed FLSA exemption cases for over 70 years. The Court held that statutory exemptions should be interpreted using the same neutral tools as any other statute. The shift signals broader deference to employer classifications going forward.
application: For Ivy Medical, this case combines with E.M.D. Sales to create a noticeably more employer-friendly classification landscape. Together they mean: lower burden of proof (preponderance) AND a more neutral interpretive lens. Still doesn't change the underlying duties tests, but disputes about borderline classifications are less hostile territory for employers now.
dos: Make classification decisions based on actual duties — both Encino and the regulations still require that
Document the analysis for any borderline cases
Update classification when duties change materially
donts: Don't read 'fair reading' as 'anything goes' — duties tests still bind
Don't change classification mid-employment without legal review
Don't ignore state-specific exemption rules — WA's salary threshold and duties tests can differ

name: FLSA Nurse Exemption Rules
holding: Registered nurses are generally exempt as 'learned professionals' if paid on a salary basis at or above the threshold ($684/week federal, $1,541.70/week WA in 2026). LPNs are NOT exempt regardless of pay. CNAs and most techs are NOT exempt.
facts: DOL's interpretive regulations under FLSA distinguish between RNs (who qualify for the learned professional exemption) and other healthcare workers. The distinction is based on the educational and licensure requirements for the role.
reasoning: The learned professional exemption requires (1) work requiring advanced knowledge, (2) in a field of science or learning, (3) customarily acquired by prolonged specialized intellectual instruction. RNs satisfy this. LPNs, CNAs, and most technicians complete shorter, more practical training and are explicitly excluded from this exemption by DOL regulation, regardless of their salary level.
application: Major operational implication for Ivy Medical: cannot make LPNs, CNAs, surgical techs, radiologic techs, or respiratory care techs exempt, no matter how much they earn. Even RNs are non-exempt unless paid true salary basis (Helix) at or above the applicable threshold. WA's threshold ($1,541.70/week = $80,168/year in 2026) is much higher than federal ($684/week = $35,568/year). Most of Ivy Medical's clinical staff are properly non-exempt by default. Don't try to 'salary' an LPN or CNA — the classification fails regardless.
dos: Default all clinical staff to non-exempt unless you've specifically analyzed the exemption
Use the higher of federal and state salary thresholds (WA is the relevant ceiling for WA work)
Reclassify LPNs/CNAs/techs as non-exempt regardless of pay level
donts: Don't exempt an LPN, CNA, or tech — DOL regulation explicitly excludes them
Don't apply the federal $684/week to a WA-based RN — WA's number is much higher
Don't assume 'all nurses' are exempt — only RNs at salary basis qualify

name: Anderson v. Mt. Clemens Pottery Co.
holding: When employer records are inadequate, employees may prove hours worked by 'just and reasonable inference.' Burden then shifts to the employer to negate the inference with evidence of the precise hours.
facts: Employees at Mt. Clemens Pottery sued for unpaid 'preliminary activities' time (walking to workstations, prep time). The employer had inadequate records of when employees actually worked.
reasoning: Justice Murphy, writing for a unanimous Court, held that the FLSA requires employers to keep accurate records of hours worked. When employers fail to do so, the employees should not be penalized by having to prove the impossible. The 'just and reasonable inference' standard allows employees to estimate based on memory, patterns, and any available evidence. The burden then shifts to the employer to come forward with precise records — and if they don't have them, they lose.
application: This is THE reason precise time tracking matters for Ivy Medical's travel hours. If a wage dispute arises and the only records show 'travel day' without start/end times, the employee can estimate aggressively under Anderson and the employer has nothing to rebut with. Combined with WA's potential doubling and Idaho's potential trebling, this is how a $3,000 dispute becomes a $30,000 dispute. Paylocity time entries should be granular: 5:30 AM departure from home, 9:15 AM arrival at gate, 11:02 AM wheels up, etc. The case is 80 years old and still the controlling rule on burden-shifting in wage litigation.
dos: Keep granular time records — segment-level for travel, minute-level for clinical work
Audit time records regularly so gaps and errors get caught early
Train employees to log their own travel times in real time rather than reconstructing later
donts: Don't accept vague time entries like 'travel day' — those become Anderson liability waiting to happen
Don't dispose of time records before the statute of limitations runs (3 years WA, 2 years FLSA but 3 if willful)
Don't argue with an employee's reasonable estimate if you have no contemporaneous record to counter it

name: Bostock v. Clayton County
holding: Title VII's prohibition on sex discrimination includes discrimination based on sexual orientation and gender identity.
facts: Three cases consolidated: Bostock (gay county employee fired), Altitude Express (skydiving instructor fired for being gay), Harris Funeral Homes (transgender employee fired after announcing transition). Each employee sued under Title VII.
reasoning: Justice Gorsuch, for a 6-3 majority, applied textualist analysis. Title VII prohibits firing 'because of...sex.' Firing someone for being gay or transgender necessarily involves treating them differently based on their sex — you can't even define 'gay' or 'transgender' without referencing sex. Therefore the firing is 'because of sex,' even if the employer's motivation was framed differently. The intent or motivation of the employer doesn't matter if sex is part of the causal chain.
application: For Ivy Medical's hiring, firing, scheduling, and accommodation practices: sexual orientation and gender identity are protected nationwide under Title VII. This applies in Idaho, Montana, and Washington — including Idaho, where state law historically didn't include these protections. Practical impact: harassment training, anti-discrimination policies, restroom and pronoun policies, dress code applications all must be neutral on sexual orientation and gender identity. WA has even broader state-law protections (RCW 49.60).
dos: Update anti-discrimination policy to explicitly include sexual orientation and gender identity
Train managers on what Bostock changed and what it means in practice
Treat gender identity accommodation requests with the same care as religious or disability requests
donts: Don't assume Idaho's lack of state-level SO/GI protection means you can discriminate — Title VII applies
Don't try to use 'religious objection' as a categorical defense — it's narrower than people think
Don't ignore harassment based on perceived sexual orientation — perception is enough under Title VII

name: Muldrow v. City of St. Louis
holding: A Title VII discrimination plaintiff need only show 'some harm' from a job transfer or reassignment — not 'significant' or 'material' harm. Lower bar than many circuits had applied.
facts: Sgt. Jatonya Muldrow, a female police officer, was transferred from a plainclothes intelligence division to a uniformed patrol division. Her pay didn't change, but her schedule, prestige, and responsibilities did. She sued claiming sex discrimination. Lower courts dismissed because she hadn't shown 'material' harm.
reasoning: Justice Kagan, for the majority, held that Title VII's text prohibits discrimination 'with respect to...terms, conditions, or privileges of employment' — it doesn't require any heightened harm. Some courts had grafted on a 'material' or 'significant' harm requirement that wasn't in the statute. SCOTUS rejected that. Plaintiffs need to show some harm, but not a particular threshold of severity.
application: For Ivy Medical: lateral transfers, schedule changes, role reassignments, and shift changes can now form the basis of a discrimination claim more easily. If a protected-class employee experiences a change in conditions — even one without pay impact — and there's any inference of discriminatory motive, the case can survive a motion to dismiss. Document non-discriminatory reasons for ALL role changes affecting protected-class employees, not just demotions. The administrative threshold for triggering risk is much lower than it used to be.
dos: Document a clean, non-discriminatory rationale for every reassignment, schedule change, or transfer
Train managers that 'lateral' doesn't mean 'safe from discrimination claims'
Treat schedule changes affecting protected-class employees with the same care as terminations
donts: Don't assume pay-neutral changes are immune from Title VII — Muldrow changed that
Don't make assumptions about what employees 'want' (e.g., 'she'd rather be home with kids') — those are evidence of bias
Don't skip documentation for lateral moves — they're now litigation targets

name: Groff v. DeJoy
holding: Title VII requires employers to grant religious accommodations unless doing so would cause 'substantial increased costs' — a much higher bar than the previous 'de minimis' standard.
facts: Gerald Groff, a USPS postal worker and observant Christian, was unable to work Sundays for religious reasons. USPS first accommodated, then assigned him Sunday shifts. He resigned and sued. Lower courts applied the long-standing 'de minimis' standard from TWA v. Hardison (1977), which let employers deny accommodations that imposed almost any cost.
reasoning: Justice Alito, for a unanimous Court, held that the actual statutory text of Title VII says employers must accommodate unless doing so creates 'undue hardship,' which the Court now interprets as 'substantial increased costs.' The 'de minimis' standard from Hardison was a one-line comment that had been over-applied for decades. Now denial requires real, documented, substantial cost.
application: For Ivy Medical: religious accommodation requests (Sabbath observance, prayer breaks, dress code exceptions, dietary needs at facility events, etc.) now require real evidence of substantial cost to deny. 'It's inconvenient' or 'might cause minor scheduling difficulty' is no longer enough. Healthcare adds complexity because patient care can't stop — but the analysis must be specific and documented. Other staff coverage, swap arrangements, and modified schedules need to be considered before denial.
dos: Engage in a documented interactive process for every religious accommodation request
Quantify the actual cost or operational impact before denying — vague concerns don't satisfy Groff
Train managers to escalate religious accommodation requests rather than denying at the line level
donts: Don't deny religious accommodation requests based on 'any cost' — that's the old standard
Don't apply different standards to different religions — disparate treatment claims compound the risk
Don't ignore creative solutions (shift swaps, voluntary coverage) before claiming undue hardship

name: WA Equal Pay & Opportunities Act
holding: Employers with 15+ employees must include wage scale or salary range, plus a general description of benefits and other compensation, in every job posting. Salary history inquiries are prohibited. Penalties $100-$10,000 per violation.
facts: WA EPOA originally enacted in 2018 (banning salary history inquiries) and expanded effective January 2023 (job posting requirements). The law amends RCW 49.58 to require pay transparency in postings.
reasoning: Pay transparency reduces gender and racial wage gaps by enabling informed negotiation. Salary history bans prevent past inequities from carrying forward into new positions.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA recruitment: every job ad must include the pay range. This applies to ads on Indeed, LinkedIn, hospital job boards, anywhere — even if the role can be performed remotely from outside WA, if it could be performed in WA. Range must be a 'good faith' range, not 'up to $200K' or a 200% spread. Cannot ask applicants about salary history. Can ask about pay expectations. Penalties have teeth — up to $10,000 per violation if intentional. WA AG's office has been active enforcing.
dos: Include a good-faith pay range and benefits description in every WA-applicable job posting
Ask about pay expectations, not pay history
Audit existing job ads for compliance before new openings post
Coordinate with recruiting platforms to ensure WA-flagged postings include the required information
donts: Don't post a range like '$50K-$200K' — that's not good faith
Don't ask applicants what they made at their last job
Don't assume non-WA-based recruiters know the rule — train them
Don't omit benefits — the statute requires a description

name: Pregnant Workers Fairness Act (PWFA)
holding: Employers with 15+ employees must provide reasonable accommodations for known limitations related to pregnancy, childbirth, or related medical conditions, unless doing so would cause undue hardship.
facts: Federal PWFA enacted in December 2022, effective June 27, 2023. Closed gaps in Pregnancy Discrimination Act (which required treating pregnant workers like other 'similar in ability' workers) and ADA (which required disability-level impairment).
reasoning: Pregnancy frequently requires accommodation that doesn't meet the ADA 'disability' threshold — breaks for nausea, lifting restrictions, time off for prenatal visits, modified duties, schedule changes. The Act creates a standalone accommodation right for any pregnancy-related limitation.
application: For Ivy Medical: pregnant clinical staff are entitled to accommodation — modified lifting requirements, additional break time, schedule changes for prenatal care, leave for childbirth recovery, etc. The interactive process is mandatory. Denial requires documented undue hardship. Doesn't preempt state law — WA, ID, and MT each have additional pregnancy-related leave or accommodation provisions that may stack.
dos: Engage in the interactive accommodation process the moment a pregnancy-related limitation is disclosed
Document the accommodation request and the analysis
Look for modified duties, equipment changes, and schedule flexibility before claiming hardship
Coordinate PWFA with FMLA (federal) and WA PFML (paid)
donts: Don't require an ADA-level impairment before accommodating — PWFA's threshold is lower
Don't force pregnant employees onto leave when modified duties would work
Don't ignore lifting, standing, or schedule restrictions because they're 'temporary' — temporary accommodations are required too

name: FMLA (Family and Medical Leave Act)
holding: 12 weeks unpaid job-protected leave per year for serious health condition, birth/adoption/foster placement, or care for family member. Up to 26 weeks for military caregiver leave. Applies to employers with 50+ employees within 75 miles.
facts: Foundational federal employment leave law. Employer threshold: 50+ employees within 75 miles. Employee threshold: 12 months of service + 1,250 hours in the prior 12 months.
reasoning: Congress recognized that workers need time off for serious health events and family additions, and that the absence of such protection particularly burdened women. FMLA balances job protection with employer manageability via the 50-employee threshold.
application: For Ivy Medical: check the headcount per location. The 'within 75 miles' rule is location-specific, not company-wide. Cross-state operations may not aggregate — a small WA clinic separated from a larger ID footprint might not be FMLA-covered. Most multi-state healthcare operations cross the threshold somewhere. Carefully run FMLA eligibility for each requesting employee: 12 months of service AND 1,250 hours in the prior 12 months. Travel nurses with patchy hours may not qualify.
dos: Run the 50-employee/75-mile analysis per location
Track employee hours so FMLA eligibility can be determined accurately
Coordinate FMLA with WA PFML (run concurrently when both apply)
Designate FMLA leave properly using DOL forms
donts: Don't assume small WA presence is FMLA-exempt — it might still qualify if combined with nearby locations
Don't deny FMLA before running the eligibility math
Don't pay FMLA leave unless company policy provides — FMLA itself is unpaid (use PTO/PFML for pay)

name: WA Paid Family & Medical Leave
holding: WA employees who work 820+ hours in the qualifying period are entitled to up to 12 weeks of paid leave (16-18 weeks for combined family/medical leave). Funded by employer and employee premiums. Job protection applies to employers with 50+ employees.
facts: WA enacted PFML in 2017, benefits began January 2020. Premiums are split between employer and employee, withheld through payroll. Benefits are paid by the WA Employment Security Department, not the employer directly.
reasoning: WA followed CA and NJ in creating a paid leave program funded by payroll premiums. The program socializes the cost of leave across the employer pool while providing actual income replacement to leave-takers.
application: For ANY Ivy Medical WA-located employee, premiums must be withheld and remitted — even for very small employers. Job protection (the right to return to the same or comparable position) applies only at 50+ employees. The benefit is administered by ESD, so the employer's role is mostly premium collection and proper coordination with FMLA. Critical: PFML runs CONCURRENTLY with FMLA when both apply — don't treat them as sequential. Premium rate adjusts annually (around 0.6-0.9% of wages typically).
dos: Withhold PFML premiums for every WA employee (verify Paylocity is configured)
Remit premiums on the schedule ESD requires
Provide the required PFML poster and new-hire notice
Run FMLA and PFML concurrently when both apply
donts: Don't skip PFML premium withholding because the employee is part-time or temporary — all WA workers are covered
Don't treat PFML and FMLA as separate leave periods — they run together
Don't promise PFML benefits — ESD administers and decides eligibility

name: WA Paid Sick Leave (RCW 49.46.210)
holding: Every WA employer must provide paid sick leave at the rate of at least 1 hour for every 40 hours worked. Applies to ALL employees — full-time, part-time, temporary, seasonal. No headcount minimum. Eligibility to USE accrued leave begins on the 90th day of employment.
facts: WA voters passed Initiative 1433 in November 2016, taking effect January 2018. Among other provisions, it created universal paid sick leave for WA workers.
reasoning: The initiative addressed the gap between FMLA (50+ employees, unpaid) and the reality that most low-wage and part-time workers had no access to any paid sick time. WA chose to require it universally.
application: For every Ivy Medical WA-located employee — full-time, part-time, per-diem, travel — paid sick leave must accrue at 1 hour per 40 worked. This is in ADDITION to PFML. Paylocity must be set up for proper accrual tracking and pay-stub disclosure. Carryover: minimum 40 hours per year carries forward. Usage triggers at 90 days of employment. Sick leave can be used for the employee's own illness, family member illness, domestic violence (per the DVLA), or public health closures. Cannot retaliate against use.
dos: Accrue paid sick leave at 1 hr per 40 worked for every WA employee, every hour
Show accrual on pay stubs (WA requires it)
Allow use starting day 90 of employment
Carry over a minimum of 40 hours per year
donts: Don't restrict sick leave to full-time employees — all WA workers accrue
Don't require documentation for absences under 3 days
Don't retaliate against employees who use accrued sick leave
Don't forget travel nurses and per-diems — they accrue on WA-worked hours too

name: Montana Wrongful Discharge from Employment Act (WDEA)
holding: Montana is the only state without at-will employment after the probationary period. Termination of an employee past probation must be for 'good cause' — meaning reasonable job-related grounds based on a failure to satisfactorily perform job duties, disruption of operations, or other legitimate business reason. Default probationary period is 12 months unless shortened by employer policy.
facts: MT enacted WDEA in 1987 as a compromise — limiting common-law wrongful discharge claims while creating a statutory remedy. Damages include up to 4 years of lost wages plus benefits, with mitigation required. Reinstatement is also a possible remedy.
reasoning: Montana broke with the at-will doctrine universal in other states. The legislature found that pure at-will employment was unfair to long-tenured employees and that a structured wrongful discharge regime was preferable to expanding common-law claims unpredictably.
application: For Ivy Medical Montana employees past probation, this is the single biggest legal difference from ID/WA. Cannot terminate without documented good cause. Performance issues need a paper trail. The probationary period defaults to 12 months — Ivy Medical should set a shorter one in writing if a shorter trial period is desired (typically 6 months). Without a written probationary policy, the default 12 applies. After probation, terminations need: (1) documented performance issues OR legitimate business reason, (2) consistent application of policy, (3) proper progressive discipline (warning, opportunity to correct), (4) no retaliation/discrimination indicators.
dos: Set a written probationary period for all new MT hires — default 12 months otherwise
Document every performance issue with dates, specifics, and the employee's response
Use progressive discipline: verbal → written → final written → termination
Get attorney review for any post-probation MT termination before action
Apply policies consistently — disparate treatment compounds WDEA exposure
donts: Don't fire a post-probation MT employee without documented good cause
Don't skip progressive discipline thinking 'they know what they did wrong'
Don't assume at-will employment language in a handbook trumps WDEA — it doesn't
Don't terminate without considering reinstatement risk — judges can order it
Don't rely on a job description alone — actual performance documentation is what matters

name: WA Final Paycheck Rule (RCW 49.48.010)
holding: WA final paychecks are due by the end of the pay period in which termination occurs (whether quit or fired). Unpaid wages can trigger double damages under RCW 49.52.070 if willfully withheld.
facts: WA's final paycheck rule is more lenient on timing than Montana (immediate if fired) and Idaho (10 days max). But the willful-withholding doubling provision under RCW 49.52.070 is brutal — designed to deter employers from holding back final pay over disputes.
reasoning: The legislature chose to give employers reasonable processing time but added a strong deterrent against using final paychecks as leverage in disputes. Doubling is a powerful remedy.
application: For Ivy Medical WA terminations: pay all wages owed by end of the pay period. Include accrued vacation if company policy provides for payout. Don't withhold over disputes about returned equipment, training repayment, or anything else without specific legal authority. If a wage dispute is real, pay what's clearly owed and litigate the disputed portion separately. Withholding to leverage is the recipe for doubled damages.
dos: Pay all undisputed wages by end of the pay period when terminating
Include accrued vacation if company policy provides payout
Document any withholdings with specific written authority (court order, valid deduction agreement)
Coordinate with Paylocity to ensure final-paycheck timing
donts: Don't withhold the final paycheck to enforce equipment return — get the equipment back separately
Don't deduct training costs or 'damages' without prior signed authorization specific to that deduction
Don't 'forget' to pay accrued vacation if policy provides for it — that's textbook willful withholding

name: Idaho Wage Claim Act
holding: Idaho Wage Claim Act allows treble damages for willfully withheld wages. Good-faith disputes don't trigger trebling. Final paycheck due earlier of next payday or 10 days (excl. weekends/holidays), with 48-hour acceleration on written request.
facts: Idaho's wage protection framework includes penalty provisions and procedural rules for wage claims. The treble damages provision is one of the strongest in the country for willful violations.
reasoning: Idaho took a harsh approach to willful wage theft while preserving employer defenses for good-faith disputes. The 3x multiplier is designed to deter intentional underpayment.
application: For Idaho-based Ivy Medical staff: pay carefully and document any wage decisions. The 'willful' threshold is the key defense — if there's a genuine, documented dispute about the rate or hours, trebling typically doesn't apply. But sloppy recordkeeping (see Anderson v. Mt. Clemens) can make a good-faith dispute look willful. Treble exposure makes Idaho wage claims potentially more expensive than WA or MT.
dos: Document any wage dispute thoroughly so 'willful' is harder to prove
Pay undisputed wages by the deadline even if part is disputed
Respond promptly to wage inquiries — silence looks willful
Honor written 48-hour requests for accelerated final pay
donts: Don't ignore wage claims hoping they go away — treble damages compound
Don't make pay decisions without a documented basis
Don't assume the FLSA limits override Idaho's more generous treble provision

name: Wright v. Home Depot U.S.A., Inc.
holding: Treble damages under the Idaho Wage Claim Act require a 'willful' withholding. Good-faith disputes — even if the employer is ultimately wrong — don't trigger trebling.
facts: Home Depot employee sued for unpaid wages and sought treble damages. The Idaho Supreme Court clarified when the treble multiplier applies.
reasoning: The court interpreted 'willful' to mean a knowing, deliberate refusal to pay wages owed, not a mere mistake or disagreement about what was owed. Documentation of the employer's reasoning matters. An employer who can show a reasonable basis for the disputed pay decision avoids trebling, even if ultimately ordered to pay.
application: For Ivy Medical, this case is the 'how to avoid trebling' guide for Idaho wage disputes. Document the basis for every pay decision. When an employee challenges a paycheck, respond in writing with the rationale. Pay the undisputed portion immediately. Litigate the disputed portion separately. The pattern of behavior matters — an employer who pays carefully and responds in good faith almost never gets hit with trebling, even on losses.
dos: Document the basis for every pay decision that could be disputed
Respond in writing to wage inquiries with specific reasoning
Pay undisputed portions immediately, litigate disputed portions separately
Train payroll staff to flag potential wage disputes for management review
donts: Don't ignore wage complaints — silence looks willful
Don't make pay decisions verbally — written rationale protects against trebling
Don't assume small dollar disputes are harmless — trebling on $2,000 = $6,000

name: Montana Wage Protection Act
holding: Montana requires payment of all wages due at termination — immediately if employer-initiated, by next regular payday or 15 days if employee-initiated. Penalty up to 110% of unpaid wages for late final payments.
facts: Montana's wage payment framework includes some of the strictest timing rules in the country, particularly for involuntary terminations.
reasoning: MT chose to align with its broader employee-protective framework (cf. WDEA) by making final paychecks immediate when the termination is at the employer's initiative. This eliminates the possibility of using final pay as leverage.
application: For Ivy Medical MT terminations: plan around payroll cycles or be ready to cut a same-day check. If you fire a Montana employee on Monday and the next payroll runs Friday, the law requires Monday payment — payroll cycle is no excuse. The 110% penalty is automatic on late payment. Coordinate with Paylocity to be able to generate on-demand checks for MT terminations.
dos: Schedule MT employer-initiated terminations when payroll can issue same-day pay
Pre-authorize on-demand check capability in Paylocity for MT use
Include all accrued wages, OT, and policy-required vacation in the final check
Document the time of termination for audit trail
donts: Don't terminate a MT employee on a payroll-cycle gap if you can't cut a same-day check
Don't 'process with next payroll' for MT involuntary terminations — that's the textbook violation
Don't forget the 110% penalty is automatic, not discretionary

name: Montana Wage Claim Cure Period (MCA 39-3-206)
holding: Before a Montana employee can sue for unpaid wages, they must submit a written wage claim and give the employer an opportunity to cure (typically 30 days). If the employer pays in full during the cure period, certain penalties are waived.
facts: Montana created this cure provision to reduce wage litigation by giving employers a chance to fix errors voluntarily. Many employers miss the cure window because they don't recognize the notice when it arrives.
reasoning: The legislature wanted to encourage voluntary correction over litigation, while preserving employee remedies if the employer doesn't act.
application: Critical procedural rule for Ivy Medical's Montana operations. If a current or former Montana employee sends a wage claim notice (often informal — 'I think I'm owed for...'), RESPOND PROMPTLY. Treat anything that looks like a wage claim as triggering the cure window. Paying in full during the cure period dramatically reduces exposure. Ignoring or delaying triggers full penalties under MCA 39-3-206 (up to 110% of unpaid wages) and waives this protection. Train HR/payroll to recognize and escalate wage claims immediately.
dos: Treat ANY written communication that mentions unpaid wages as a wage claim
Calculate the disputed amount within 7 days and respond in writing
If the claim has merit, pay in full during the cure period (30 days)
Document the cure response — it's the defense to future trebling claims
donts: Don't ignore informal wage complaints from Montana employees — they can ripen into formal claims
Don't let the cure window expire while internal review continues — get an extension or pay
Don't assume an employee's vague complaint isn't a 'real' wage claim

name: WA Noncompete Ban (HB 1155 of 2026)
holding: Effective June 30, 2027: ALL employment noncompetition agreements for WA-based workers are void and unenforceable. Includes employees and independent contractors regardless of pay. Existing noncompetes also unenforceable; employers must notify workers by Oct 1, 2027.
facts: WA Gov. Bob Ferguson signed Engrossed Substitute HB 1155 on March 23, 2026, replacing the earlier income-threshold-based noncompete framework (which had set the 2026 enforceability threshold at $126,858 for employees, $317,147 for contractors). The new law is far broader.
reasoning: The legislature found that even the income-threshold approach was insufficient to protect worker mobility and wage growth. WA joins CA, MN, ND, OK, and a growing list of states with near-total noncompete bans. The legislature explicitly stated the prior law 'did not go far enough.'
application: For Ivy Medical: cannot bind any WA-based clinical staff to noncompete agreements after June 30, 2027. Use these alternatives instead: nonsolicitation (limited to current customers/clients, max 18 months, narrowly drafted), confidentiality, IP/invention assignment, trade-secret protection. Educational expense repayment agreements are allowed only if (a) the obligation expires within 18 months of start, (b) is prorated, and (c) is released for 'good cause' separations. The new definition of 'noncompetition covenant' is liberally construed and includes forfeiture-for-competition clauses. Before June 30, 2027, the income-threshold law (RCW 49.62) still applies — $126,858 minimum for 2026.
dos: Audit all existing WA employee noncompetes — they'll be unenforceable June 30, 2027
Send written notice to current and former WA employees with active noncompetes by Oct 1, 2027
Shift to nonsolicitation, confidentiality, and IP-protection agreements going forward
Review any 'training repayment' or 'sign-on bonus repayment' clauses for 18-month and prorated compliance
donts: Don't enforce or threaten to enforce any WA noncompete after June 30, 2027 (penalty: $5K + attorney fees)
Don't draft new WA noncompetes — they'll be void on signing after the effective date
Don't confuse this 2026 HB 1155 with the 2019 HB 1155 (healthcare breaks) — same bill number, totally different topic
Don't try to use 'sale of business' carveouts to bind W-2 employees — that exception is narrow

name: Montana Noncompete Statute (MCA 28-2-703)
holding: Any contract restraining a person from exercising a lawful profession, trade, or business is void, except in narrow sale-of-business and partnership-dissolution scenarios.
facts: Montana has been one of the most noncompete-hostile states for decades. The statute predates most modern noncompete reform but reaches the same result.
reasoning: Montana's policy preference is worker mobility. The legislature has consistently rejected efforts to expand noncompete enforceability.
application: For Ivy Medical Montana staff: employment noncompetes are essentially unenforceable. Rely on confidentiality agreements, narrow nonsolicitation (current customers only, short duration), and trade-secret protection under MUTSA. Same baseline applies as WA will have post-2027 — Montana just got there first.
dos: Use confidentiality and nonsolicitation agreements instead of noncompetes for MT staff
Limit nonsolicitation to specific current customers, max 12-18 months
Rely on Montana Uniform Trade Secrets Act for IP protection
donts: Don't draft any noncompete for a MT employee — it's void on signing
Don't attempt to enforce a noncompete signed in another state against a MT employee — choice of law usually defaults to MT
Don't confuse this with the sale-of-business exception, which is narrow

name: Blaskiewicz v. Spine Institute of Idaho
holding: Idaho non-competes on physicians are not per se illegal, but courts must weigh employer interests against patient access to care. The 2008 statute (I.C. § 44-2701 et seq.) requires the restriction to be no greater than reasonably necessary.
facts: Dr. Blaskiewicz, a physician at Spine Institute of Idaho, had a non-compete preventing him from practicing in defined areas after departure. The case asked whether the 2008 Idaho non-compete statute superseded older case law (Intermountain Eye & Laser Centers v. Miller, 2005) protecting patient choice of physician.
reasoning: The Idaho Supreme Court held the 2008 statute and Intermountain Eye are consistent. Non-competes must be reasonable in duration, geography, and scope, AND must not impose a greater restraint than necessary to protect legitimate business interests. The court found that restricting patient access to care is NOT a 'legitimate business interest' — so a physician non-compete that effectively denies patient access can still be unenforceable, even under the 2008 framework.
application: If Ivy Medical ever employs physicians or NPs in Idaho under non-competes, the agreement must be narrowly tailored. Courts will refuse enforcement if patients lose access to needed care. For nurses, the same reasonableness framework applies (duration, geography, scope) — though the patient-access analysis is most acute for physicians and primary providers. Under I.C. § 44-2704: 18-month presumption of reasonableness; longer terms need additional consideration.
dos: Tailor any healthcare non-compete to the actual service area, max 18 months
Document a legitimate business interest other than 'preventing patient access'
Consider whether the geographic scope leaves patients with reasonable alternative providers
donts: Don't use 'state-wide' or 'within 100 miles' for a healthcare non-compete — too broad
Don't draft over 18 months without additional consideration
Don't ignore the patient-access factor — courts have refused enforcement on this ground

name: Idaho Non-Compete Framework (I.C. § 44-2701 to 2704)
holding: Non-competes in Idaho enforceable only if reasonable as to duration, geography, and scope. 18-month rebuttable presumption of reasonableness. Restrictions over 18 months require additional consideration.
facts: Idaho's 2008 non-compete statute set parameters for what makes a non-compete enforceable. The 'key employee' framework under § 44-2704 creates a rebuttable presumption of reasonableness.
reasoning: Idaho took a middle path between Montana (largely banned) and traditional employer-friendly states. The 18-month cap with extra consideration for longer terms balances employer protection with worker mobility.
application: For Ivy Medical Idaho non-competes (if used): cap at 18 months unless willing to pay additional consideration. Limit geographically to actual service areas. Limit scope to the line of business the key employee performed. For most clinical staff, non-competes are probably more trouble than they're worth — nonsolicitation and confidentiality are more enforceable and less controversial.
dos: Cap Idaho non-competes at 18 months
Limit geography to actual areas the employee worked
Limit scope to the specific line of business
Provide additional consideration for longer terms
donts: Don't draft Idaho non-competes longer than 18 months without specific extra consideration
Don't apply Idaho non-compete law to WA or MT staff — different rules entirely
Don't assume the rebuttable presumption guarantees enforcement — courts still scrutinize

name: WA Silenced No More Act
holding: Employers cannot require non-disclosure or non-disparagement clauses that prevent employees from disclosing illegal conduct (including discrimination, harassment, retaliation, wage theft, sexual assault). Applies to settlement agreements, severance agreements, employment contracts, and policies. Existing NDAs that purport to cover these areas are unenforceable.
facts: WA's Silenced No More Act, enacted in 2022, was part of the broader #MeToo legislative response. Modeled on similar laws in CA and NY.
reasoning: Confidentiality agreements that prevent disclosure of illegal conduct enable continued harm. The Act preserves NDA enforceability for legitimate purposes (trade secrets, confidential client information) while carving out disclosure of unlawful acts.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA workforce: review all NDAs, severance agreements, employment contracts, and handbook confidentiality clauses. Cannot require employees to keep illegal conduct confidential. Cannot retaliate for disclosure of illegal conduct. Cannot enforce existing NDAs that cover this territory. Settlement agreements for harassment/discrimination claims have specific requirements (employee can still discuss the underlying facts even after settling).
dos: Audit existing NDAs and severance agreements for Silenced No More compliance
Carve out 'illegal conduct disclosure' explicitly in new confidentiality clauses
Train HR on what employees CAN disclose, even after signing an NDA
donts: Don't try to enforce a broad NDA against an employee disclosing harassment, discrimination, or wage theft
Don't retaliate against employees who disclose illegal conduct — that's its own claim
Don't include non-disparagement clauses that prevent factual disclosure of illegal acts

name: Epic Systems Corp. v. Lewis
holding: Employment arbitration agreements with class-action waivers are enforceable under the Federal Arbitration Act.
facts: Multiple companies had arbitration agreements requiring individual (not class) arbitration of employment claims. Employees sued to invalidate the class waivers, arguing they conflicted with the NLRA's protected concerted activity provisions.
reasoning: Justice Gorsuch, writing for a 5-4 majority, held that the FAA's strong policy favoring arbitration enforces class waivers, and the NLRA does not override this. Individual arbitration is a valid contract term that courts must enforce.
application: For Ivy Medical: arbitration agreements with class waivers can significantly reduce wage-and-hour litigation exposure. If employees can only bring individual arbitrations, large class actions are foreclosed. Trade-offs: individual arbitration costs add up; employees may be MORE willing to bring small claims; certain claims (sexual harassment post-Silenced No More in WA) cannot be forced into arbitration. Before implementing, weigh the costs and consult counsel.
dos: Evaluate whether arbitration with class waivers fits Ivy Medical's risk profile
Draft arbitration agreements with attorney review — small details matter for enforceability
Carve out sexual harassment claims in WA per Silenced No More requirements
donts: Don't impose arbitration without considering the volume of individual claims it may trigger
Don't enforce arbitration of sexual harassment claims in WA — Silenced No More overrides
Don't assume arbitration is always pro-employer — small individual cases can still bite

name: WA Hospital Staffing Committee Law (RCW 70.41.420)
holding: WA hospitals must form staffing committees with ≥50% direct-care nursing staff (non-supervisory), file staffing plans annually with DOH, document any patient-unit assignments out of compliance with the plan, and report monthly when under 80% compliance. DOH penalties up to $10,000 per 30-day non-compliance period.
facts: WA expanded its 2008 nurse staffing committee law dramatically via SB 5236 in 2023, adding enforcement teeth via DOH oversight. The first hospitals were fined in 2024 for late charter submission (e.g., MultiCare Deaconess $15,500).
reasoning: WA found that voluntary staffing committees without enforcement teeth were not changing actual staffing practices. The 2023 law added mandatory documentation, DOH oversight, and meaningful penalties.
application: Only applies if Ivy Medical operates an acute care or psychiatric hospital licensed under Ch. 70.41 or 71.12. Does NOT apply to clinics, nursing homes, or birthing centers. If Ivy Medical grows into hospital operations in WA, this becomes a major compliance area: staffing committee, annual plan, monthly compliance reporting starting Jan 31, 2026 (semiannual). Active enforcement underway.
dos: Verify whether any Ivy Medical WA operation falls under Ch. 70.41 or 71.12 licensing
If a hospital, form the staffing committee with the required 50% direct-care representation
File the annual staffing plan with DOH on time
Track monthly compliance against the staffing plan
donts: Don't apply this to non-hospital WA operations — it's hospital-specific
Don't ignore the deadlines — fines have already been issued
Don't appoint the committee with mostly management — 50% must be direct-care, non-supervisory

name: WA Domestic Violence Leave Act (RCW 49.76)
holding: WA employers must provide reasonable leave (paid or unpaid) for employees who are victims of domestic violence, sexual assault, or stalking — or whose family members are victims — to seek medical care, counseling, legal services, or relocation. Paid sick leave may be used.
facts: WA enacted RCW 49.76 in 2008. Unlike FMLA, it has NO headcount minimum — applies to every WA employer regardless of size.
reasoning: Domestic violence victims often face job loss when they need time off for safety planning, court appearances, or medical care. The Act preserves employment while victims address their safety.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA workforce (even very small): cannot retaliate, terminate, or discriminate against an employee who takes DV leave or requests safety accommodations. Records are confidential. Healthcare staff are statistically more likely to encounter this — both as victims and through patient/colleague disclosures. Coordinate with paid sick leave (RCW 49.46.210) which can be used for DV reasons. Train managers on confidentiality and supportive response.
dos: Apply this rule to every WA employee — no headcount minimum
Keep all DV leave requests and records confidential
Train managers on supportive (non-prying) response to DV disclosures
Coordinate with paid sick leave for paid coverage
donts: Don't ask invasive questions about the DV situation — minimal verification only
Don't disclose DV leave to coworkers or supervisors without need-to-know
Don't refuse leave because the employer is small — this law has no headcount floor

name: WA Day of Rest Law (RCW 49.12.108)
holding: Mercantile establishment employees cannot be required to work seven consecutive days; must have at least 24 consecutive hours off in each calendar week. Does NOT apply to healthcare facilities.
facts: Old WA statute from 1973 governing retail/mercantile workplaces. Healthcare is explicitly excluded.
reasoning: Originally aimed at department stores and similar retail. Not extended to healthcare because of the 24/7 nature of patient care.
application: Not directly applicable to Ivy Medical clinical staff. Worth knowing because some scheduling software flags this rule and creates false alarms for healthcare schedulers. If Ivy Medical adds retail/office support roles in WA (e.g., a billing office), the rule could apply to those roles.
dos: Recognize the rule exists in scheduling tools but ignore the flag for clinical roles
Apply the rule to any non-clinical retail/mercantile WA staff if added
donts: Don't apply this to nurses or other healthcare workers — they're excluded
Don't let scheduling software false alerts drive staffing decisions

name: Idaho Right-to-Work (I.C. § 44-2001)
holding: Employees cannot be required to join a union or pay union dues/fees as a condition of employment. Union security clauses are void in Idaho.
facts: Idaho enacted right-to-work in 1985, joining the (now majority) of states with this framework.
reasoning: Idaho's policy preference is voluntary unionism. The federal NLRA still applies but does not preempt right-to-work statutes.
application: For Idaho-based Ivy Medical staff: cannot be required to join a union or pay fair-share fees. WA and MT are NOT right-to-work states — different rules apply if Ivy Medical staff in those states unionize. Federal NLRA Section 7 still applies in all three states (protected concerted activity, even in non-union workplaces).
dos: Understand that Idaho staff have no obligation to fund unions even if a union represents the workplace
Recognize that WA and MT staff CAN be required to pay agency fees under contract
Coordinate with labor counsel if any unit becomes unionized
donts: Don't assume right-to-work means no NLRA — concerted activity protection still applies
Don't apply Idaho's rule to WA or MT staff — they're different states for this purpose

name: NLRA § 7 — Protected Concerted Activity
holding: Employees — even in non-union workplaces — have the right to engage in 'concerted activities' for 'mutual aid or protection,' including discussing wages, working conditions, and complaints with coworkers. Employer retaliation is an unfair labor practice.
facts: Section 7 of the National Labor Relations Act has been federal law since 1935. The NLRB has consistently applied it to non-union workplaces.
reasoning: The Act protects collective activity — whether or not a union is involved. Two or more employees discussing terms of employment is concerted activity. One employee acting on behalf of others is also protected.
application: This is a major gotcha for handbook policies. CANNOT discipline an Ivy Medical employee for: discussing pay rates with coworkers (group chat, breakroom, social media); complaining about scheduling collectively; criticizing management in a group context. Common policy violations: 'employees may not discuss salary,' 'all complaints must go through HR only,' 'do not disparage the company on social media' (overly broad). Apply across all three states — Idaho/Montana/Washington — and to both union and non-union staff.
dos: Audit the employee handbook for overly broad confidentiality, social media, and 'professional conduct' clauses
Train managers that pay discussions among coworkers are PROTECTED
Treat group complaints about working conditions as protected activity until proven otherwise
donts: Don't prohibit discussion of wages or working conditions — that's an unfair labor practice
Don't discipline an employee for posting work complaints on social media without first checking NLRA protection
Don't include 'no disparagement' clauses that prevent factual criticism of working conditions

name: WA Bone Marrow/Organ Donation Leave (RCW 49.12.265)
holding: WA employers with 50+ employees must provide up to 5 days paid leave for bone marrow donation and up to 30 days paid leave for organ donation. Job protection during leave required.
facts: WA encourages organ donation through paid leave incentives. The federal tax code also provides a small credit to employers offering this leave.
reasoning: Organ donation requires significant recovery time. Without paid leave, donations decline. WA created the program to remove this barrier.
application: Rarely invoked but a real obligation if Ivy Medical hits 50 WA employees. Stack with FMLA and WA PFML for total job protection. Track separately from sick leave and vacation. Healthcare staff occasionally use this — both for personal donations and (rarely) family situations.
dos: Apply this rule if/when Ivy Medical hits 50 WA employees
Track organ donation leave separately from sick/vacation
Coordinate with FMLA and PFML for layered job protection
donts: Don't require employees to use sick or vacation time for organ donation leave (in lieu of this benefit)
Don't retaliate against employees who request this leave
Don't forget the rule exists — it's obscure and easy to overlook in a benefit audit

name: WA Local Minimum Wage Gotcha (Seattle/SeaTac/Tukwila)
holding: 2026 city minimum wages exceed the WA state floor of $17.13: Seattle $21.30, SeaTac $20.74 (hospitality/transp.), Tukwila $21.65, Renton $21.57, Burien up to $21.63, Bellingham $19.13, Everett $20.77. Local rate applies based on where work is performed.
facts: WA has the highest state minimum wage in the country at $17.13 (2026). Several cities have enacted local minimums above the state floor. Rates adjust annually.
reasoning: Cities with higher cost of living have used local authority to set higher minimums. Coverage thresholds vary (e.g., Seattle requires 2+ hours/2 weeks in city; SeaTac applies only to hospitality and transportation).
application: For Ivy Medical's $20/hr travel pay: UNSAFE inside Seattle, SeaTac, Tukwila, Burien, and Renton city limits. The rate must clear the city floor where the work is performed. Bellingham at $19.13 leaves only $0.87 cushion at $20 — fine but no headroom. Always verify the work-site city before setting a sub-$22 rate. Annual review needed because local rates adjust each January.
dos: Verify the work-site city BEFORE setting any travel or hourly rate for a WA assignment
Adjust the travel rate up where local minimums require
Re-audit travel rates every January when local rates adjust
Map out which Ivy Medical assignments touch the high-minimum-wage cities
donts: Don't apply $20/hr travel rate to work performed in Seattle, SeaTac, Tukwila, Burien, or Renton
Don't assume 'WA' means $17.13 — local rates vary widely
