Don't forget that even Bellingham is close to the wire ($19.13 vs $20)

name: WA Independent Contractor 'Six-Part Test'
holding: WA uses a strict six-part test for independent contractor status (different for workers' comp vs. unemployment). To qualify, the worker must be free from control, the work must be outside the employer's usual business, AND the worker must have an independently established business — among other factors. Failure on any element = employee.
facts: WA has multiple independent contractor tests applied by different agencies (L&I, ESD, DOR). Most are stricter than the federal IRS test.
reasoning: WA tightened contractor classification to combat misclassification — workers being denied workers' comp, unemployment, and wage protections.
application: Cannot classify a WA nurse, CNA, or tech as a 1099 contractor — clinical care is part of Ivy Medical's usual business and the employer controls the work. Misclassification triggers L&I assessments for unpaid premiums, back wages, penalties, and interest. Even short-term gig arrangements need W-2 unless the worker truly runs an independent practice. Federal IRS test is much looser; a worker can be a federal contractor but a WA employee.
dos: Default all WA clinical workers to W-2 status
Run the six-part test (with counsel) before classifying any WA worker as 1099
Treat per-diem and short-term WA staff as W-2 unless they pass every element
donts: Don't assume a worker who's 1099 for the IRS is automatically 1099 for WA
Don't classify a clinical worker as 1099 just because they prefer it — preference doesn't matter
Don't use 1099 status to dodge PFML, paid sick leave, or workers' comp obligations

name: WA Healthcare Workplace Improvements (Distinct from 2026 noncompete HB 1155)
holding: WA hospitals must implement and update workplace violence prevention plans, provide annual training, and report violence incidents. Healthcare workers have the right to refuse mandatory overtime (cross-references RCW 49.28).
facts: The 2019 HB 1155 (Healthcare Workplace Improvements Act) bundled multiple healthcare worker protections: mandatory OT ban expansion, uninterrupted break requirements, and workplace violence prevention. Note: this is a DIFFERENT HB 1155 from the 2026 noncompete ban — same bill number, totally different topic.
reasoning: Healthcare workplaces have unusually high rates of workplace violence. The legislature mandated documented prevention programs to address this.
application: If Ivy Medical operates hospital-based services in WA, must have a documented workplace violence prevention plan, annual employee training, and incident reporting. L&I enforces. Clinic and non-hospital operations have lighter requirements but should still maintain violence prevention basics.
dos: Maintain a written workplace violence prevention plan for WA hospital operations
Provide annual training to all hospital staff
Document violence incidents and the response
Be careful not to confuse this 2019 HB 1155 with the 2026 HB 1155 (noncompetes)
donts: Don't ignore the workplace violence component — it's actively enforced
Don't conflate this with the noncompete bill — same number, very different topic
Don't apply hospital-level requirements to non-hospital settings (different lighter standard applies)

name: Kasten v. Saint-Gobain Performance Plastics
holding: FLSA anti-retaliation protection covers ORAL complaints to the employer, not just written or formal complaints to an agency.
facts: Kasten verbally complained to his supervisors and HR about the placement of time clocks (which he argued caused unpaid 'donning and doffing' time). He was fired. The lower courts held that FLSA's anti-retaliation provision (Section 215(a)(3)) protected only 'filed' complaints — meaning written, formal complaints to government agencies.
reasoning: Justice Breyer, for a 6-2 majority, held that 'filed' should be read to include oral complaints to the employer, provided they are sufficiently clear and detailed to put the employer on notice that the employee is asserting FLSA rights. The Court reasoned that the FLSA's protective purpose would be undermined if only formal written complaints triggered protection.
application: Big practical impact for Ivy Medical. If an employee verbally complains to a supervisor about: not being paid for travel time, missed breaks, off-the-clock work, or other wage issues — they are protected from retaliation under federal law. Document any such complaint when it happens (date, employee, complaint, response). Document the non-retaliatory rationale for any subsequent discipline or termination. The complaint doesn't have to be formal or in writing to trigger protection.
dos: Train managers to recognize informal wage complaints as protected activity
Document every wage-related complaint (oral or written) with timestamp and substance
Maintain a clean, documented rationale for any adverse action following a wage complaint
Pause any disciplinary action that comes within weeks of a wage complaint for legal review
donts: Don't dismiss oral complaints as 'unofficial' — they trigger FLSA protection
Don't fire or discipline shortly after a wage complaint without strong, pre-existing documentation
Don't make sarcastic or retaliatory comments after a wage complaint — they become evidence

name: WA Pay Stub Requirements (RCW 49.46.070)
holding: WA pay stubs must show: hours worked, rate(s) of pay, gross wages, all deductions (itemized), pay period dates, employer name/address, and net pay. Two-rate jobs must show both rates and hours at each rate.
facts: WA's pay stub statute requires specific itemization. The two-rate disclosure requirement is critical for any employer using multi-rate pay structures.
reasoning: Pay stub transparency enables employees to verify correct payment and detect underpayment. Inadequate stubs make wage claims harder to bring and create their own independent violation.
application: Critical for Ivy Medical's two-rate travel pay: the WA pay stub MUST show clinical hours at the clinical rate AND travel hours at $20 separately. Confirm Paylocity is configured to display this. Inaccurate or incomplete pay stubs are themselves a wage violation. Pair with the weighted-average OT requirement — if a stub shows only the lower rate for OT, that's a red flag.
dos: Verify Paylocity pay stubs show both rates and hours at each rate for WA employees
Show OT premium calculation transparently when weighted-average method is used
Include all required elements: employer name, dates, hours, rates, deductions, net pay
Audit a sample of pay stubs quarterly to confirm format compliance
donts: Don't roll travel and clinical hours into a single line item — they must show separately
Don't omit any required element — even minor omissions create exposure
Don't rely on default Paylocity formats without verifying WA compliance

name: WA Wage Payment Act (RCW 49.48.010)
holding: WA employers must pay all wages owed upon termination (quit or fired) by the end of the pay period in which termination occurs. Unpaid wages willfully withheld trigger doubling under RCW 49.52.070.
facts: RCW 49.48.010 is the operative final-paycheck statute for WA. The 'end of the pay period' standard is more lenient than MT (immediate on involuntary termination) and ID (10 days max), but the doubling provision under the companion willful-withholding statute is harsh.
reasoning: The legislature gave employers reasonable processing time but coupled it with strong deterrence against using final pay as leverage. Coupled with RCW 49.52.070, willful withholding doubles the unpaid amount and adds attorney's fees.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA correctional medical staff: pay all wages owed by the end of the pay period when terminating. Include any accrued vacation if company policy provides payout (RCW 49.48.010(3) requires it if policy creates the right). Don't withhold over disputed equipment returns, training repayment, or anything else without specific written authorization. Pay the undisputed portion immediately if any portion is disputed.
dos: Pay all undisputed wages by end of pay period at termination
Include accrued vacation if policy provides payout
Get specific written authorization for any deduction
Document timing of termination and final pay for the audit trail
donts: Don't withhold final pay to enforce equipment return
Don't deduct training costs without prior signed authorization specific to that deduction
Don't withhold to force release signing — that's textbook willful withholding

name: WA Pay Frequency (RCW 49.46.020)
holding: WA employers must pay employees at regular intervals at least once per month on regular paydays designated in advance. The pay period must close no more than 10 days before payday. Wages must be paid on the established payday.
facts: WA's pay frequency framework sets minimum cadence and timing. Most employers run weekly, biweekly, or semi-monthly cycles.
reasoning: Regular, predictable pay cycles protect workers from cash-flow uncertainty and make wage claims easier to identify.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA staff: confirm Paylocity is set to a regular cycle (biweekly is standard) with paydays designated in advance. The pay period close-to-payday gap can't exceed 10 days. Communicate any payday changes in advance and in writing — sudden shifts trigger wage claims.
dos: Set paydays in advance and communicate them in writing (handbook + offer letter)
Keep the pay-period-close to payday gap at 10 days or less
Notify staff in advance of any payday changes
donts: Don't shift paydays without advance written notice
Don't run a pay period longer than a month
Don't bury the payday schedule in fine print — it should be obvious

name: WA No Tip Credit (RCW 49.46.020(3))
holding: WA does not allow tip credits. Tipped employees must receive the full state (or applicable local) minimum wage before tips. Tips are the employee's property and may not offset the wage floor.
facts: Unlike federal law and most states, WA prohibits using tips to satisfy minimum wage. Service charges have separate rules (RCW 49.46.160) — must be disclosed and distributed.
reasoning: WA chose universal minimum wage protection over the federal tip-credit model.
application: Probably not directly relevant to Ivy Medical's correctional medical staff (no tips in jail medicine). Worth knowing if Ivy ever expands into any tipped-staff context. The Service Charge Disclosure provision (RCW 49.46.160) has analogous logic if any service-charge fees ever come up.
dos: Pay full minimum wage to any tipped role regardless of tip volume
Treat tips as 100% employee property
donts: Don't apply federal tip-credit math to a WA tipped employee
Don't use service charges to satisfy minimum wage without proper RCW 49.46.160 disclosure

name: WA Garnishment Procedure (RCW 6.27)
holding: Garnishment of wages in WA follows Ch. 6.27 RCW. Employers must respond to writs within 20 days, withhold the proper amount, and may not retaliate against employees subject to garnishment. WA's minimum exemption is more generous than the federal CCPA floor (greater of 35x state minimum wage OR 80% of disposable earnings per week, depending on debt type).
facts: RCW 6.27 sets the procedure: writ served, employer answers, withholding begins, employer reports. Penalties for non-response or improper withholding can include judgment for the full debt against the employer.
reasoning: Garnishment is a powerful collection tool, but legislatures consistently set floor exemptions to ensure garnishment doesn't push workers below subsistence. WA's exemption is among the most generous in the country.
application: For Ivy Medical: every garnishment writ needs prompt processing through Paylocity. Confirm Paylocity calculates the WA exemption correctly — it's MORE generous than the federal floor, so a payroll system defaulting to FLSA/CCPA math will OVER-withhold. Garnishments for child support, taxes, and student loans have different rules. Never use garnishment status as a basis for any adverse action — that's an independent claim under RCW 6.27.170.
dos: Respond to writs within 20 days
Verify Paylocity applies WA's exemption (35x state min wage = ~$599.55/week in 2026), not federal floor
Process child support, tax levy, and creditor writs through their own rules
Document each writ separately for audit trail
donts: Don't retaliate against an employee subject to garnishment (independent claim)
Don't rely on Paylocity defaults without verifying WA exemption math
Don't ignore a writ — judgment can be entered against Ivy for the full debt

name: WA Willful Wage Withholding (RCW 49.52.050-070)
holding: Willful withholding of any wages owed is a misdemeanor and exposes the employer to double damages plus attorney's fees. 'Willful' means knowing and intentional — good-faith disputes don't trigger doubling, but sloppy recordkeeping can make a dispute look willful.
facts: RCW 49.52.050 makes willful underpayment a misdemeanor; RCW 49.52.070 creates the civil double-damages remedy. The pairing is unusually severe — most states have either criminal or doubling, not both.
reasoning: WA chose strong deterrence against intentional underpayment. The 'willful' threshold preserves a defense for honest disputes.
application: For Ivy Medical: this is the doubling provision that gives WA wage claims their teeth (cf. Idaho's trebling for willful, Montana's 110% penalty). Pair with the 3-year statute of limitations and Anderson v. Mt. Clemens (poor records = employee estimates win) and you have a structure where a $5K dispute can become a $30K judgment. Document every pay decision, respond promptly to inquiries, and pay the undisputed portion of any dispute immediately.
dos: Document the basis for every pay decision in writing
Respond to wage inquiries in writing with specific rationale
Pay undisputed portions immediately if any portion is disputed
Train payroll staff to flag potential wage disputes for escalation
donts: Don't ignore wage complaints — silence reads as willful
Don't rely on undocumented oral explanations for unusual pay decisions
Don't withhold pay to leverage anything (release, equipment, agreement)

name: WA Personnel Records Access (RCW 49.12.240) — Amended HB 1308 (2025)
holding: Effective July 27, 2025, WA employers must produce a copy of an employee's personnel file within 21 calendar days of a written request from a current or former employee (within 3 years of separation). HB 1308 added a statutory definition of 'personnel file' covering: job application; performance evaluations; non-active disciplinary records; medical records; payroll records; and all employment agreements.
facts: Prior law required production in a 'reasonable time' with no defined scope. HB 1308 closed both ambiguities — fixed 21-day deadline and statutory definition. Penalties for non-compliance include statutory damages plus attorney's fees.
reasoning: Personnel-file access is increasingly important to employees defending wrongful-discharge, discrimination, and wage claims. The legislature standardized the response framework to reduce litigation over the procedural fight itself.
application: For Ivy Medical: any current or former WA employee (within 3 years of separation) who submits a written request gets their file within 21 days. Build a checklist for what 'personnel file' includes per the statute. Note: ADA requires medical records be in a SEPARATE confidential file — don't co-mingle with the personnel file even though both must be produced. Train HR to log incoming requests and start the clock. Failure to respond is its own claim.
dos: Log every personnel-file request with date received and 21-day deadline
Maintain medical/ADA records in a separate confidential file (still producible on request)
Include all 6 statutory categories: application, evaluations, non-active discipline, medical, payroll, agreements
Train HR to recognize and route requests immediately
donts: Don't slow-walk requests beyond 21 days — statutory damages accrue
Don't redact based on company preference — only legally required redactions
Don't lose track of former employees within 3 years of separation
Don't mix ADA medical records into the general personnel file

name: WA Written Discharge Statement (RCW 49.12.241) — HB 1308 (2025)
holding: Effective July 27, 2025, WA employers must provide a discharged employee, upon written request, a written statement of the reasons for discharge and the effective date. Must be provided within a reasonable time after the request.
facts: HB 1308 added this requirement alongside the personnel-records changes. Prior law had no analogous statement requirement.
reasoning: Forcing employers to commit a discharge rationale to writing reduces post-hoc justification and creates a discrete record for wrongful-discharge analysis.
application: Major operational change for Ivy Medical's WA terminations. Every fired WA employee can demand a written discharge statement. PRACTICAL IMPACT: the statement locks Ivy into the reason given. If Ivy says 'attendance' and then a discrimination complaint surfaces, the company can't pivot to 'performance' without looking pretextual. Implication: do the discharge analysis BEFORE the termination, document the actual reason precisely, and don't issue the discharge statement until that analysis is solid. Coordinate with legal review on borderline terminations.
dos: Build a discharge documentation template that includes a draft written discharge statement
Do the discharge analysis BEFORE termination, not after
Have legal review borderline terminations before the discharge statement issues
Keep the statement narrow, factual, and specific
donts: Don't issue a generic 'no longer a good fit' statement — it's evidence of pretext
Don't refuse to provide the statement on request — that's its own violation
Don't issue a discharge statement that contradicts contemporaneous documentation
Don't pivot rationales mid-litigation after the statement is on record

name: WA Mini-WARN Act (Mass Layoff Notice, 2025)
holding: Effective July 27, 2025, WA employers with 50 or more employees must provide 60 days' advance written notice of mass layoffs or business closures to: (1) the WA Employment Security Department (ESD), and (2) affected employees or their bargaining representatives. ESD notice must include names and addresses of affected employees.
facts: Federal WARN Act applies at 100+ employees. WA's mini-WARN lowers the threshold to 50 and triggers on smaller events.
reasoning: Federal WARN missed many WA layoffs because of the 100-employee threshold and its 50-employee/single-site rules. The state version captures more events.
application: For Ivy Medical: if total WA headcount hits 50+, this applies. A 'mass layoff' generally means 33% of the workforce OR 50+ employees within a 30-day window. Losing a major jail contract that triggers staff RIFs would likely qualify. Build a layoff playbook now: 60-day notice, ESD filing template with employee data, affected-employee notice template. Penalties include back pay and benefits for the notice period to each affected worker. Federal WARN may also apply if at 100+.
dos: Track WA headcount — at 50+ this applies
Build the ESD notice template before it's needed
Coordinate with federal WARN at 100+ employees
Time layoff events to allow the 60-day notice
donts: Don't skip notice on contract-loss layoffs — those count
Don't issue partial notice — ESD AND employees both need it
Don't assume bargaining representative notice substitutes for employee notice
Don't underestimate the back-pay exposure for a notice-violation finding

name: WA Driver's License Hiring Restriction (SB 5501, 2025)
holding: Effective July 27, 2025, WA employers may NOT require a valid driver's license as a condition of employment, nor state that one is required in a job posting, UNLESS driving is an essential function of the position OR is tied to a legitimate business purpose. Statutory damages of $5,000 or actual damages, plus interest and attorney's fees.
facts: Modeled on similar laws in other states aimed at reducing barriers for applicants without licenses (including immigrants, low-income workers, and people with revoked licenses).
reasoning: Driver's license requirements frequently screen out qualified candidates for jobs that don't actually require driving. The 'essential function' standard mirrors ADA analysis.
application: For Ivy Medical: nursing roles in a jail don't require driving as an essential function. Strip 'valid driver's license' from job postings for clinical roles unless the position involves driving (e.g., facility-to-facility travel as a regular duty). Update job descriptions, postings, and applications. The 'legitimate business purpose' carve-out is narrow — convenience is not a business purpose. For a clinical position with occasional travel to another site, document specifically why driving is essential or pivot to 'reliable transportation.'
dos: Audit all WA clinical job postings for driver's license language
Document essential-function analysis for any position retaining the requirement
Use 'reliable transportation' language instead when driving isn't central
Update applications to remove blanket license questions
donts: Don't post 'valid driver's license required' for a clinical role without analysis
Don't use 'company policy' or 'standard requirement' as the business purpose
Don't ignore the actual damages exposure — back pay for screened-out applicants is meaningful

name: WA EPOA Cure Period Amendments (2025)
holding: Effective July 27, 2025, WA employers get a 5-day window after notification to correct a noncompliant job posting before penalties accrue (sunset July 27, 2027). Statutory damages now DISCRETIONARY at $100-$5,000 (was a flat $5,000). Fixed wage amount allowed in lieu of range when the job offers a fixed amount. Employers not liable for unauthorized third-party postings where they demanded correction or removal.
facts: WA's 2023 EPOA pay-transparency law generated a wave of class-action settlements (no cure period meant any noncompliant posting was strict liability). The 2025 amendments soften enforcement while keeping the substantive requirements.
reasoning: Strict liability without a cure window produced excessive litigation over technical violations. The 5-day window and discretionary damages range bring proportionality.
application: For Ivy Medical: still must include a good-faith pay range (or fixed amount) plus benefits description in every WA job posting. New protection: if a noncompliant posting is identified, Ivy has 5 days to fix it before liability attaches. Build a process for monitoring postings on all platforms (Indeed, LinkedIn, Idealist, jail/sheriff career sites). The third-party safe harbor requires demonstrated demand to correct — keep records.
dos: Maintain a posting monitoring process across all platforms
Issue takedown/correction demands to third parties in writing (preserves the safe harbor)
Use the 5-day cure window before July 27, 2027 — it sunsets
Use 'fixed amount' for fixed-pay roles instead of synthetic ranges
donts: Don't post unauthorized third-party listings without record of demand to correct
Don't rely on the cure window after July 27, 2027
Don't post fake ranges — good-faith is still required (e.g., $50K-$200K is not good faith)

name: WA EPOA Expanded Protected Classes (HB 1905, 2025)
holding: HB 1905 expands EPOA's pay equity protections to a broader set of protected classes beyond sex — now covers race, color, religion, national origin, citizenship, sexual orientation, gender identity, age, disability, marital status, honorably discharged veteran status, and members of any other protected class under WLAD.
facts: Original EPOA (2018) was sex-focused. HB 1905 broadens the comparable-work and pay-equity provisions to mirror WLAD's full protected-class list.
reasoning: Pay equity analysis applies the same logic across protected classes — disparate pay for substantially similar work without legitimate business justification is unlawful regardless of which protected characteristic correlates with the lower pay.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA clinical staff: pay equity analysis can't be limited to gender. If an RN of color is paid less than a white RN doing substantially similar work, the same equal-pay framework applies. Audit pay structures across all protected classes, document any pay differential rationales (experience, certification, location, market rate), and ensure rationales are applied consistently.
dos: Audit pay across all WLAD protected classes, not just gender
Document factor-based rationales for pay differentials (experience, certs, market, location)
Apply rationales consistently — if 'experience' justifies one person's higher pay, it must work the same way for others
Use written compensation guidelines to standardize the analysis
donts: Don't limit pay equity analysis to male-female comparisons
Don't justify differentials with 'salary history' — that's prohibited
Don't use 'budget' as a rationale for paying members of protected classes less

name: WA Paid Sick Leave — Immigration Proceedings Expansion (HB 1875, 2025)
holding: Effective July 27, 2025, WA employees may use accrued paid sick leave to prepare for or participate in any judicial or administrative immigration proceeding involving the employee or a designated family member. Documentation requirements are flexible — employer must accept employee's written statement as verification.
facts: HB 1875 expanded the permissible uses of paid sick leave under RCW 49.46.210. Pre-amendment, sick leave was for medical/health uses only.
reasoning: The legislature recognized that immigration proceedings often happen on short notice, are difficult to reschedule, and create the same employment risk as health emergencies. The flexible documentation provision protects employees from having to disclose immigration status details to employers.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA staff: cannot deny sick leave for immigration proceedings, cannot demand proof of the proceeding's nature, and must accept the employee's written statement. Train managers — the natural reaction ('what kind of appointment?') becomes legally risky. Treat the request the same as any sick-leave request: approve, track usage, don't probe.
dos: Accept employee written statements as verification — don't demand documentation
Train managers not to probe the nature of the proceeding
Apply uniform sick-leave approval process regardless of cited reason
Update sick leave policy language in handbook to reflect this expansion
donts: Don't ask 'what kind of immigration appointment' — that's its own claim
Don't deny based on documentation insufficiency — written statement suffices
Don't disclose the reason to coworkers or supervisors who don't need to know

name: WA Workers' Compensation (Title 51 RCW)
holding: WA is a monopolistic workers' comp state — coverage is provided through L&I's State Fund (not private insurance), unless the employer self-insures. Coverage is mandatory and exclusive: workers give up the right to sue the employer for workplace injuries in exchange for no-fault benefits. Employee premium contribution is unusual — WA is one of few states where workers pay a portion through payroll deduction.
facts: Title 51 RCW is comprehensive — Ch. 51.04 to 51.98 cover coverage, benefits, premium calculation, claims procedure, retrospective rating, self-insurance, and appeals. L&I administers through the State Fund. Premium rates vary by classification code (risk class) and the employer's experience factor.
reasoning: WA's monopolistic system was created in 1911 to address industrial accidents. Private insurance is not allowed for the basic coverage. Self-insurance is available to qualified large employers.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA staff: coverage runs through L&I State Fund. Premium rates depend on classification — corrections healthcare is typically a higher-risk classification than office work due to inmate assault exposure. Confirm Paylocity withholds the employee share correctly and remits Ivy's share quarterly. Document EVERY workplace injury including minor assaults — under-reporting drives experience-rated premium increases later and exposes Ivy to claim-suppression accusations. WORKER ASSAULT IS THE KEY EXPOSURE for correctional medical staff — see RCW 51.32.020 on intentional employer acts (rare carve-out from exclusive remedy).
dos: Use the correct risk classification code for corrections healthcare
Document every workplace injury, however minor — under-reporting is its own exposure
Train staff on injury reporting and the 24-hour expectation
Coordinate with the facility on inmate-on-staff assault response and documentation
donts: Don't try to handle a claim outside L&I — exclusive remedy means you can't 'settle' a workplace injury privately
Don't discourage claims — claim suppression is a separate violation under RCW 51.48.025
Don't ignore the employee-share withholding — it's part of the premium structure
Don't classify corrections work as 'office work' for premium savings — misclassification is fraud

name: WA Assault on Healthcare Worker — WC Considerations (RCW 51.32.220)
holding: WA L&I provides additional benefits and presumptions for healthcare workers assaulted on the job. Time-loss benefits apply during recovery; PTSD claims from workplace violence have established a framework. The 'intentional act' exception to exclusive remedy (RCW 51.24.020) is narrow but exists for cases where the employer deliberately created the exposure.
facts: Healthcare workers experience workplace violence at far higher rates than other industries. WA's WC framework recognizes this with specific provisions for injury, time-loss, and mental-health claims.
reasoning: The legislature recognized that assault is a foreseeable occupational hazard for healthcare workers, especially in psychiatric and corrections settings.
application: Major exposure area for Ivy Medical's correctional medical staff. Inmate-on-staff assault is statistically certain over a long enough horizon. Build the response protocol now: incident report, medical evaluation, L&I claim filing within 1 year (best practice within 24 hours), PTSD screening for serious incidents, return-to-work coordination. The 'intentional act' exception is rare but lives — sending a staff member into a known-dangerous situation without proper backup could trigger it. Maintain documented safety protocols and follow them.
dos: Have an assault response protocol in writing, with steps and timing
File L&I claims promptly for every assault incident
Screen for PTSD on serious assaults and provide referrals
Coordinate with the facility on dangerous-inmate flags and notification protocols
Document staffing levels for any assault that occurred during a coverage gap
donts: Don't discourage assault reporting — every incident gets documented
Don't send staff into known-dangerous situations without proper backup (intentional-act exposure)
Don't treat PTSD claims as illegitimate — they're well-established
Don't try to handle severe injuries outside L&I

name: WA Law Against Discrimination — WLAD (RCW 49.60)
holding: WLAD is among the broadest state anti-discrimination statutes. Protected classes include race, creed, color, national origin, citizenship/immigration status, sex, marital status, sexual orientation, gender identity, age (40+), honorably discharged veteran status, disability (broader than ADA), pregnancy, and use of a trained service animal. Coverage threshold is 8+ employees (lower than Title VII's 15). Damages are uncapped — punitive damages allowed.
facts: Ch. 49.60 RCW is the consolidated state anti-discrimination law, enforced by WA Human Rights Commission. WLAD is broader than Title VII in coverage, protected classes, and remedies.
reasoning: WA chose maximum employee protection — lower coverage threshold, more protected classes, broader disability definition, no damages cap.
application: For Ivy Medical: WLAD applies if Ivy has 8+ WA employees (essentially always). The lower threshold matters — a small WA contract still triggers WLAD. Uncapped damages and punitive availability make WLAD claims more dangerous than federal claims. The disability definition is broader than ADA — even temporary impairments may qualify. Train managers on WLAD's full protected-class list; the citizenship/immigration-status protection in particular is unusual and easy to violate accidentally (e.g., 'do you have a green card?' in an interview).
dos: Apply WLAD coverage from 8 WA employees, not 15
Use WLAD's protected-class list (longer than Title VII)
Treat citizenship/immigration status as protected — no 'where are you from' questions
Document interactive process for disability accommodations with the broader WLAD definition in mind
donts: Don't assume Title VII compliance equals WLAD compliance — WLAD is broader
Don't ask citizenship-status questions outside the legally required I-9 process
Don't underestimate WLAD damages — uncapped + punitive available

name: WA WLAD Anti-Retaliation (RCW 49.60.210)
holding: WLAD prohibits retaliation against any person for opposing a practice forbidden by WLAD, filing a charge or complaint, or testifying in a WLAD proceeding. 'Opposition' is broadly construed — includes informal complaints to supervisors. Damages are the same as substantive WLAD claims (uncapped, punitive available).
facts: RCW 49.60.210 is the WLAD anti-retaliation companion to the substantive discrimination provisions. WA Supreme Court has interpreted 'opposition' broadly.
reasoning: Without strong anti-retaliation protection, the substantive rights are unenforceable.
application: For Ivy Medical: a WA employee who informally complains to a supervisor about discrimination, harassment, or accommodation issues is protected from retaliation under WLAD — even if the complaint is wrong on the merits. This is the WA-state parallel to Kasten v. Saint-Gobain (FLSA oral complaints). Document any post-complaint adverse action with strong, pre-complaint documentation. The 'temporal proximity' of an adverse action to a complaint is the most common retaliation indicator — pause discipline within 30-60 days of a complaint for legal review.
dos: Train managers to recognize informal complaints as protected activity
Pause adverse actions within 30-60 days of a complaint for legal review
Document the pre-existing non-retaliatory rationale for any adverse action
Apply discipline standards consistently — disparate treatment compounds retaliation claims
donts: Don't say or write anything 'punishing' the complaint, even sarcastically — becomes evidence
Don't reassign or schedule-change a complainant without strong documented rationale
Don't underestimate temporal proximity — fired 2 weeks after complaining is a hard case to win

name: WA Healthcare Whistleblower Protection (RCW 43.70.075)
holding: Healthcare workers in WA are specifically protected from retaliation for reporting unsafe patient care, quality issues, or violations of healthcare laws. Applies to retaliation by any healthcare facility or employer. Remedies include reinstatement, back pay, and damages.
facts: WA created industry-specific healthcare whistleblower protection separate from general anti-retaliation. The provision recognizes that healthcare workers face unique pressure to suppress safety reports.
reasoning: Patient safety depends on willingness to report unsafe conditions. Without protection, reporting drops.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA correctional medical staff: a clinician who reports unsafe medical conditions in the jail (inadequate equipment, denied access to inmates needing care, falsified records) is protected. This is especially important in the correctional context because Ivy's clinicians often see violations of their professional obligations driven by jail policy. Document every concern raised through formal channels. Never retaliate against a clinician who reports up the chain — Ivy's contract may give the jail leverage to demand changes, but the clinician is statutorily protected. Coordinate response with legal counsel.
dos: Maintain a clear, documented incident-report channel for clinical safety concerns
Investigate every report through a documented process
Coordinate with legal counsel on any post-report adverse action toward the reporter
Train clinical staff that they have statutory protection for good-faith reports
donts: Don't retaliate against a clinician who reports unsafe conditions to the jail or to outside authorities
Don't use 'team fit' or 'attitude' as cover for adverse action following a safety report
Don't pressure clinicians to keep concerns 'in-house' — they have statutory rights to report

name: WA Industrial Safety & Health Act — WISHA (RCW 49.17)
holding: WISHA is WA's state OSHA equivalent — WA operates an OSHA-approved state plan. Standards in Ch. 296 WAC parallel federal OSHA but with several WA-specific additions (workplace violence prevention in healthcare, outdoor heat exposure, ergonomics in healthcare). Enforced by L&I DOSH.
facts: WA is one of 22 OSHA state-plan states. WISHA applies WA's OSHA-approved standards, generally stricter than or equal to federal OSHA.
reasoning: State-plan approval allows WA to set higher safety standards while maintaining federal pre-emption protection.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA operations: WISHA applies, not federal OSHA. DOSH conducts inspections. Specific standards relevant to correctional medical work: WAC 296-800 (general industry — bloodborne pathogens, PPE, hazard communication), workplace violence prevention requirements for healthcare settings, ergonomics for clinical work. Maintain a written safety program, conduct required training, document inspections. WISHA penalties parallel federal OSHA but state inspectors are sometimes more accessible than federal.
dos: Maintain a written safety program complying with applicable WAC 296 standards
Conduct required training (bloodborne pathogens, PPE, workplace violence) and document
Coordinate with the jail on facility-specific hazards (inmate handling, sharps in cells, contraband)
Respond promptly to DOSH inspections — non-response amplifies penalties
donts: Don't assume federal OSHA standards are sufficient — WISHA can be stricter
Don't ignore the bloodborne pathogen standard — corrections healthcare exposure is constant
Don't skip training documentation — it's the first thing DOSH asks for

name: WA Lactation Accommodation (RCW 43.10.005 / federal PUMP Act overlay)
holding: WA requires reasonable break time and a private, non-bathroom location for lactating employees to express milk for up to 2 years after child's birth. Federal PUMP Act (2022) provides similar federal protection. Both require accommodation that doesn't impose 'undue hardship.'
facts: Federal PUMP Act amended FLSA to extend lactation protection to exempt and non-exempt workers. WA's state law applies in parallel.
reasoning: Workplace lactation accommodation is necessary for postpartum employees to maintain breastfeeding while working. The 2-year window covers the WHO/AAP-recommended breastfeeding duration.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA staff: lactating clinical staff must have reasonable break time and a private space (not a bathroom) for pumping. In a jail environment, finding a compliant space can be tricky — coordinate with the facility on which non-public areas are usable. Build the accommodation into the schedule rather than treating it as an interruption. Discrimination against lactating employees is also a WLAD violation (sex/pregnancy).
dos: Identify a private, non-bathroom space at each facility before it's needed
Build pumping breaks into the schedule rather than reacting to interruptions
Coordinate with the jail on access to a compliant space
Treat lactation accommodation requests with the same care as any other accommodation
donts: Don't direct an employee to use a bathroom — explicit violation
Don't deny lactation accommodation on 'clinical operations' grounds without documented hardship analysis
Don't subject a lactating employee to subtle pressure (workload, scheduling jabs) — that's retaliation

name: WA Sexual Harassment Policies & Training (RCW 49.60.515)
holding: WA isolating/hospitality industries are required to provide annual sexual harassment prevention training and panic-button equipment under RCW 49.60.515. The broader WLAD harassment framework applies to all WA employers — periodic training, written policy, accessible complaint mechanism, and documented investigation procedures are best-practice but not statutorily mandated outside isolated industries.
facts: WA's specific training mandate targets isolating industries (hotel housekeeping, security guards, in-home caregivers) but the WLAD framework presumes all employers will have policies and training.
reasoning: WA recognized industries where workers are isolated face higher harassment risk and codified specific protections. The general WLAD framework allows the courts and HRC to assess training adequacy as part of liability analysis.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA staff: while the specific training mandate doesn't directly apply, an effective sexual harassment prevention program is a key defense to WLAD claims. Maintain written policy, annual training, accessible complaint channels, and documented investigation procedures. In a correctional setting, harassment may come from coworkers, supervisors, OR INMATES — Ivy's policy needs to address all three. Inmate harassment claims raise unique exposure under PREA and Title VII.
dos: Maintain written sexual harassment policy in employee handbook
Conduct annual training and document attendance
Provide multiple complaint channels including outside the immediate supervisor
Address inmate-on-staff harassment in policy (PREA + Title VII)
donts: Don't rely on policy alone without training
Don't make the complaint chain dependent on the alleged harasser's supervision
Don't dismiss inmate harassment as 'part of the job' — it's still a Title VII issue

name: WA Drug Testing Restrictions (RCW 49.44.240 / Off-duty Cannabis SB 5123)
holding: Effective Jan 1, 2024, WA SB 5123 prohibits pre-employment drug testing for off-duty cannabis use (with limited safety-sensitive exceptions). Post-hire impairment testing is allowed when based on reasonable suspicion. Federally-regulated positions (DOT, security clearance) and 'safety-sensitive' positions are carved out.
facts: WA followed several other legal-cannabis states in protecting off-duty cannabis use from pre-employment drug screening. Implementation has been turbulent — many employers initially over-applied the safety-sensitive carve-out.
reasoning: Cannabis is legal in WA, and pre-employment THC tests detect use weeks after impairment ends. The legislature limited employer use of such testing for non-safety-sensitive positions while preserving employer rights for genuine safety roles.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA hiring: cannot screen out applicants for off-duty cannabis use UNLESS the position qualifies as 'safety-sensitive' or is federally regulated. Correctional medical staff: STRONG case for safety-sensitive (patient safety, controlled-substance handling, working around vulnerable population). Document the safety-sensitive analysis for each role before testing. The carve-out is narrower than employers often assume — get attorney input on borderline classifications. Post-hire reasonable-suspicion testing remains allowed regardless.
dos: Document the safety-sensitive analysis for any role subject to pre-employment cannabis testing
Continue reasonable-suspicion impairment testing for current employees
Update job postings to clarify drug testing only where lawful
Coordinate with legal counsel on borderline safety-sensitive classifications
donts: Don't apply blanket pre-employment cannabis testing to all positions
Don't use the safety-sensitive label loosely — courts scrutinize
Don't confuse pre-employment screening (restricted) with post-hire reasonable suspicion (allowed)

name: WA Fair Chance Act (RCW 49.94)
holding: WA Fair Chance Act prohibits employers from inquiring into criminal history on applications or in initial application processing. Employers may inquire AFTER an initial determination of qualification. Carve-outs for positions involving direct contact with vulnerable populations, financial trust, and other narrow categories. Seattle ordinance is stricter and applies inside city limits.
facts: RCW 49.94 codifies WA's ban-the-box framework. Seattle's earlier ordinance and the Spokane ordinance overlay with additional requirements within city limits.
reasoning: Background-check screening early in the hiring process disproportionately excludes minority applicants. Delaying the inquiry until after a tentative qualification determination preserves the employer's ultimate decision while reducing pretextual screening.
application: For Ivy Medical: clinical staff in correctional medicine likely fall under the 'direct contact with vulnerable populations' carve-out, which permits earlier inquiry. BUT — the analysis is specific. Even for carved-out positions, EEOC's Arrest & Conviction Guidance still requires individualized assessment of the criminal record against the position's actual requirements. Don't auto-disqualify based on any conviction. Document the relationship between the conviction and the position. Coordinate with the facility's background-check requirements (which are typically stricter and govern access to the jail).
dos: Remove criminal-history questions from initial applications even when the carve-out applies (cleaner)
Conduct individualized assessment for any disqualifying conviction
Document the position-specific rationale for any conviction-based denial
Coordinate with the jail's background check requirements (separate from Ivy's hiring framework)
donts: Don't apply blanket no-conviction policies — EEOC guidance prohibits
Don't extend inquiries beyond the position's actual security/care requirements
Don't confuse Ivy's hiring decision with the jail's facility-access decision — they're separate

name: Idaho Minimum Wage (I.C. § 44-1502)
holding: Idaho's minimum wage tracks the federal FLSA minimum wage. Currently $7.25/hour (unchanged since 2009). Tipped minimum cash wage $3.35 with tip credit up to $3.90. Youth training wage $4.25 for under-20 employees in first 90 days. Local jurisdictions prohibited from setting higher minimums (I.C. § 44-1502(4)).
facts: Idaho is among ~20 states still at the $7.25 federal floor. The statute's auto-tracking provision means any federal increase would automatically apply in Idaho. Multiple legislative efforts to raise the state floor have failed in recent sessions (HB 485 in 2025 died in committee).
reasoning: Idaho's policy preference is market-set wages. The pre-emption of local minimum-wage ordinances (added in 2015) closed the door on Boise or Coeur d'Alene setting higher local floors.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho clinical staff: minimum wage is $7.25. RNs and providers always exceed this; CNAs and support staff are well above it; the floor is rarely the binding constraint. The pre-emption matters: a Boise jail's local-government employer might pay higher, but Ivy as a private contractor isn't subject to a higher local wage. Idaho's federal-tracking means an FLSA increase would automatically apply — monitor federal minimum wage proposals.
dos: Use $7.25 as the Idaho floor
Monitor federal FLSA changes — they auto-apply
Document tip credit usage carefully if applicable (probably not in clinical roles)
donts: Don't apply WA or MT minimum-wage thinking to Idaho
Don't expect higher local minimum wages — pre-empted
Don't pay under $7.25 even for short-term or per-diem staff

name: Idaho Wage Payment & Frequency (I.C. § 45-606 to § 45-617)
holding: Idaho employers must pay wages at least monthly on regular paydays designated in advance (I.C. § 45-608). Final pay due earlier of next payday or 10 working days from separation; written 48-hour request accelerates to 48 hours (I.C. § 45-606). Wages must be paid in lawful money or by check/direct deposit. Civil penalty up to $500 per pay period for repeated late payment.
facts: Idaho's wage payment framework is contained in Title 45 Chapter 6. Notification of wage rate at hire is required (I.C. § 45-617). Deductions limited to those legally required or expressly authorized in writing.
reasoning: Idaho codified standard wage-payment protections with deliberately employer-friendly timing. The 10-working-day window is more lenient than WA's end-of-pay-period or MT's immediate-on-firing.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho clinical staff: final paychecks due within 10 working days OR by next payday, whichever is earlier. Written 48-hour requests must be honored — train Paylocity / payroll on this. Wage rate notice at hire (I.C. § 45-617) means new-hire paperwork must include hourly rate, pay frequency, paydays, and any agreed differentials (call pay, weekend differential, etc.). The Idaho Wage Claim Act (separate entry) imposes trebling for willful violations, so document everything.
dos: Provide wage rate notice at hire including all differentials and pay frequency
Pay final wages within 10 working days or by next payday, whichever is earlier
Honor 48-hour acceleration requests when made in writing
Get written authorization for any non-statutory deduction
donts: Don't deduct without specific written authorization for that deduction
Don't push final pay beyond 10 working days hoping to negotiate
Don't pay in cash without proper documentation (creates Anderson-style issues)

name: Idaho Wage Deductions (I.C. § 45-609)
holding: Idaho employers may deduct from wages only: (1) deductions required by law (taxes, garnishments); (2) deductions authorized in writing by the employee for the employee's benefit (benefits premiums, retirement); (3) deductions reimbursing the employer for cash advances. Other deductions require specific written authorization.
facts: I.C. § 45-609 sets the closed list of permissible deductions. Like most states, Idaho allows broad authorized-in-writing deductions but requires specificity.
reasoning: Standard wage protection framework — employees should not have wages reduced without consent or legal authority.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho clinical staff: training repayment, uniform charges, equipment damages, and similar deductions need specific signed written authorization SPECIFIC TO THAT DEDUCTION. A general 'I authorize all lawful deductions' clause in an offer letter is usually insufficient. Build a deduction authorization template with line-by-line consent for benefits, retirement, voluntary garnishments, etc.
dos: Get specific signed authorization for each non-statutory deduction
Build a clear deduction authorization template
Audit Paylocity deduction setup to ensure each has corresponding authorization
Document the basis for any deduction in writing
donts: Don't rely on a general authorization clause for specific deductions
Don't deduct for damages or losses without explicit per-deduction authorization (often unenforceable anyway)
Don't withhold final pay over disputed deductions — pay the undisputed amount immediately

name: Idaho Human Rights Act (I.C. § 67-5901 to § 67-5912)
holding: Idaho Human Rights Act prohibits employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, national origin, age (40+), or disability. Coverage threshold is 5+ employees. Notably does NOT include sexual orientation, gender identity, or pregnancy as separate protected classes under state law — though federal Title VII (via Bostock) covers SO/GI nationwide and PWFA covers pregnancy.
facts: I.C. § 67-5901 et seq. is enforced by the Idaho Human Rights Commission. Idaho is one of about 20 states that has not added SO/GI to state-law protected classes. The 5-employee threshold is lower than Title VII's 15.
reasoning: Idaho's anti-discrimination framework largely tracks the federal Civil Rights Act but has not been updated to match WLAD-level breadth.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho clinical staff: IHRA applies at 5+ employees. Title VII (15+) and ADA (15+) overlay. KEY GAP: Idaho state law doesn't independently protect SO/GI — Title VII does (post-Bostock), so the protection exists in practice, but enforcement runs through EEOC rather than Idaho HRC for those claims. Pregnancy protection runs through federal PWFA and PDA. Treat all federal protected classes as applicable regardless of state coverage. The lower 5-employee threshold matters — even small Idaho operations are covered.
dos: Apply IHRA at 5+ employees, Title VII at 15+
Treat SO/GI as protected under Title VII (Bostock) even though IHRA is silent
Apply PWFA for pregnancy regardless of IHRA scope
Use Title VII's broader framework operationally — covers all federal protected classes
donts: Don't read IHRA's silence on SO/GI as permission to discriminate — Title VII applies
Don't assume Idaho's smaller protected-class list means lower exposure — federal law fills gaps
Don't classify Idaho operations as 'no discrimination law applies' under 15 employees — IHRA hits at 5

name: Idaho Workers' Compensation (I.C. Title 72)
holding: Idaho workers' compensation is mandatory for all employers with 1+ employees (limited exceptions). Coverage through private insurance OR state self-insurance OR Idaho State Insurance Fund (a state-created competitive insurer, not monopolistic). Exclusive remedy doctrine applies. Administered by Idaho Industrial Commission.
facts: Idaho's workers' comp is a hybrid system — unlike WA's monopolistic L&I, Idaho permits private insurance through admitted carriers OR coverage through the State Insurance Fund. Max TTD benefit for 2026 is $1,021.50/week. Workers' comp rates dropped 2.5% effective Jan 1, 2026.
reasoning: Standard exclusive-remedy framework. Idaho's competitive insurance market provides more pricing flexibility than monopolistic states.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho clinical staff: get workers' comp coverage in place before hire #1 — Idaho is mandatory at 1+ employees. Most private carriers can write it; the State Insurance Fund is the backstop. Correctional medical workers' comp classification (NCCI codes) is typically elevated due to assault exposure. Document every assault incident — premium savings from suppression isn't worth the IIC fraud exposure. Workers' comp claims go through the Idaho Industrial Commission, not the courts.
dos: Maintain workers' comp coverage from first hire
Use the correct NCCI classification for correctional medicine
Document every assault or injury incident promptly
Coordinate IIC reporting with claim filing
donts: Don't operate without coverage even briefly — Idaho is strict
Don't suppress claims to manage premium — IIC fraud penalties bite
Don't try to litigate workplace injuries outside IIC — exclusive remedy applies

name: Idaho Employment Security Law (I.C. § 72-1366) — HB 54 (2025) Amendments
holding: Idaho Employment Security Law governs unemployment insurance. UI taxable wage base for 2026 is $58,300; base employer tax rate is 0.729%. HB 54 (2025) revised definitions of 'able to work' to better protect claimants with disabilities, clarified rules around labor market withdrawal due to injury, and sunset certain provisions.
facts: Title 72 Chapter 13 is the UI framework, administered by Idaho Department of Labor. HB 54 was Idaho's main 2025 employment-related statutory change.
reasoning: Standard UI structure with periodic legislative refinements. HB 54 addressed disparate treatment concerns for claimants with disabilities.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho operations: standard UI premium remittance required (confirm Paylocity is set up correctly). Separation reporting matters — UI determinations turn on how Ivy characterizes the separation (voluntary quit, discharge for misconduct, layoff, etc.). Idaho UI hearings are surprisingly often won by employers when documentation is solid. After HB 54: if a separated employee has a disability and the separation involves injury or labor market withdrawal, the eligibility analysis is more claimant-favorable than before.
dos: Verify Paylocity is set to the 2026 UI taxable wage base ($58,300)
Maintain separation documentation for UI hearing readiness
Train HR on the separation-type classifications and their UI implications
Track HB 54 implementation for claimants with disabilities
donts: Don't characterize layoffs as 'misconduct' to fight UI — that's its own claim
Don't ignore UI hearings — they affect Ivy's experience rating
Don't fail to respond to UI determinations within deadlines

name: Idaho Drug Testing (I.C. § 44-1701 to § 44-1707)
holding: Idaho's voluntary drug testing law provides a 'safe harbor' — employers that follow the statutory procedure for drug testing get specific protections (5% workers' comp premium discount, immunity from defamation claims for accurate reporting). Procedure requires written policy, employee notice, certified labs, confirmation testing, and confidentiality. Idaho does NOT restrict cannabis-specific testing (cannabis remains illegal in Idaho).
facts: I.C. § 44-1701 et seq. is a model voluntary drug testing law. Employers can drug test without using the safe harbor framework, but lose the statutory protections. Idaho's cannabis prohibition (no medical or recreational use legal) means cannabis-specific testing is straightforward.
reasoning: The safe harbor structure incentivizes good testing practices while preserving employer flexibility.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho clinical staff: substantial benefit to using the safe harbor framework — premium discount, defamation immunity. Steps: written policy distributed in writing, employee notice, certified lab, MRO review, confirmation testing, confidentiality. Unlike WA's SB 5123, Idaho permits pre-employment cannabis testing without restriction. Correctional medical staff have clear safety-sensitive justification for testing. Document the policy and follow it consistently.
dos: Implement the safe harbor framework — premium discount and defamation immunity are real benefits
Distribute written drug testing policy in writing with acknowledgment
Use certified labs and MRO review for all testing
Apply the policy consistently across all hires and incidents
donts: Don't drug test without a written policy on file with employee acknowledgment
Don't skip confirmation testing — a positive initial screen needs GC/MS confirmation
Don't share results outside the need-to-know circle

name: Idaho Child Labor (I.C. §§ 44-1301 to 44-1308)
holding: Idaho prohibits employment of children under 14 in most commercial settings. Workers 14-15 have strict hour limits (3 hrs/school day, 18 hrs/school week, 8 hrs/non-school day, 40 hrs/non-school week). Workers 16-17 have no state hour caps but federal hazardous occupation orders still apply. No work permit required in Idaho.
facts: Title 44 Chapter 13 governs child labor. Federal FLSA child labor rules apply in parallel and are sometimes stricter.
reasoning: Standard child labor framework.
application: Probably not applicable to Ivy Medical — clinical staff are all 18+. Worth knowing for any office or support roles if Ivy ever hires summer interns. If a 16-17 year old is hired, federal hazardous occupation orders restrict their work — and correctional facility access likely requires 18+ for jail security reasons.
dos: Verify age for any non-adult hire
Apply federal FLSA child labor rules in parallel
donts: Don't place anyone under 18 in clinical or jail-access roles
Don't ignore federal hazardous occupation orders for 16-17 year olds

name: Idaho Jury Duty Protection (I.C. § 2-218)
holding: Idaho employers cannot discharge, threaten to discharge, or take any adverse action against an employee called for jury service. Employee must give reasonable notice. Failure to comply is a misdemeanor and creates a civil cause of action.
facts: Standard jury duty protection statute. Idaho does NOT require employers to pay during jury service — unpaid leave is the default.
reasoning: Jury service is a civic duty that requires statutory protection against employer retaliation.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho staff: protect jury service. Leave is unpaid by default — employees can use PTO if Ivy policy allows. Document the jury notice and the leave dates. Never characterize a post-jury performance issue as related to the absence.
dos: Accept jury duty notices and document the leave
Allow PTO use if employee elects
Restore the employee to their position upon return
donts: Don't retaliate against an employee called for jury service
Don't require employees to find their own coverage
Don't deduct jury duty days from PTO without employee authorization

name: Idaho Wage Notice at Hire (I.C. § 45-617)
holding: Idaho employers must notify employees at hire of the agreed wage rate, the basis of pay (hourly, salary, commission), and the regular payday.
facts: I.C. § 45-617 is a relatively light disclosure obligation compared to states like NY (full wage theft prevention act). Failure to comply doesn't create direct damages but can support a wage-claim or wage-confusion dispute.
reasoning: Wage transparency at hire reduces post-hoc disputes about agreed rates.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho hires: offer letter or signed wage agreement should include hourly/salary rate, all differentials (call, weekend, holiday), pay frequency, and paydays. Standard practice — but the documentation is the defense against Wage Claim Act disputes later. Coordinate with the travel-pay wage agreement (existing entry — 29 CFR 778.318) if dual-rate applies.
dos: Include all wage info in offer letters or hire paperwork
Get signed acknowledgment of the wage rate
Document any rate changes during employment with signed notice
Use a standardized wage agreement template
donts: Don't rely on verbal wage agreements
Don't change rates without written notice and acknowledgment
Don't omit differentials — they're part of the agreed wage

name: Idaho Noncompete Statutory Cap (I.C. § 44-2701 to § 44-2704)
holding: Already in IMELD as 'idaho-nurse-noncompete-physicians' — cross-reference. Idaho enforces noncompetes if reasonable as to duration, geography, and scope, with 18-month rebuttable presumption.
facts: Companion entry to the existing noncompete framework. Linking for completeness.
reasoning: Cross-reference for the Pass 1A index.
application: See existing entries 'idaho-nurse-noncompete-physicians' and 'blaskiewicz-2022' for full analysis. For Ivy Medical's Idaho correctional clinical staff, noncompetes are generally not worth the complication — use nonsolicitation, confidentiality, and Idaho Trade Secrets Act (I.C. § 48-801) protections instead.
dos: See related entries for full do/don't list
donts: Don't draft noncompetes longer than 18 months without additional consideration

name: Idaho Background Check Framework (Federal FCRA + I.C. § 18-7901)
holding: Idaho does NOT have a state ban-the-box law for private employers (state government employer ban-the-box exists). Federal FCRA governs third-party background check procedures: written disclosure on stand-alone form, written consent, pre-adverse action notice with copy of report, opportunity to dispute, final adverse action notice. Idaho-specific criminal records framework under I.C. § 18-7901.
facts: Idaho's private-employer hiring framework is comparatively unrestricted. FCRA still applies to all third-party background checks nationwide.
reasoning: Idaho preserved private-employer flexibility while federal FCRA standardizes the procedural protections.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho hiring: can ask about criminal history on the initial application (no state ban-the-box for private). MUST comply with federal FCRA for third-party checks (stand-alone disclosure form, written consent, pre-adverse action notice, dispute window). The jail's own background check is SEPARATE — facility access requires county/sheriff clearance regardless of Ivy's hiring decision. Coordinate the two: even Ivy's 'hired' candidate may fail the jail's clearance and be unable to work the contract.
dos: Use stand-alone FCRA disclosure form (not embedded in application)
Get written consent before any third-party check
Provide pre-adverse action notice with copy of report and dispute window (5-7 business days)
Coordinate Ivy's hiring decision with the jail's separate facility-access clearance
donts: Don't embed FCRA disclosure in the application form — must be stand-alone
Don't take adverse action without the pre-adverse-action notice and dispute window
Don't assume a candidate cleared by Ivy will be cleared by the jail — separate processes

name: Idaho Military Leave (I.C. § 46-407) + Federal USERRA
holding: Idaho military leave law mirrors federal USERRA. Employees called to military service (including National Guard) entitled to leave, reemployment at same or equivalent position with seniority intact, and protection from discrimination. Idaho National Guard service for state purposes covered by § 46-407.
facts: Federal USERRA provides the floor; states can add. Idaho's state-level provisions primarily address state-active-duty Guard service.
reasoning: Standard military protection framework.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho clinical staff who serve in National Guard or Reserve: must provide leave for training, deployment, and state activations. Reemployment rights kick in at return. Document the military notice and the leave dates. Coordinate with USERRA's escalator principle — returning employee gets the position they would have held had they remained continuously employed (raises, promotions). The Idaho corrections context: Guard activations for emergencies (wildfires, civil unrest) are real and trigger this protection.
dos: Accept and document military service notices
Reemploy returning service members at the escalator-principle position
Apply USERRA's 5-year cumulative limit correctly
Track Guard activations distinct from federal Reserve duty
donts: Don't terminate or 'replace' an employee on military leave
Don't reemploy at a lower position than the escalator would dictate
Don't apply non-military attendance points to military-leave absences

name: Idaho Political Speech in Employment (no specific statute)
cite: No comprehensive Idaho statute; common-law at-will + federal NLRA
holding: Idaho does NOT have a state statute protecting employee political speech or political activity outside of work. Employees are largely at-will and can be disciplined or terminated for political expression (subject to federal NLRA § 7 protected concerted activity for workplace-conditions speech).
facts: Unlike Montana (MCA § 39-2-313 — broad political activity protection), Idaho's at-will employment framework allows employer regulation of political expression with limited statutory restraint.
reasoning: Idaho's at-will preference means political-activity restraint is generally permissible.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho clinical staff: more discipline flexibility than Montana for off-duty political conduct. BUT — NLRA § 7 still protects workplace-conditions speech (pay, working conditions, scheduling) even if framed politically. The distinction: 'pay nurses more' (NLRA-protected) vs. 'vote for candidate X' (not protected, but discrimination based on perceived political affiliation may still implicate other claims). Consult before disciplining for social media political content.
dos: Distinguish workplace-conditions speech (protected) from pure political expression (not protected)
Coordinate with legal counsel before disciplining for social media political activity
Apply social media policies consistently across political viewpoints
donts: Don't discipline for workplace-condition complaints framed politically — NLRA still applies
Don't selectively enforce policies based on political viewpoint — that's its own claim
Don't extend Idaho's flexibility to Montana — MT § 39-2-313 is materially different

name: Idaho Wage Garnishment (I.C. § 11-712)
holding: Idaho wage garnishment follows federal CCPA limits — 25% of disposable earnings or amount by which disposable earnings exceed 30x federal minimum wage, whichever is less. No state floor above federal. Garnishment for child support, taxes, and student loans has separate rules.
facts: Idaho conforms to the federal CCPA garnishment framework without adding state protections.
reasoning: Default federal framework adopted without state modification.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho clinical staff: standard CCPA garnishment math via Paylocity defaults. No additional state exemption to layer in (unlike WA). Respond to writs promptly. Coordinate child support and tax levy garnishments through their separate frameworks.
dos: Respond to writs within state-specified deadlines
Apply CCPA math correctly (25% or 30x fed min wage rule)
Process child support and tax levies through their separate frameworks
donts: Don't apply WA's more generous exemption to Idaho garnishments
Don't retaliate against an employee subject to garnishment
Don't ignore a writ — judgment can attach to Ivy

name: Montana Wage Payment Act (MCA § 39-3-201 to § 39-3-216)
holding: Montana employers must pay wages at least semi-monthly (twice per month) on regular paydays designated in advance (MCA § 39-3-204). Final pay timing under § 39-3-205: immediately upon involuntary termination unless employer has written policy extending to next payday or within 15 days; voluntary quit pays at next regular payday or within 15 days.
facts: Montana's wage payment framework is in Title 39 Chapter 3 Part 2. Semi-monthly default is more frequent than WA's monthly minimum. The immediate-pay rule for involuntary termination is Montana's signature wage-payment quirk.
reasoning: Montana's policy choice — semi-monthly cadence and immediate involuntary-termination pay — reflects strong worker-protective framing consistent with WDEA.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana clinical staff: pay semi-monthly minimum. CRITICAL: involuntary termination triggers immediate pay UNLESS Ivy has a written policy extending to next payday or 15 days. WITHOUT THAT WRITTEN POLICY, the moment Ivy terminates a Montana employee, all wages are due that day — payroll cycle is no excuse. Build this into the handbook NOW: written policy extending final pay to next regular payday or 15 days, whichever is earlier. Otherwise prepare for same-day check cutting.
dos: Adopt written policy extending final pay to next regular payday or 15 days (eliminates same-day requirement)
Pay semi-monthly minimum
Document the timing of every termination for the audit trail
Coordinate with Paylocity for on-demand check capability as backup
donts: Don't terminate a MT employee without checking the written policy is in place
Don't assume 'process with next payroll' works in Montana — without written policy, immediate pay required
Don't lose the written policy in handbook revisions — it has to remain in effect

name: Montana Wage Deductions (MCA § 39-3-209)
holding: Montana employers may deduct from wages only those amounts: (1) required by law; (2) authorized in writing by the employee for the employee's benefit; OR (3) board, room, and incidentals at reasonable value. Third-party deductions benefitting the employer cannot reduce wages below minimum.
facts: MCA § 39-3-209 follows standard wage-deduction framework.
reasoning: Standard worker protection against unauthorized wage reductions.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana clinical staff: same disciplined approach as Idaho and WA — specific written authorization for each non-statutory deduction. The 'board, room, incidentals' provision is unlikely to apply in correctional medicine but exists. The minimum-wage floor for any employer-benefit deduction means uniform charges, equipment, training repayment cannot push wages below $10.85/hour (2026).
dos: Get specific written authorization per deduction
Verify any benefit-of-employer deduction doesn't push wages below $10.85/hr (2026 MT min)
Build a clear deduction authorization template
donts: Don't rely on general authorization for specific deductions
Don't reduce wages below state minimum through 'voluntary' deductions
Don't deduct for employer-benefit items without specific authorization

name: Montana Human Rights Act (MCA Title 49 Chapter 2)
holding: Montana Human Rights Act prohibits discrimination based on race, creed, religion, color, sex, national origin, age, physical or mental disability, marital status, AND political beliefs. Coverage threshold is 1+ employee. Marital status protection is distinctive. Like Idaho, MHRA does NOT explicitly include sexual orientation or gender identity, though Title VII (Bostock) covers these.
facts: MCA Title 49 Ch. 2 is the consolidated state anti-discrimination law, enforced by Montana Human Rights Bureau (within DLI). The 1-employee coverage threshold is the lowest in the country.
reasoning: Montana chose maximum coverage — every employer with any employee is subject to MHRA. The political beliefs protection is unusual and ties to MT's broader political activity protections.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana clinical staff: MHRA applies from the first hire. The political beliefs protection is unique — disciplining for political expression is potentially MHRA-actionable, in addition to MT § 39-2-313. Marital status protection covers single/married/divorced status. Coordinate with federal Title VII for SO/GI (Bostock). The 180-day filing deadline with MHB is critical — claims fail on procedural grounds often.
dos: Apply MHRA from the first Montana hire
Include marital status and political beliefs in EEO policies
Treat SO/GI as protected under Title VII regardless of MHRA silence
Train MT managers on political beliefs protection — easy to violate accidentally
donts: Don't apply Idaho's 5-employee threshold to Montana — MT is 1
Don't underestimate political beliefs protection — coordinates with § 39-2-313
Don't ignore the 180-day MHB filing deadline (employee or employer)

name: Montana Political Activity Protection (MCA § 39-2-313)
holding: Montana employers may not coerce, intimidate, or discharge employees for engaging in lawful political activity outside of work. Includes voting, supporting candidates, contributing money, attending political events, and political speech. One of the broadest political-activity protections in any state.
facts: MCA § 39-2-313 traces to early 1900s legislation protecting industrial workers from employer political pressure. The provision has been broadly construed.
reasoning: Montana's strong preference for workplace non-interference with citizenship activities.
application: Big practical difference from Idaho and Washington. For Ivy Medical's Montana clinical staff: CANNOT discipline for off-duty political activity. Social media political posts, campaign yard signs, attending rallies — all protected. Combined with MHRA's political-beliefs protection and the WDEA good-cause framework, post-probation MT employees have very strong protection. The narrow exception is when the political activity creates a direct, demonstrated workplace problem (e.g., harassment of coworkers during work hours). Train MT supervisors carefully — the casual reaction to a controversial social media post can violate three statutes simultaneously.
dos: Train MT managers that off-duty political activity is protected
Apply social media policies consistently across political viewpoints
Document workplace-impact specifically for any discipline that touches political conduct
Coordinate with legal counsel before any discipline related to political activity
donts: Don't discipline an MT employee for political speech, campaign work, or political donations
Don't apply 'professional conduct' standards selectively against political activity
Don't extend WA or ID flexibility to MT — § 39-2-313 is materially different
Don't underestimate the stacking — § 39-2-313 + MHRA + WDEA = serious exposure

name: Montana Workers' Compensation (MCA Title 39 Ch. 71)
holding: Montana workers' comp is mandatory for all employers (limited exceptions). Three-plan system: Plan 1 (self-insurance), Plan 2 (private insurance), Plan 3 (Montana State Fund — state-created competitive insurer). Exclusive remedy. Administered by Montana Workers' Compensation Court and DLI.
facts: MT's three-plan structure provides more flexibility than WA's monopolistic system. State Fund is the largest WC insurer in MT but not exclusive.
reasoning: Standard mandatory WC framework with insurance flexibility.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana clinical staff: WC coverage required from hire #1. Choose between private carrier and State Fund. Correctional medicine NCCI classification is elevated due to assault risk. Document every workplace injury. Workers' Compensation Court (a specialized court) handles disputes. Coordinate with the jail on inmate-on-staff assault reporting protocols.
dos: Maintain WC coverage from first MT hire
Use correct NCCI classification for correctional medicine
Document every workplace injury promptly
Coordinate WC claims with the jail's assault-reporting protocols
donts: Don't operate uncovered even briefly
Don't suppress claims to manage premium
Don't litigate workplace injuries outside the Workers' Comp Court (exclusive remedy)

name: Montana Unemployment Insurance (MCA Title 39 Ch. 51)
holding: Montana UI follows the standard federal-state framework. UI taxable wage base for 2026 reflects DLI's annual calculation. Administered by Montana Department of Labor & Industry. Misconduct-based discharges generally disqualify; WDEA-driven 'good cause' terminations don't necessarily mean misconduct under UI standards.
facts: MT UI is standard in structure. The interesting wrinkle is the WDEA overlay — what's 'good cause' for WDEA purposes isn't always 'misconduct' for UI purposes. They're different standards.
reasoning: Standard federal-state UI framework with MT-specific implementation.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana terminations: WDEA good-cause analysis and UI misconduct analysis are SEPARATE. A WDEA-permitted termination may still result in UI eligibility if the misconduct standard isn't met. Don't conflate the two. Maintain documentation supporting BOTH the WDEA justification AND any UI misconduct argument. Respond promptly to UI determinations — they affect Ivy's experience rating.
dos: Maintain separation documentation for both WDEA and UI standards
Respond to UI determinations within deadlines
Verify Paylocity is set to 2026 MT UI taxable wage base
Train HR on the distinct WDEA-good-cause vs. UI-misconduct standards
donts: Don't assume WDEA good cause = UI misconduct
Don't ignore UI hearings — they affect experience rating
Don't characterize layoffs as misconduct to fight UI claims

name: Montana Blacklisting Prohibition (MCA § 39-2-303 to § 39-2-305)
holding: Montana prohibits employers from preventing former employees from obtaining future employment through false statements or blacklists. Truthful statements about job performance are protected — Montana has a statutory truthful-reference privilege (MCA § 39-2-802). 'Service letter' on request requires the employer to provide a written statement of length of service, position held, and reason for separation.
facts: MCA § 39-2-303 is part of Montana's broader truthful-reference framework. The service-letter provision (MCA § 39-2-801) requires written response to discharge inquiries.
reasoning: Balancing protection against blacklisting with employer ability to share truthful performance information.
