application: For Ivy Medical's Montana former employees: be careful with reference checks. Sharing truthful, performance-based information is protected. False statements or actively trying to prevent re-employment is not. The service-letter requirement means a written request from a former employee triggers an obligation to provide length of service, position, and reason for separation. This connects to the WA written discharge statement and creates a consistent documentation discipline: the reason for separation must be true, specific, and defensible.
dos: Provide truthful, performance-based references when contacted
Respond to service-letter requests with accurate length of service, position, and reason
Use the statutory truthful-reference privilege (MCA § 39-2-802) to support honest disclosure
Coordinate references with discharge statements for consistency
donts: Don't make false statements about a former employee — blacklisting exposure
Don't refuse to give any reference if asked under the service-letter provision
Don't share information beyond what the privilege protects (e.g., medical info, confidential investigation details)

name: Montana Military Leave (MCA § 10-1-1003 et seq.) + USERRA
holding: Montana military leave provisions mirror federal USERRA with additional protections for state-active-duty Montana National Guard. Reemployment rights, anti-discrimination, escalator principle apply.
facts: Federal USERRA provides the floor; MT adds state-active-duty Guard coverage.
reasoning: Standard military protection framework with MT National Guard overlay.
application: For Ivy Medical's MT clinical staff in National Guard or Reserve: same USERRA-style protections as Idaho and WA. Document service notice and leave dates. Reemployment at escalator-principle position. Guard activations for state emergencies (wildfires, search and rescue) trigger MT state-law protections in addition to USERRA.
dos: Apply USERRA's escalator principle on reemployment
Document military leave separately from other absences
Apply MT National Guard state-activation protections in addition to USERRA
donts: Don't 'replace' an employee on military leave permanently
Don't count military absences against attendance policies
Don't reemploy at a lower position than the escalator dictates

name: Montana Jury Duty Protection (MCA § 3-15-301)
holding: Montana employers cannot discharge, discipline, or threaten an employee for responding to a jury summons. Reasonable notice required. Unpaid leave by default.
facts: Standard jury duty protection.
reasoning: Standard civic-duty protection.
application: Same operational handling as Idaho and WA. Accept the notice, restore the position, no adverse action.
dos: Accept jury duty notices and grant leave
Restore the employee to their position upon return
donts: Don't retaliate for jury service
Don't require self-arranged coverage

name: Montana Genetic Information Protection (MCA § 49-2-310)
holding: Montana prohibits employment discrimination based on genetic information. Mirrors federal GINA (42 U.S.C. § 2000ff) but with Montana's 1-employee coverage threshold.
facts: Federal GINA applies at 15+ employees; Montana's at 1+ via MHRA.
reasoning: Genetic information includes family medical history, genetic test results, and participation in genetic services. Standard protection framework.
application: For Ivy Medical: don't request or use genetic information in any employment decision. Beware of accidental acquisition — overhearing family medical history in conversation can complicate later employment decisions. Train managers to redirect such conversations.
dos: Train managers to avoid genetic information acquisition
Apply GINA-style firewall between any wellness program data and employment decisions
Document the source of any medical information learned about an employee
donts: Don't request family medical history in employment context
Don't use accidentally-acquired genetic information in any decision
Don't conflate ADA disability accommodation analysis with genetic information

name: Montana Safety Act (MCA Title 50 Ch. 71)
holding: Montana operates under federal OSHA (not a state-plan state). MCA Title 50 Ch. 71 (Montana Safety Act) supplements federal OSHA with additional provisions. Federal OSHA standards apply in full; MT state law adds employer safety committee requirements (15+ employees) and reporting obligations.
facts: Unlike WA (state-plan), Montana is under direct federal OSHA jurisdiction. MT Safety Act adds state-level structure but doesn't supplant OSHA standards.
reasoning: Montana chose federal OSHA coverage over a state plan.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana operations: federal OSHA standards govern (29 CFR 1910 general industry, including bloodborne pathogens, PPE, hazard communication). MT Safety Act requires safety committees at 15+ employees. Document the committee structure if applicable. Coordinate with the jail on facility-specific hazards. Federal OSHA inspections are less frequent than WA's DOSH but penalties for findings are real.
dos: Apply federal OSHA standards in full
Form a safety committee at 15+ MT employees per MT Safety Act
Maintain bloodborne pathogen training and PPE for correctional medical staff
Document safety training and incident response
donts: Don't assume MT lighter enforcement means lighter standards — they're identical to federal
Don't skip the safety committee at 15+ employees
Don't ignore the bloodborne pathogen standard in correctional settings

name: Idaho OSHA — Federal Jurisdiction
holding: Idaho is NOT a state-plan state. Federal OSHA applies directly. 29 CFR 1910 (general industry) and 29 CFR 1904 (recordkeeping) apply. No Idaho state-level supplement.
facts: Idaho relies entirely on federal OSHA. No state safety committee requirement, no state plan.
reasoning: Idaho's policy preference for federal-baseline compliance.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho operations: federal OSHA standards apply directly. Bloodborne pathogens (29 CFR 1910.1030), PPE (29 CFR 1910.132), hazard communication (29 CFR 1910.1200) all govern. OSHA Form 300 / 301 / 300A recordkeeping required. Coordinate with the jail on facility hazards.
dos: Apply federal OSHA standards in full
Maintain OSHA 300/301/300A logs
Train clinical staff on bloodborne pathogens, PPE, hazcom
donts: Don't assume Idaho's federal-coverage status means lighter rules
Don't skip OSHA recordkeeping — federal forms are mandatory
Don't ignore bloodborne pathogen training — high-stakes in corrections

name: Montana Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MCA § 30-14-401 to § 30-14-409)
holding: Montana adopted the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, protecting trade secrets against misappropriation by current or former employees. 'Trade secret' requires (1) information of economic value (2) from not being generally known and (3) subject to reasonable secrecy efforts. Remedies include injunction, damages, and attorney's fees.
facts: MUTSA is the statutory backbone for confidentiality protection in Montana given MUTSA's near-total ban on noncompetes.
reasoning: MUTSA balances worker mobility with protection against actual trade secret misappropriation. Doesn't require a noncompete; protects against specific bad acts.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana operations: MUTSA is the primary tool for protecting clinical protocols, pricing models, contract terms, and client information. Combined with confidentiality agreements and the Montana ban on noncompetes, the approach is: clear written confidentiality agreement at hire, mark confidential information, take reasonable secrecy measures (need-to-know access, password protection, document marking), and rely on MUTSA remedies if a former employee misappropriates. Document that secrecy measures exist — without them, MUTSA protection fails.
dos: Use written confidentiality agreements at hire
Mark confidential information as such
Apply need-to-know access controls
Document the reasonable secrecy measures so MUTSA protection holds up
donts: Don't rely on MUTSA without taking reasonable secrecy measures — that's a fatal flaw
Don't claim trade secret status for information that's actually public or industry-standard
Don't try to use MUTSA as a backdoor noncompete — MT courts will see through it

name: Estelle v. Gamble
holding: Deliberate indifference to a prisoner's serious medical needs violates the Eighth Amendment's prohibition on cruel and unusual punishment. The standard requires both an objectively serious medical need AND a subjectively culpable state of mind (knowing of and disregarding an excessive risk to inmate health or safety).
facts: J.W. Gamble was a Texas prisoner who suffered a back injury during a work assignment. He alleged inadequate medical care over several months — delayed treatment, missed appointments, dismissive responses to his complaints. He sued under 42 U.S.C. § 1983.
reasoning: Justice Marshall, for the Court, established that the Eighth Amendment imposes a duty on government to provide medical care to those it incarcerates. The 'deliberate indifference' standard distinguishes constitutional violations from mere negligence or medical malpractice. Negligent medical care is a state tort issue; deliberate indifference is constitutional.
application: **The foundational case for Ivy Medical's existence.** Every clinician working in a jail is operating under the Estelle framework. Ivy as a private contractor providing medical services is a state actor for § 1983 purposes (West v. Atkins, 1988) and can be sued directly. Operational implications: (1) document every clinical encounter, including refusals to treat and reasons; (2) take all medical complaints seriously — written sick-call procedures matter; (3) don't let custody staff override clinical judgment without documentation; (4) clinical decisions delayed by custody constraints (lockdown, transport unavailability) need documentation showing the medical urgency was assessed; (5) train clinical staff that 'deliberate indifference' isn't malpractice — it's knowingly disregarding a serious risk.
dos: Document every clinical encounter including triage decisions, refusals, and follow-up plans
Treat any inmate medical complaint as triggering an assessment obligation, even if appears trivial
Maintain a written sick-call system with documented response times
Escalate cases where custody constraints (lockdown, transport) delay needed care
Train staff on the deliberate indifference standard — it's a knowledge-plus-disregard test
donts: Don't dismiss inmate complaints as 'malingering' without clinical assessment and documentation
Don't let custody staff override clinical decisions without escalation and documentation
Don't delay urgent care for administrative convenience
Don't write sarcastic or dismissive notes in the medical record — they become exhibits
Don't conflate § 1983 deliberate indifference with state-law malpractice — they're different standards

name: Farmer v. Brennan
holding: Defines the 'deliberate indifference' standard subjectively: the official must (1) know of a substantial risk of serious harm AND (2) disregard that risk. Mere negligence — even gross negligence — is not enough. The official's actual knowledge can be proven by circumstantial evidence (the risk was obvious).
facts: Dee Farmer, a transgender woman housed in a federal men's prison, was sexually assaulted by another inmate. She sued under Bivens (the federal analog to § 1983), alleging prison officials knew of her vulnerability and were deliberately indifferent. The case clarified what 'deliberate indifference' means.
reasoning: Justice Souter held that the test is subjective — the official must actually know of the risk. BUT actual knowledge can be inferred from circumstances; if the risk was 'obvious,' a factfinder can conclude the official knew. The standard sits between mere negligence (not enough) and purpose to harm (more than required).
application: For Ivy Medical's clinicians: the subjective knowledge test is BOTH a protection and a trap. Protection: a clinician who genuinely didn't know about a condition isn't constitutionally liable for missing it. Trap: documented warnings, prior incidents, or 'obvious' presentations create knowledge that disregarding becomes deliberate indifference. The practical rule: when something is flagged (by inmate, by custody, by another clinician, by prior records), the response must be documented. Silence in the chart equals disregard of a known risk. Train clinical staff: if you see a risk flag, address it on the record — even if the answer is 'monitored, no intervention indicated at this time.'
dos: Document the basis for clinical decisions when risk flags exist (prior records, family history, complaint patterns)
Respond in the chart to communications from custody about inmate condition
Note 'obvious' presentations even when ruling out intervention — silence reads as disregard
Maintain ongoing assessment for known-risk inmates (mental health holds, suicide watch, withdrawal)
donts: Don't ignore documented warnings or prior incident reports — that creates knowledge
Don't rely on lack of formal notice — Farmer allows knowledge to be inferred from obvious circumstances
Don't treat the subjective test as a free pass; obvious risks ARE imputed knowledge

name: West v. Atkins
holding: A private physician contracted by the state to provide medical care to prisoners acts under color of state law for purposes of § 1983 liability. Private contractor status does NOT insulate clinicians (or contractor entities) from constitutional claims.
facts: Quincy West, a North Carolina prisoner, received care from Dr. Atkins, a private physician under contract with the state. West sued Dr. Atkins under § 1983 for inadequate care. The 4th Circuit dismissed on the ground that Dr. Atkins was a private actor. SCOTUS reversed.
reasoning: Justice Blackmun held that when a state assumes a constitutional duty (medical care for those it incarcerates) and contracts that duty out, the contractor is performing a state function and acts under color of state law. The state cannot delegate away its constitutional obligations.
application: **Critical for Ivy Medical's risk posture.** Ivy and every Ivy clinician are state actors for § 1983 purposes despite Ivy being a private contractor. The county sheriff's delegation of medical services to Ivy does NOT shift constitutional liability away from Ivy. Both the entity (Ivy) and individual clinicians can be named directly in § 1983 actions. Practical implications: maintain professional liability insurance with § 1983 coverage (not all malpractice policies include it); the corporate indemnification provisions in Ivy's contracts with sheriffs cannot waive § 1983 claims by inmates; clinical staff need to understand they personally can be sued in addition to Ivy.
dos: Maintain professional liability insurance with explicit § 1983 coverage (not all policies include it)
Train clinical staff on personal liability exposure — they're sued individually, not just Ivy
Document clinical decision-making with the assumption every chart entry may be litigation evidence
Coordinate insurance and indemnification provisions in jail contracts with counsel
donts: Don't assume private-contractor status insulates Ivy or its clinicians from § 1983
Don't rely on jail contract indemnification to cover § 1983 claims — inmates aren't bound by it
Don't let clinicians believe 'the county is liable, not me' — they're personally exposed

name: Monell v. Department of Social Services
holding: Municipalities (and by extension private entities acting under color of state law, including correctional medical contractors) can be sued under § 1983, but ONLY for constitutional violations caused by an official policy, practice, or custom — NOT under respondeat superior (employer liability for employees' acts).
facts: NYC employees sued under § 1983 challenging maternity leave policy. The Court overruled Monroe v. Pape's holding that municipalities couldn't be sued, while limiting the basis for entity liability.
reasoning: Justice Brennan held entity § 1983 liability requires a 'policy, practice, or custom' — meaning the entity itself, through its official decision-makers or established patterns, caused the constitutional violation. A single employee's misconduct without entity-level cause doesn't make the entity liable.
application: **The most important § 1983 doctrine for Ivy Medical's institutional exposure.** Ivy is NOT automatically liable when an Ivy clinician commits deliberate indifference — Monell requires showing Ivy's policy, practice, or custom caused the violation. Common Monell theories that ARE used against medical contractors: (1) inadequate staffing policies that made the deliberate indifference foreseeable; (2) deficient training (failure to train clinicians on suicide risk assessment, MAT, withdrawal management); (3) cost-cutting policies that delayed or denied care; (4) widespread practice tolerated by management. **PROTECTION:** maintain robust written policies, document training, audit clinical performance against policy. **EXPOSURE:** verbal-only policies, untrained staff, and chronic understaffing that management knew about are textbook Monell evidence.
dos: Maintain comprehensive written policies covering sick-call, MAT, suicide risk, withdrawal, chronic care
Document training of every clinician on every policy with attendance records
Audit clinical performance against policy and document corrective actions
Track staffing levels and document escalation of staffing concerns to ownership
donts: Don't rely on verbal policies — Monell plaintiffs love 'unwritten rules'
Don't tolerate widespread non-compliance with written policy — it becomes a 'custom'
Don't ignore chronic understaffing — it's the #1 Monell theory in correctional medicine
Don't conflate Ivy's policies with the jail's — they're separate; document Ivy's chain of clinical authority

name: City of Canton v. Harris
holding: Inadequate training of employees can be the 'policy' that supports Monell entity liability — but only when the failure to train amounts to 'deliberate indifference' to the rights of persons with whom the untrained employees come into contact.
facts: Geraldine Harris was arrested and held in a Canton, Ohio jail. She had medical needs that police missed. The Court considered whether the city's failure to train officers on identifying medical needs supported § 1983 liability.
reasoning: Justice White held that failure-to-train claims require (1) inadequate training, (2) deliberate indifference to the consequences of inadequacy, AND (3) actual causation of the constitutional violation. 'Deliberate indifference' in the training context means the entity knew of a need for training and chose not to provide it — typically shown by a pattern of similar incidents.
application: For Ivy Medical: failure-to-train is the second most common Monell theory after staffing. Specific high-risk training topics where inadequate training becomes deliberate indifference: (1) suicide risk assessment, (2) opioid withdrawal management, (3) recognition of acute mental health crises, (4) MAT protocols, (5) detoxification monitoring, (6) communicable disease screening (TB, hep), (7) PREA staff obligations. Document every training, every attendee, every assessment of competency post-training. After ANY adverse incident, do a documented training-adequacy review. Pattern incidents without training response is textbook Canton.
dos: Maintain a training matrix mapping high-risk clinical topics to training records
Document every training session — date, attendees, content, assessment of competency
Conduct training-adequacy reviews after adverse incidents
Track patterns of similar incidents to identify training gaps before plaintiffs do
donts: Don't rely on initial orientation as 'sufficient training' for high-risk topics — ongoing reinforcement is the standard
Don't ignore patterns of adverse incidents in the same clinical area — that's Canton 'deliberate indifference' to training gaps
Don't have clinicians signing off on training they didn't actually receive — that's worse than no training

name: PREA Federal Standards (28 CFR Part 115)
holding: Prison Rape Elimination Act federal standards require facilities to: prevent, detect, and respond to sexual abuse and harassment of inmates. Standards cover staff training, inmate screening, reporting mechanisms, investigation procedures, medical/mental health care for victims, and audits. Applies to adult and juvenile facilities. Failure to certify compliance can affect federal funding.
facts: PREA (42 U.S.C. § 15601 et seq.) was enacted in 2003; the implementing regulations at 28 CFR Part 115 became final in 2012. Standards apply to adult prisons/jails (Subpart A), lockups (Subpart B), community confinement facilities (Subpart C), and juvenile facilities (Subpart D).
reasoning: Congress and DOJ recognized that sexual abuse in detention was widespread, under-reported, and harmful to inmates and staff. The standards create operational obligations and external audit requirements.
application: **Critical for Ivy Medical clinicians as both responders to sexual abuse and as potential subjects of PREA screening.** Operational implications: (1) all Ivy clinical staff need PREA training before working in any facility (28 CFR 115.31 / 115.32); (2) clinical staff are mandatory reporters for any PREA allegation they learn about — failure to report violates standards; (3) victims of sexual abuse get specific medical/mental health care under 115.83 — Ivy clinicians provide this; (4) Ivy must screen its own staff for PREA-related disqualifying history under 115.17; (5) cross-gender supervision rules affect when male clinical staff can supervise female inmates and vice versa; (6) zero-tolerance policy required in writing. The PREA compliance check is part of every Ivy contract with a sheriff.
dos: Train every clinical staff member on PREA before they work in any facility (115.31)
Document PREA training with attendance and content records
Treat every PREA allegation learned by clinical staff as triggering mandatory reporting
Maintain PREA victim medical/mental health response protocols per 115.83
Screen all clinical hires per 115.17 (sexual abuse/harassment background)
Apply cross-gender supervision restrictions in clinical encounters where required
donts: Don't treat PREA training as one-time — refresher training is required
Don't allow clinical staff to dismiss inmate sexual abuse disclosures — mandatory reporting
Don't conduct cross-gender intimate exams without PREA-compliant protocols
Don't hire clinical staff without the 115.17 screening (background, prior employment, sexual abuse history)

name: PREA Staff Screening Standard (28 CFR 115.17)
holding: Facilities must not hire or promote anyone who has engaged in sexual abuse in confinement, has been convicted of sexual abuse, OR has been civilly or administratively adjudicated for sexual abuse. Background checks must include criminal records, prior employer contact, and inquiry about substantiated allegations.
facts: PREA's screening standard creates a strict bar on hiring individuals with substantiated sexual abuse history in any setting. Applies to all staff, contractors, and volunteers who have contact with inmates.
reasoning: DOJ recognized that sexual abuse in detention often involves perpetrators with prior history. Pre-employment screening is a primary prevention mechanism.
application: For Ivy Medical: every clinical hire who will have inmate contact (essentially all) must pass 115.17 screening. Specific obligations: (1) criminal background check including sexual offense history; (2) contact every prior employer back at least 5 years to ask about substantiated sexual abuse allegations; (3) ask the applicant directly about substantiated allegations or convictions — false answer is grounds for refusal/termination; (4) document the screening for each hire. The check is in addition to FCRA background check and state professional licensing verification. Keep the documentation in the personnel file — facilities will audit.
dos: Conduct 115.17 screening on every clinical hire and contractor
Contact prior employers back 5 years and document the inquiry and response
Ask applicants directly about substantiated allegations and convictions
Maintain 115.17 documentation in personnel files for PREA audit
Apply screening to clinicians being promoted into supervisory roles
donts: Don't skip prior-employer outreach because it's awkward — required by 115.17
Don't hire someone with substantiated sexual abuse history regardless of how good their other credentials are
Don't lose the screening documentation — auditors require it

name: PREA Victim Medical & Mental Health Care (28 CFR 115.83)
holding: Inmates who report sexual abuse must receive (1) timely, unimpeded access to emergency medical treatment and crisis intervention services, (2) follow-up medical and mental health care, (3) confidential mental health counseling, and (4) sexual abuse forensic examination by a Sexual Assault Forensic Examiner (SAFE) or Sexual Assault Nurse Examiner (SANE) when possible.
facts: The standard codifies the medical/mental health response to PREA incidents.
reasoning: Adequate post-incident care is both a humane obligation and a forensic necessity for criminal/administrative response.
application: For Ivy Medical's clinical staff in jails: when a PREA allegation comes through, the clinical response is non-discretionary. Steps: (1) immediate medical assessment and emergency care; (2) preserve evidence per chain-of-custody protocols if a SAFE/SANE exam is being conducted; (3) crisis intervention available within hours, not days; (4) confidential mental health counseling — confidential means the substance is protected from custody disclosure unless the inmate consents (intersects with HIPAA correctional carve-out below); (5) document the medical response. Have a written PREA medical response protocol approved by Ivy clinical leadership and the facility.
dos: Maintain a written PREA medical response protocol per facility
Train clinical staff on the steps: assessment, evidence preservation, crisis intervention, follow-up
Coordinate with local SAFE/SANE programs for forensic exams when needed
Treat PREA mental health counseling as confidential unless inmate consents otherwise
donts: Don't delay PREA medical response for custody convenience
Don't conduct sexual assault forensic exams without SAFE/SANE-equivalent training and chain of custody
Don't share PREA mental health counseling content with custody absent inmate consent
Don't write off complaints as 'inmate-to-inmate drama' without medical response

name: Hudson v. McMillian
holding: Excessive force claims under the Eighth Amendment don't require 'serious injury' — the question is whether force was applied 'in a good-faith effort to maintain or restore discipline, or maliciously and sadistically to cause harm.' Significant for clinical staff who document injuries from use-of-force events.
facts: Keith Hudson was beaten by Louisiana corrections officers while shackled. The 5th Circuit dismissed because injuries were 'minor.' SCOTUS reversed.
reasoning: Justice O'Connor held that the constitutional inquiry is about the nature of the force and intent, not the magnitude of injury. The Eighth Amendment is violated by maliciously inflicted force even if the injury is minor.
application: For Ivy Medical clinicians: you will document injuries from use-of-force events. The chart entry matters constitutionally. Specific guidance: (1) document objective findings — location, type, severity of injuries; (2) note the patient's account of what happened (this is evidence); (3) photograph injuries when policy permits; (4) don't characterize or minimize ('superficial,' 'no significant injury') — let the objective findings speak; (5) recognize that 'minor' injuries can support § 1983 claims if force was malicious. Coordinate with facility on use-of-force documentation and reporting — Ivy clinicians may be witnesses in administrative or civil proceedings.
dos: Document objective findings from use-of-force exams without editorial characterization
Photograph injuries when policy permits and chain of custody is maintained
Record the patient's account of the incident as reported history
Coordinate with facility on use-of-force documentation requirements
donts: Don't minimize injuries in documentation — 'minor' carries no protective weight constitutionally
Don't write narrative conclusions about whether force was justified — that's not the clinical role
Don't let custody pressure modify clinical documentation of injuries

name: Kingsley v. Hendrickson
holding: For PRETRIAL DETAINEES (not yet convicted), excessive force claims are evaluated under the Fourteenth Amendment Due Process Clause using an OBJECTIVE reasonableness standard — NOT the subjective 'maliciously and sadistically' Eighth Amendment standard. This is a more plaintiff-friendly standard for pretrial detainees.
facts: Michael Kingsley was a pretrial detainee in a Wisconsin jail who was tased by officers. He sued under § 1983. The case asked whether his Fourteenth Amendment claim required subjective intent (like Hudson) or objective reasonableness (like Graham v. Connor for arrests).
reasoning: Justice Breyer held that pretrial detainees, unlike convicted prisoners, are not being 'punished' — so the Eighth Amendment doesn't apply. The Fourteenth Amendment Due Process Clause governs, and the standard is objective reasonableness — a less demanding test for plaintiffs.
application: **Hugely important for Ivy Medical in COUNTY JAILS** — county jails hold mostly pretrial detainees (haven't been convicted). The Kingsley objective standard applies, not the Estelle/Farmer subjective standard, for excessive force AND increasingly for medical care claims. Circuits have split on whether Kingsley extends to pretrial detainee medical care claims; 2nd, 7th, 9th Circuits say yes; others say Estelle still applies. **For Ivy operations: assume the more plaintiff-friendly objective standard applies to pretrial detainees in any case.** This means showing 'didn't know about the risk' is harder — objective unreasonableness is judged from outside. Document clinical decisions thoroughly because the question becomes 'was this objectively reasonable given the information?' not 'did the clinician subjectively know.'
dos: Document clinical decisions with the assumption objective reasonableness is the standard for pretrial detainees
Treat county jail inmates as pretrial detainees by default (most are)
Train clinicians that the legal standard differs for pretrial vs. post-conviction inmates
Track circuit-specific application of Kingsley to medical care claims (9th Cir. is plaintiff-friendly for WA/ID/MT cases)
donts: Don't apply the subjective deliberate-indifference standard reflexively to pretrial detainees — Kingsley may apply
Don't underestimate how much harder the objective standard is to defend
Don't conflate pretrial detainees (presumption of innocence) with convicted prisoners (different constitutional analysis)

name: HIPAA Correctional Carve-out (45 CFR 164.512(k)(5))
court: U.S. Dept. of Health & Human Services
holding: Covered entities (including correctional medical contractors) MAY disclose protected health information (PHI) of an inmate to a correctional institution or law enforcement official having custody of the inmate, without authorization, IF the disclosure is necessary for: (A) providing health care to the inmate; (B) the health and safety of the inmate, other inmates, or staff; (C) the health and safety of officers/employees transporting inmates; (D) law enforcement on the premises; OR (E) the administration and maintenance of the safety, security, and good order of the correctional facility.
facts: 45 CFR 164.512(k)(5) is the HIPAA correctional carve-out. It's the most significant departure from standard HIPAA privacy in any setting. Importantly: the carve-out is PERMISSIVE — covered entities MAY disclose, but are not required to.
reasoning: HHS recognized that the inmate context creates a tension between privacy and the legitimate operational needs of custody. The carve-out resolves it in favor of disclosure when needed for one of the enumerated purposes. The carve-out ends when the inmate is released — at that point, standard HIPAA applies again.
application: **The single most important HIPAA rule for Ivy Medical's operations.** Operational implications: (1) clinicians CAN share PHI with custody staff when needed for the enumerated purposes — diabetic management, medication administration, mental health monitoring, suicide watch, infectious disease control; (2) clinicians SHOULD share — refusing to share necessary clinical info with custody can become deliberate indifference if it causes harm; (3) the carve-out does NOT cover: disclosure unrelated to the listed purposes (e.g., gossip, retaliation, casual interest), disclosure after release; (4) PREA-related mental health counseling has a CONFIDENTIALITY exception (28 CFR 115.83) that operates differently — confidential counseling content shouldn't be disclosed to custody without inmate consent; (5) maintain a written disclosure protocol distinguishing routine clinical info-sharing from confidential mental health content. Train clinical staff carefully — this is where HIPAA-trained civilian clinicians get tripped up; they default to non-disclosure when disclosure is permitted and clinically necessary.
dos: Train clinicians on the 5 enumerated purposes — health care, inmate/staff safety, transport, law enforcement on premises, facility security
Share clinical info with custody when needed for the enumerated purposes (medication, infectious disease, suicide risk)
Maintain a written disclosure protocol distinguishing routine sharing from PREA-confidential content
Recognize the carve-out ENDS at release — standard HIPAA resumes for follow-up care, continuity records
Document the basis for sharing PHI with custody (clinical purpose under the rule)
donts: Don't apply standard hospital HIPAA reflexively in jails — disclosure is often permitted
Don't share PHI with custody for purposes OUTSIDE the 5 enumerated categories
Don't disclose PREA-confidential mental health content to custody without inmate consent
Don't continue sharing PHI with the jail after the inmate is released
Don't withhold clinically necessary info from custody and then blame HIPAA — that's deliberate indifference

name: WA City and County Jails Act (RCW 70.48)
holding: Ch. 70.48 RCW is the statutory framework for WA city and county jails. Establishes the Washington State Corrections Standards Board (CSB), the jail classification system, and authority for the CSB to adopt minimum standards (codified at WAC Title 289). Medical care provisions require adequate medical care for inmates.
facts: RCW 70.48 originally enacted 1977. Establishes CSB (RCW 70.48.040), jail classification (RCW 70.48.080), and standard-setting authority (RCW 70.48.050). The CSB's standards in WAC Title 289 are the operational requirements.
reasoning: WA created a state-level oversight framework for county and city jails, including health-care standards. Unlike Idaho (voluntary sheriff's association standards) or Montana (DOC-based standards), WA has formal regulatory standards.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA jail contracts: RCW 70.48 + WAC Title 289 standards apply to the jails Ivy serves. Ivy's clinical operations must align with the WAC 289 health care standards (medical examining room, secure medication storage, prescription drug security, etc.). The contract with the sheriff should reference the CSB standards. Audit Ivy's operations against the WAC 289 standards at each facility — gaps create both regulatory and § 1983 exposure (failure to meet state-mandated minimums supports Monell claims).
dos: Map Ivy's clinical operations to WAC Title 289 health-care requirements per facility
Maintain physical infrastructure (exam room, medication storage) per WAC 289 specs
Reference CSB standards in Ivy-sheriff contracts
Audit each facility against WAC 289 standards periodically
donts: Don't assume the sheriff handles CSB compliance — Ivy's clinical operations are part of the equation
Don't ignore physical-plant requirements (e.g., medical exam room specs) just because they pre-date Ivy's contract
Don't operate medication storage outside WAC 289 security requirements

name: WA Jail Health Care Standards (WAC Title 289, Health Care)
holding: WAC Title 289 sets minimum health care standards for WA jails: medical screening at intake, sick-call procedures, medication administration, mental health screening, suicide prevention, dental access, communicable disease screening, prescription drug security. Standards apply to the jail; medical contractors operate within them.
facts: WA's regulatory standards for jail health care, adopted by the Corrections Standards Board under authority of RCW 70.48.050. Specific subsections govern intake screening, sick call, medical records, medication administration, etc.
reasoning: WA chose to regulate jail health care at the state administrative level, providing more concrete operational standards than the constitutional baseline alone.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA jail operations: WAC 289 health-care standards are the operational floor. Specific high-attention areas: (1) intake medical screening within the WAC-specified time after booking; (2) sick-call request system with documented response times; (3) medication storage and administration per WAC security requirements; (4) mental health screening at intake (suicide risk); (5) communicable disease screening (TB primarily) per WAC 289 + public health rules. Build Ivy's WA protocols around WAC 289 explicitly. The state standard becomes important in Monell defense — 'we met or exceeded WAC 289' is strong evidence against deliberate indifference at the policy level.
dos: Reference WAC 289 specifically in Ivy's WA clinical protocols
Audit intake screening turnaround times against WAC 289 standards
Document sick-call response times consistent with WAC 289 requirements
Maintain medication administration logs per WAC 289 security specs
donts: Don't run a sick-call system without documented response standards
Don't skip intake screening for short stays — WAC 289 doesn't carve out by length of detention
Don't store medications outside WAC 289 security requirements

name: Idaho Jail Standards (ISA Voluntary)
holding: Idaho has NO state-level regulatory standards for jails. The Idaho Sheriffs' Association (ISA), a non-governmental body, maintains voluntary jail standards including a Health Care Services chapter. ICRMP (Idaho Counties Risk Management Program) requires 8 mandatory standards for insurance coverage, including Health Care Services. Inspections are voluntary and annual; results are not public.
facts: ISA created the standards in 1978 to forestall § 1983 litigation. April 2024 master version is the current edition. The ICRMP framework makes 8 of the standards effectively mandatory for sheriffs who want insurance: Personnel, Training, Fire Safety, Inmate Observation, Strip Searches, Health Care Services, Inmate Rights re: Rape, Physical Facility. As of 2025 reporting, one-third of Idaho jails failed ISA inspections, with delayed medical care among common findings.
reasoning: Idaho's policy choice favors local sheriff autonomy over state-level regulation. The ISA + ICRMP framework provides quasi-regulation through insurance and association pressure rather than law.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho jail contracts: the regulatory floor is the constitutional minimum (Estelle/Farmer/West/Monell) plus the ISA Health Care Services standard for ICRMP-covered jails. Operational implications: (1) Idaho has the WEAKEST regulatory framework of the three states — most exposure flows from constitutional standards, not state law; (2) ISA standards are persuasive but not binding — they're useful evidence in § 1983 cases ('Ivy met the ISA standard' or 'the jail violated its own ISA-track standard'); (3) check whether each Ivy-served jail is ICRMP-covered — if yes, ICRMP's audit findings will affect insurance, indirectly affecting Ivy's operations; (4) maintain documented compliance with ISA Health Care Services standards even where the jail doesn't insist — the audit trail protects Ivy in litigation.
dos: Obtain and follow the current ISA Jail Standards Health Care Services chapter
Document compliance with ISA standards even where the jail doesn't insist
Check ICRMP coverage for each Ivy-served jail
Use ISA standards as benchmark in Ivy's internal clinical audits
donts: Don't treat 'no state oversight' as 'no liability' — § 1983 exposure is the same
Don't ignore ISA standards because they're voluntary — they're persuasive in litigation
Don't rely on the jail's ISA compliance — verify Ivy's clinical operations meet the standard independently

name: Montana Jail Standards (DOC)
holding: Montana DOC publishes Montana Jail Standards covering admission, classification, security, fire safety, food, health protection (Standard 09.01), medical care, mental health, and PREA. Standards reference ALDF (American Correctional Association Adult Local Detention Facilities) and ARM Title 37 Ch. 110 for food and health protection. Compliance is monitored by DOC but enforcement varies.
facts: Montana's jail standards integrate state administrative rules (ARM) with national association standards. The 2016 update is the most recent comprehensive version. DOC has policy directives (4.5.x series) specifically for health care in DOC-run facilities; county jails have parallel standards.
reasoning: Montana's framework is hybrid — DOC-published standards with cross-references to state regulatory rules and national accreditation standards.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana jail contracts: Montana Jail Standards Health Protection chapter (09.01) is the operational reference. Specific obligations: (1) food service compliance with ARM 37 Ch. 110 (when Ivy clinical staff are involved in dietary planning); (2) restraint equipment used only on physician direction for medical reasons per Standard 07.10.01; (3) medical examination requirements at intake; (4) mental health screening protocols; (5) PREA compliance (cross-references federal standards). Coordinate with the sheriff/jail administrator on which Montana Jail Standards apply at each facility. Document compliance for both regulatory and § 1983 defense purposes.
dos: Obtain the current Montana Jail Standards from DOC and align Ivy clinical operations
Apply Standard 09.01 (Health Protection) per facility
Document compliance with restraint-equipment-medical-direction rules (07.10.01)
Cross-reference ARM Title 37 Ch. 110 for food service obligations when applicable
donts: Don't authorize restraint equipment use for non-medical reasons — clinical role is narrow under 07.10.01
Don't apply WA-style state oversight framework to Montana — DOC oversight is different
Don't ignore the standards because they're DOC-published rather than ARM-codified — they're operational

name: WA Nursing Practice in Correctional Settings (RCW 18.79)
holding: RNs and LPNs practicing in WA correctional settings remain subject to the full Nursing Practice Act (RCW 18.79) and Nursing Commission rules (WAC 246-840). Scope of practice does NOT expand because of the setting. RNs may assess and triage; LPNs perform delegated tasks under RN/MD supervision. Medication administration follows standard scope.
facts: WA's Nursing Commission has not adopted setting-specific rules expanding correctional nursing scope. The setting can affect what's clinically appropriate (austerity, security constraints) but not the licensing scope.
reasoning: Professional licensing boards generally don't relax scope for difficult settings — the standard of care is the same, even when operational conditions are tougher.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA clinical staff: nursing scope rules apply unchanged. Common scope traps in correctional medicine: (1) LPN doing assessments that require RN scope (initial intake, suicide risk screening — typically RN); (2) RN making prescribing decisions outside protocol scope; (3) nursing staff signing for medication that wasn't administered per scope; (4) telephonic provider orders documented without proper RN authentication. Maintain Ivy's nursing scope-of-practice protocol that maps tasks to licensure level. Spot-audit charts for scope compliance — Nursing Commission complaints from inmates do happen and licensure exposure is personal.
dos: Maintain written scope-of-practice protocols mapping correctional tasks to licensure level
Train LPNs on the line between delegated tasks and RN-scope assessments
Audit nursing documentation for scope compliance periodically
Coordinate provider-order workflows so RN authentication is documented
donts: Don't let LPNs perform RN-scope assessments because RN coverage is thin
Don't extend scope based on 'jail nursing tradition' — the licensing rules don't bend
Don't ignore the personal licensure exposure — Nursing Commission can investigate inmate complaints

name: Idaho Nursing Practice in Correctional Settings (I.C. § 54-1401 et seq.)
holding: Idaho's Nursing Practice Act (I.C. § 54-1401 et seq.) and Board of Nursing rules (IDAPA 23.01.01) apply uniformly to all settings including correctional. RN scope includes assessment, planning, intervention; LPN scope is more limited and supervised. Idaho's APRN scope is among the broader in the country (full practice authority).
facts: Idaho's Nursing Practice Act parallels other states. Idaho is a full-practice-authority state for APRNs (NPs can practice independently without physician supervision agreement), which simplifies some correctional NP arrangements.
reasoning: Standard professional licensing framework with Idaho's APRN-friendly approach.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho clinical staff: same scope rules as WA — the setting doesn't expand scope. Idaho's APRN independence is operationally useful: Ivy NPs in Idaho jails don't need supervising physician agreements (in contrast to WA's collaboration requirement or MT's similar rule). This can simplify staffing models. RN and LPN scope same as WA — maintain scope protocols mapped to licensure level.
dos: Leverage Idaho's APRN full-practice authority for streamlined NP staffing models
Maintain scope protocols mapped to RN/LPN/APRN licensure
Coordinate APRN scope with controlled substances rules (DEA registration still required)
donts: Don't conflate Idaho's APRN independence with expanded RN/LPN scope — those are unchanged
Don't operate APRN scope outside the licensure category just because Idaho is FPA

name: Montana Nursing Practice in Correctional Settings (MCA Title 37 Ch. 8)
holding: Montana's Nursing Practice Act (MCA § 37-8-101 et seq.) and Board of Nursing rules (ARM 24.159) apply uniformly to all settings. RN and LPN scope follow standard professional framework. Montana is a full-practice-authority state for APRNs (consistent with Idaho).
facts: Montana parallels Idaho in nursing framework, including APRN full practice authority.
reasoning: Standard professional licensing framework.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana clinical staff: same scope discipline as WA and Idaho. Montana APRN independence simplifies NP staffing similar to Idaho. Maintain scope protocols. Note: Montana Board of Nursing has been active in correctional cases — they take inmate complaints seriously.
dos: Maintain scope protocols mapped to RN/LPN/APRN licensure
Leverage Montana APRN full-practice authority for NP staffing
Take Board of Nursing inquiries seriously — Montana is active in correctional discipline cases
donts: Don't extend scope based on setting — Montana's rules don't bend
Don't ignore Board inquiries — Montana has reciprocity with ID and WA boards

name: MAT in Jails — Federal & State Framework (21 U.S.C. § 823; DEA)
holding: Medication-Assisted Treatment (MAT) with buprenorphine: DATA 2000 X-waiver REPEALED by Consolidated Appropriations Act 2023 — any DEA-registered practitioner with Schedule III authority can now prescribe buprenorphine without a separate X-waiver. Methadone for opioid use disorder still requires Opioid Treatment Program (OTP) certification. Naltrexone has no special restriction. Constitutional duty to provide MAT in jails is now well-established under Estelle framework — denying MAT to inmates with opioid use disorder can be deliberate indifference.
facts: The X-waiver removal in Jan 2023 dramatically simplified buprenorphine prescribing in jails. The constitutional duty side has matured through multiple federal cases (Pesce v. Coppinger, Smith v. Aroostook County, P.G. v. Jefferson County) — all finding denial of established MAT regimens to incoming inmates likely violates the ADA and § 1983.
reasoning: Federal policy has dramatically liberalized MAT access. Coupled with constitutional duty cases, jails are now squarely obligated to maintain (or initiate) MAT for inmates with OUD.
application: **Major operational area for Ivy Medical.** Most county jail inmates with opioid use disorder need MAT. Operational implications: (1) Ivy clinicians can prescribe buprenorphine without X-waiver if they have DEA Schedule III; (2) intake screening must identify inmates on existing MAT regimens — abrupt discontinuation is constitutionally risky; (3) protocols needed for buprenorphine induction, maintenance, and discontinuation in the jail context; (4) methadone requires OTP partnership (most jails don't run OTPs in-house); (5) ADA applies — MAT is treatment for OUD, which can be an ADA-qualifying disability. Build Ivy's MAT protocol now if not already in place. The litigation curve is sharply against jails that don't provide MAT.
dos: Identify inmates on existing MAT at intake — continuation is the safest path
Maintain Ivy's MAT protocol covering buprenorphine, methadone (via OTP partnership), and naltrexone
Ensure prescribing clinicians have DEA Schedule III registration for buprenorphine
Coordinate with community OTP for methadone continuation
Treat OUD as ADA-qualifying — accommodation framework applies
donts: Don't discontinue MAT abruptly at intake — that's the textbook deliberate indifference case
Don't require X-waiver for buprenorphine prescribing — it's been repealed
Don't deny MAT based on jail policy alone — constitutional and ADA exposure
Don't run methadone outside an OTP framework — federal regulation specifically requires it

name: Suicide Prevention in Jails — Constitutional & Standards Framework
cite: Estelle progeny; NCCHC suicide standards; state jail standards
holding: Constitutional duty to provide suicide prevention to detained persons is well-established under the Estelle/Farmer framework. Failure to: (1) screen for suicide risk at intake, (2) reassess on placement changes, (3) provide adequate observation for at-risk inmates, OR (4) train staff on suicide risk indicators can support deliberate indifference claims. The first 24-48 hours after intake are statistically the highest-risk window.
facts: Suicide is the leading cause of death in U.S. jails. Federal courts have consistently held that suicide prevention is part of the constitutional duty. NCCHC standards (which Ivy doesn't use formally per user note) and most state jail standards include specific suicide prevention requirements. The case law is rich and unforgiving toward jails that miss obvious warning signs.
reasoning: The convergence of (1) statistical reality that jail suicide is common, (2) screening tools that are widely available, and (3) intervention measures that are practical means courts find very little excuse for failure to implement basic suicide prevention.
application: **Highest-frequency adverse-event area in jail medicine.** Operational requirements that should be baseline for Ivy: (1) validated suicide risk screening at intake (e.g., Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale, or jail-specific tools); (2) reassessment at placement changes (segregation, lockdown, bad news); (3) communication protocol with custody for inmates flagged as risk; (4) observation level coordination (no fewer-than-15-minute checks for active risk, continuous for active intent); (5) suicide watch cells designed per standard (anti-ligature fixtures, smock-only clothing); (6) mental health follow-up within the window required by standards; (7) post-incident review with documented quality improvement. Every Ivy clinician working in a jail needs annual suicide risk training. The case law makes clear: warning signs that staff documented but didn't act on are the single most common Monell theory.
dos: Use validated suicide risk screening at intake — Columbia or jail-validated equivalent
Reassess risk at placement changes (segregation, lockdown, court denials)
Train every clinical staff member annually on suicide risk indicators
Coordinate observation level protocols with custody — written, signed, dated
Document the basis for downgrade decisions from active suicide watch
Conduct documented post-incident reviews with QI follow-through
donts: Don't downgrade observation level without documented clinical reassessment
Don't rely on inmate self-report alone — collateral data (family, prior records, staff observation) matters
Don't skip screening for short stays — the first 24-48 hours are highest risk
Don't conduct screening without privacy — accuracy depends on it
Don't let downgrade decisions be driven by housing pressure rather than clinical judgment

name: Jail Staff Background Checks (State Overlay over PREA + FCRA)
holding: Working in a jail requires layered background screening: (1) Ivy's hiring background check under FCRA; (2) PREA 115.17 sexual abuse history screening; (3) the facility's separate access clearance (typically county sheriff's office) — fingerprinting, FBI check, sometimes state-level investigation; (4) state-specific exclusions (e.g., disqualifying felony convictions, domestic violence convictions under the Lautenberg Amendment if firearm access is needed). Ivy's hiring decision does NOT guarantee facility access.
facts: The layered nature of correctional staff vetting creates operational complexity. An applicant Ivy wants to hire may fail facility-access clearance; an applicant cleared by Ivy may be denied access for reasons that don't show in Ivy's checks (e.g., prior disciplinary history at another jail, gang affiliation, family connections to current inmates).
reasoning: Custody facilities maintain access control independently of contractor hiring because the security implications are facility-specific.
application: **Critical workflow consideration for Ivy Medical's hiring process.** Operational steps: (1) Make Ivy job offers contingent on facility access clearance — every offer letter must include this language; (2) coordinate Ivy's background check timing with the facility's clearance process to avoid duplication; (3) communicate to the candidate that two separate processes apply; (4) document Ivy's hiring decision separately from the facility's clearance decision; (5) understand that a facility's clearance denial isn't itself a basis for Ivy to fire — but absence of facility access makes the position untenable. Build a written clearance-failure protocol: if a hire fails facility clearance after Ivy hires them, the position is impossible to perform; treat as a no-fault separation if possible.
dos: Make all Ivy job offers contingent on facility access clearance — explicit language in offer letter
Run Ivy's background check and PREA 115.17 screening in parallel with facility clearance
Communicate the two-track process clearly to candidates
Document Ivy's hiring rationale separately from the facility's access decision
Maintain a written protocol for clearance-failure separations
donts: Don't assume Ivy clearance = facility clearance
Don't extend a start date until facility clearance is confirmed
Don't use facility-clearance denial as a basis for Ivy adverse action without separate cause
Don't blur the lines between Ivy's hiring decision and the sheriff's access decision in documentation

name: Lautenberg Amendment Firearm Ban (18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(9))
holding: Anyone convicted of a misdemeanor crime of domestic violence is prohibited from possessing firearms or ammunition under federal law. No 'public interest' exception for law enforcement or corrections.
facts: The Lautenberg Amendment closed the gap where law enforcement / military / corrections had been exempt from the federal firearms prohibition. Now applies to everyone with a qualifying conviction.
reasoning: Domestic violence conviction creates federally-recognized firearm ineligibility.
application: For Ivy Medical: clinical staff in jails generally don't carry firearms (Ivy's clinical staff typically don't), but the principle matters because (1) some Ivy roles MAY have firearm access (executive protection, specific transport contexts); (2) facility access policies sometimes have parallel restrictions even where firearms aren't carried. Background checks should surface qualifying domestic violence convictions. This is rare in clinical hiring but worth being aware of as part of the layered screening framework.
dos: Surface qualifying domestic violence convictions in background screening
Coordinate with facility on firearm-adjacent roles if any apply to Ivy clinical staff
donts: Don't ignore misdemeanor DV convictions — federal firearm prohibition is automatic
Don't assume 'misdemeanor' = 'not disqualifying' — Lautenberg is federally enforced

name: WA Mandatory Reporting in Correctional Healthcare (RCW 26.44, 74.34)
holding: WA's mandatory reporting laws apply to healthcare professionals in any setting including corrections: RCW 26.44 requires reporting suspected child abuse/neglect; RCW 74.34 requires reporting suspected abuse/neglect of vulnerable adults. Corrections setting doesn't carve healthcare workers out — Ivy clinicians remain mandatory reporters. In addition, PREA requires reporting of inmate sexual abuse regardless of state law.
facts: WA's mandatory reporting framework covers all healthcare professionals. The reporting obligations exist independently of whether the abuse occurred inside or outside the facility — an inmate disclosing past child abuse triggers a report; an inmate disclosing current vulnerable-adult abuse triggers a report.
reasoning: Mandatory reporting framework is designed to capture disclosures regardless of setting because healthcare providers are uniquely positioned to learn of abuse.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA clinical staff: mandatory reporter obligations apply. Common scenarios in jails: (1) inmate discloses past or ongoing abuse of a child not currently in custody — RCW 26.44 report to DSHS/CPS; (2) inmate discloses vulnerable adult abuse (typically against a family member or caregiver) — RCW 74.34 report to DSHS; (3) staff observation suggests in-custody abuse — PREA + state-law report; (4) inmate discloses past abuse with no current safety concern — still triggers report under WA framework. Train clinical staff on the dual obligations: state-law mandatory reporting AND PREA. Document the report (who, when, what was reported) — failure to report is criminal in WA (gross misdemeanor for first offense).
dos: Train every clinical staff member on WA mandatory reporting obligations
Document every report (recipient agency, date, content reported)
Coordinate state-law reporting with PREA reporting for in-custody incidents
Maintain a list of reporting contacts (DSHS CPS, APS, local law enforcement)
donts: Don't treat the jail setting as a carve-out from mandatory reporting — it isn't
Don't delay reporting to coordinate with custody — your duty runs to the reporting agency
Don't fail to document the report — that's how prosecutors prove non-reporting

name: Idaho Mandatory Reporting in Correctional Healthcare (I.C. § 16-1605, § 39-5303)
holding: Idaho's mandatory reporting framework applies to healthcare professionals in correctional settings: I.C. § 16-1605 requires reporting suspected child abuse to Idaho Department of Health & Welfare; I.C. § 39-5303 covers vulnerable adult abuse. Failure to report is a misdemeanor.
facts: Same operational framework as WA. Idaho reporting agencies are H&W (Health & Welfare) for both child and adult abuse.
reasoning: Standard mandatory reporting framework.
application: Same operational guidance as WA. Idaho clinical staff are mandatory reporters regardless of correctional setting. Report to Idaho H&W (children) or H&W APS (vulnerable adults). Document every report. Coordinate with PREA for in-custody incidents.
dos: Apply Idaho mandatory reporting to clinical staff in correctional settings
Report to Idaho Department of Health & Welfare per statute
Document every report
donts: Don't assume Idaho's narrower mandatory reporter list excludes correctional clinical staff — it doesn't
Don't delay reporting for custody coordination

name: Montana Mandatory Reporting in Correctional Healthcare (MCA § 41-3-201, § 52-3-811)
holding: Montana mandatory reporting applies to healthcare professionals in correctional settings: MCA § 41-3-201 (child abuse) reports to Montana Department of Public Health & Human Services (DPHHS); MCA § 52-3-811 (vulnerable adult) reports to Adult Protective Services.
facts: Same operational structure as Idaho and WA. Montana adds specific provisions for reporting communicable disease in correctional settings under MCA § 50-18.
reasoning: Standard mandatory reporting framework with public health overlay.
application: Same operational guidance — Montana clinical staff are mandatory reporters. Reports go to DPHHS (children) or APS (vulnerable adults). Additional communicable disease reporting under MCA § 50-18 (TB especially relevant in jails). Document all reports.
dos: Apply Montana mandatory reporting in correctional settings
Report communicable disease per MCA § 50-18 (especially TB in jail setting)
Coordinate reporting with PREA
donts: Don't miss the communicable disease reporting layer — jails have elevated TB risk
Don't delay child or vulnerable adult abuse reports for custody coordination

name: Clinician Role in Use of Force — Limits and Documentation
cite: Constitutional + professional ethics + Montana Jail Standard 07.10.01
holding: Clinical staff have specific role limitations in use-of-force events: (1) they may evaluate inmates for ability to be restrained, but the decision to use force is custody's; (2) they may order medical restraints for medical reasons only (e.g., to prevent self-injury), not for security reasons; (3) they document injuries from force events; (4) they should NOT direct, witness for, or otherwise participate in force-for-control. Professional ethics codes (AMA, ANA) reinforce these limits.
facts: Use-of-force events are common in jails. Clinical staff are frequently present afterward and sometimes during. The dual-loyalty problem (custody employer vs. patient duty) creates ethics traps.
reasoning: Professional ethics and constitutional caselaw converge on a narrow clinical role in force events. Going beyond this role creates both ethical and legal exposure.
application: For Ivy Medical clinicians: train staff on the role boundaries. Specific guidance: (1) post-force medical assessment — objective documentation only (see Hudson v. McMillian); (2) medical restraints (e.g., 4-point restraint of a self-harming patient) must be clinically indicated, time-limited, documented, and reviewed; (3) clinicians should NOT clear pepper spray use, taser use, or other force in advance — that's custody's call; (4) clinicians should NOT participate in cell extractions in any direct way — observer only; (5) if asked to certify an inmate as 'fit for solitary confinement,' decline — that's a custody decision, not a clinical one. Maintain a written clinical scope-in-force-events policy.
dos: Maintain a written clinical scope policy for use-of-force events
Train clinicians to do objective post-incident assessment without role-creep
Order medical restraints only for medical indications, with time limits and reassessment
Document the medical/clinical basis for any restraint authorization
donts: Don't 'clear' inmates for force in advance — that's a clinician overreach
Don't authorize restraints for security purposes — only medical
Don't participate in cell extractions beyond observer/post-incident assessment
Don't certify 'fitness for solitary' — out of scope and ethically problematic

name: WA Controlled Substances Act in Corrections (RCW 69.50)
holding: WA Uniform Controlled Substances Act (RCW 69.50) applies in correctional settings with additional security requirements. Schedule II-V controlled substances must be stored, dispensed, and disposed per WAC 246-887 (Pharmacy Quality Assurance Commission rules) AND meet the facility's heightened security requirements. Diversion is a constant threat — inmates manipulating medical staff to obtain controlled substances or stealing during administration.
facts: Correctional medication management creates unique controlled-substance exposure. Both state pharmacy law and federal DEA rules apply. Standard practices: 'cheek and tongue' checks, crushed/floated medications for high-diversion-risk drugs (opioids, benzos, stimulants), keep-on-person (KOP) medications limited to low-risk inmates.
reasoning: Inmate population has elevated rates of substance use disorder and incentive to obtain controlled substances. Standard pharmacy security is insufficient.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA jail operations: maintain controlled substance protocols specific to the correctional environment. Specific requirements: (1) double-lock controlled substance storage (state rule + facility rule); (2) two-person verification of count at shift change; (3) mouth checks after administration of opioids, benzos, stimulants; (4) discontinue KOP for any inmate showing diversion behavior; (5) DEA registration covers individual prescriber, not the facility — make sure prescribing clinicians' DEA registrations list correctional facility as an address if doing on-site prescribing; (6) Schedule II prescriptions still need full DEA-compliant ordering; (7) document every administration and refusal.
dos: Use double-lock controlled substance storage meeting state and facility requirements
Two-person count verification at shift change
Mouth checks after high-diversion-risk medication administration
Limit KOP medications based on individual diversion risk assessment
Document every administration and refusal in the MAR
donts: Don't apply standard hospital med-administration protocols in jails — security is different
Don't trust inmate self-report of medication use without verification
Don't extend KOP privileges to inmates with documented diversion behavior
Don't operate controlled-substance prescribing without explicit facility-address DEA registration

name: Idaho Controlled Substances in Corrections (I.C. § 37-2700; IDAPA 22.01.06)
holding: Idaho Uniform Controlled Substances Act (I.C. § 37-2700) and Pharmacy Board rules (IDAPA 22.01.06) apply in corrections. Same general framework as WA — controlled substance storage, dispensing, and disposal subject to state pharmacy law plus facility security overlay.
facts: Idaho's framework parallels other states. The Idaho Board of Pharmacy regulates pharmacist-level activities; nursing administration follows IDAPA 23.
reasoning: Standard controlled substance framework.
application: Same operational guidance as WA — double-lock storage, two-person verification, diversion controls, documented administration. Idaho's controlled substance prescribing is straightforward; APRN full-practice authority makes NP prescribing simpler operationally.
dos: Apply WA-equivalent controlled substance protocols in Idaho
Leverage Idaho APRN FPA for simplified NP controlled substance prescribing
donts: Don't relax security because Idaho has 'lighter' overall framework — diversion risk is the same

name: Montana Controlled Substances in Corrections (MCA § 50-32; ARM 24.174)
holding: Montana Uniform Controlled Substances Act (MCA § 50-32) and Board of Pharmacy rules (ARM 24.174) apply in correctional settings.
facts: Parallels Idaho and WA.
reasoning: Standard framework.
application: Same operational discipline as Idaho and WA. Montana APRN FPA helps NP staffing models.
dos: Apply consistent controlled substance protocols across all three states
Use Montana APRN FPA for NP staffing
donts: Don't vary controlled substance security by state — diversion risk is identical

name: Clinical Staff Safety in Jails — Convergence of OSHA, WC, and PREA
cite: OSHA general duty; state WC; PREA staff-on-staff/inmate-on-staff
holding: Clinical staff safety in jails involves convergence of: (1) OSHA General Duty Clause (29 U.S.C. § 654(a)(1)) — employer must provide workplace free of recognized hazards; (2) state workers' comp — coverage for assaults, exposure, mental health injury; (3) PREA staff sexual abuse protections; (4) state workplace violence prevention provisions where applicable. Ivy as the W-2 employer has primary OSHA and WC duty; the facility has overlapping duty.
facts: Correctional medical staff face elevated rates of: physical assault by inmates, sexual harassment/assault, exposure to bloodborne pathogens, infectious disease, and psychological injury (PTSD). The legal framework for staff safety is multi-source.
reasoning: Multiple regulatory frameworks converge because none was designed specifically for the correctional medical contractor scenario.
application: **Major obligation area for Ivy.** Operational requirements: (1) maintain OSHA-compliant safety program including bloodborne pathogen plan (29 CFR 1910.1030), PPE (29 CFR 1910.132), and workplace violence prevention; (2) ensure facility safety protocols are in writing — Ivy can't supervise the facility, but Ivy CAN document the safety expectations in the contract; (3) workers' comp coverage with correctional medicine classification; (4) PREA staff sexual abuse training (115.31, 115.32); (5) post-incident psychological support after assaults; (6) communicate to staff that they have the RIGHT to refuse to enter unsafe areas (e.g., disturbance in progress) and document the refusal protocol. The facility-Ivy interface is the highest-risk zone for staff injuries — coordinate.
dos: Maintain bloodborne pathogen, PPE, and workplace violence programs OSHA-compliant
Document facility safety expectations in Ivy-sheriff contracts
Provide post-incident psychological support after assaults (peer support, EAP, professional referral)
Train staff on right to refuse unsafe assignments and the protocol for doing so
Workers' comp classification appropriate for correctional medicine (elevated rates)
donts: Don't rely on the sheriff for OSHA compliance — Ivy is the W-2 employer with primary duty
Don't send staff into known disturbances or unsecured areas
Don't discount mental health injuries from inmate assault — those are workers' comp claims
Don't have staff working alone without communication tools in dangerous areas

name: Idaho Trade Secrets Act (I.C. § 48-801 to § 48-807)
holding: Idaho adopted the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Protects trade secrets against misappropriation by current or former employees and others. 'Trade secret' requires (1) information of economic value, (2) from not being generally known or reasonably ascertainable, AND (3) subject to reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Remedies include injunction, damages, and attorney's fees for willful misappropriation.
facts: Idaho's UTSA mirrors most other states. Paired with Idaho's noncompete framework (I.C. § 44-2701), trade secrets law provides employers with a tool to protect confidential information without relying solely on noncompetes.
reasoning: UTSA balances employee mobility with protection against bad-faith misappropriation of confidential business information.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho operations: UTSA is the primary tool for protecting clinical protocols, contract pricing models, sheriff-relationship information, and operational know-how. Pair with confidentiality agreements at hire. The 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy' element is the most-litigated — if Ivy claims something is a trade secret but treats it casually, the claim fails. Document the reasonable measures: confidentiality agreements, marked confidential materials, need-to-know access controls, password protection.
dos: Use written confidentiality agreements at hire for clinical and operational staff
Mark confidential documents (clinical protocols, contract terms, pricing) as confidential
Apply need-to-know access controls to sensitive information
Document the 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy' for trade-secret claims to hold up
donts: Don't claim trade secret status for information you treat casually — fatal to the claim
Don't use UTSA as a backdoor noncompete — courts will see through it
Don't rely solely on UTSA — pair with NDAs and specific protection of identified secrets

name: Idaho — No Personnel Records Access Statute (absence of law)
holding: Idaho has NO state statute requiring private employers to provide employees access to their personnel files. Unlike Washington (RCW 49.12.240, requiring 21-day production), Idaho leaves access entirely to employer discretion. Employees can obtain records through litigation discovery if a dispute arises, but no proactive access right exists.
facts: Idaho's silence on personnel records is consistent with its broader employer-friendly framework. About 20 states have access statutes; Idaho is not among them.
reasoning: Idaho's policy preference for limited statutory employer obligations.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho clinical staff: NOT required to provide personnel files on request, in contrast to WA's strict 21-day rule. PRACTICAL ADVICE: still provide records on reasonable request — refusing creates discovery disputes later and signals to plaintiffs' lawyers that the employer has something to hide. The cost of providing is near zero; the litigation value of having voluntarily provided is real. Create an internal policy that grants access even though Idaho doesn't require it. Critical inconsistency to avoid: don't provide records for some employees and refuse others — that creates discrimination claims.
dos: Create an internal personnel records access policy even though Idaho doesn't require one
Apply the policy consistently — same treatment for all employees and former employees
Maintain organized personnel files so requests are quick to fulfill
Document any denied request with specific business reason
donts: Don't refuse all requests as a blanket policy — looks suspicious in later litigation
Don't selectively grant or deny requests — discrimination exposure
Don't lose track of former employees' files — even Idaho's silence doesn't help if the file is gone

name: Idaho Protection of Public Employees Act (I.C. § 6-2101 to § 6-2109)
holding: Idaho's whistleblower protection applies to GOVERNMENTAL employees only — not private-sector. Protects public employees who report waste, violations of law, mismanagement, or abuses of authority. Idaho has NO general private-sector whistleblower statute, though the common-law public-policy exception to at-will employment provides limited protection.
facts: I.C. § 6-2101 covers state and local government employees but not contractors or private employees. For private-sector retaliation claims, Idaho relies on the common-law public policy tort (Sorensen v. Comm Tek, Inc., 118 Idaho 664) and federal whistleblower statutes (Sarbanes-Oxley, OSH Act § 11(c), FLSA § 15(a)(3), etc.).
reasoning: Idaho's policy choice to limit statutory whistleblower protection to public employees.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho clinical staff: Idaho's whistleblower statute doesn't apply because Ivy is private. BUT — clinical staff who report unsafe medical practices, fraud against Medicaid/Medicare, or constitutional violations in jails ARE protected through other channels: (1) common-law public-policy tort; (2) federal False Claims Act if reporting Medicare fraud; (3) federal OSH Act § 11(c) for safety reporting; (4) PREA retaliation prohibition for sexual abuse reporting; (5) Title VII for discrimination reporting. Train Idaho managers that the absence of a state whistleblower statute does NOT mean no protection — multiple federal frameworks apply.
dos: Train Idaho managers on federal whistleblower frameworks (OSH Act, FCA, PREA, Title VII)
Apply common-law public policy protection to clinical safety reports
Document the legitimate non-retaliatory rationale for any adverse action against a reporting employee
Treat clinical staff reports as protected even where Idaho state law is silent
donts: Don't assume Idaho's narrow statute means no protection — federal law fills the gap
Don't retaliate against clinical staff for safety reports — multiple frameworks apply
Don't underestimate the common-law public policy tort — Idaho courts recognize it

name: Idaho Wage Assignment & Consumer Credit (I.C. § 28-45)
holding: Idaho restricts assignment of future wages to satisfy consumer debt. Wage assignments require the employee's written consent and are limited in amount. Employers receiving wage assignment requests should verify the assignment meets statutory requirements before honoring.
facts: I.C. § 28-45 is the Idaho consumer credit chapter governing wage assignments separate from court-ordered garnishment. Less common than garnishment but operationally distinct.
reasoning: Consumer protection framework preventing creditors from compelling wage assignments without proper consent.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho operations: when receiving a wage assignment request (typically from a creditor or collection agency), verify it meets statutory requirements — written employee consent, proper amounts, lawful purpose. Don't honor improperly-formed wage assignments. Coordinate with Paylocity. Court-ordered garnishments (I.C. § 11-712) follow a separate, more robust framework.
dos: Verify written employee consent on any wage assignment request
Coordinate with Paylocity on assignment processing
Distinguish between voluntary wage assignment and court-ordered garnishment — different rules
donts: Don't honor wage assignments without verified employee consent
Don't process wage assignments through the same workflow as garnishments — they have different requirements

name: Montana Personnel Records & Service Letter (MCA § 39-2-801 to § 39-2-805)
holding: Montana requires employers, upon written request from a former employee, to provide a 'service letter' stating length of service, position held, and reason for separation (MCA § 39-2-801). Truthful reference statements are privileged (MCA § 39-2-802). Personnel records access for current employees is less explicit than WA's framework — practice is generally to allow reasonable access.
facts: MCA § 39-2-801 et seq. provides the service-letter framework. Unlike WA's specific 21-day production rule, Montana's framework focuses on the discharge documentation step.
reasoning: Montana's service-letter requirement gives former employees concrete documentation of their separation while protecting employers from defamation claims for truthful references.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana former employees: written requests for service letters trigger an obligation to respond with length of service, position, and reason for separation.
