The 'reason for separation' lock-in mirrors WA's 2025 written discharge statement requirement (RCW 49.12.241). Be consistent: the service letter, the discharge statement (if Montana ever adopts one), and the contemporaneous personnel file must all align. Use the truthful-reference privilege (§ 39-2-802) to support honest but non-defamatory reference disclosures.
dos: Respond to service-letter requests with accurate length of service, position, and reason
Apply truthful-reference privilege to honest reference disclosures
Maintain consistency across service letters, references, and personnel files
Build a service-letter response template aligned with WDEA documentation standards
donts: Don't refuse a properly-made service-letter request — statutory obligation
Don't provide a discharge reason that contradicts contemporaneous documentation
Don't share information beyond the statutory scope — defamation risk if not covered by the privilege

name: Montana Off-Duty Conduct Protection (MCA § 39-2-313)
holding: Already in IMELD as 'mt-political-activity' for the political-activity dimension. Note: § 39-2-313's protection extends beyond pure political activity — it broadly protects against discrimination for lawful conduct outside of work. Montana courts have applied it to off-duty alcohol use, recreational activities, and personal associations. Combined with MHRA and WDEA, Montana off-duty conduct receives unusually strong protection.
facts: The political-activity dimension of § 39-2-313 was captured in the existing entry. This cross-reference notes the broader off-duty-conduct application that Montana courts have developed.
reasoning: Montana's policy of workplace non-interference with personal lives extends beyond political activity to encompass other lawful off-duty conduct.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana clinical staff: assume that ANY off-duty lawful conduct is protected, not just political activity. Common examples of protected off-duty conduct that look 'manageable' but aren't: (1) social media posts about hobbies, lifestyle, or personal beliefs; (2) recreational marijuana use in legalization states (note: Montana has medical and limited recreational, but federal Schedule I status and employer drug-testing rights complicate this — see related entries); (3) personal associations with controversial figures; (4) participation in lawful protests, including outside of political contexts. Discipline for off-duty conduct in Montana requires demonstrated workplace impact — and even then, multiple frameworks intersect (§ 39-2-313, MHRA political beliefs, WDEA good cause).
dos: Train Montana managers that off-duty lawful conduct is broadly protected
Require demonstrated workplace impact for any off-duty-conduct discipline
Coordinate with legal counsel before any discipline based on off-duty conduct
Document workplace-impact specifically — abstract 'professionalism' concerns are insufficient
donts: Don't read § 39-2-313 as limited to political activity — Montana courts apply it broadly
Don't discipline based on social media off-duty content without workplace-impact evidence
Don't extend Idaho or WA off-duty discipline flexibility to Montana

name: Montana Whistleblower Protection (within WDEA, MCA § 39-2-904)
holding: Montana's Wrongful Discharge from Employment Act (WDEA) defines wrongful discharge to include termination 'in retaliation for the employee's refusal to violate public policy or for reporting a violation of public policy.' This is a STATUTORY whistleblower protection embedded in WDEA, applicable to all post-probation private employees. Idaho has nothing comparable; WA has narrower whistleblower statutes for specific industries (healthcare, public).
facts: WDEA § 39-2-904(1)(a) integrates whistleblower protection into Montana's broader good-cause termination framework. The protection covers refusal to violate public policy AND reporting violations.
reasoning: Montana's WDEA framework folds whistleblower protection into the general wrongful-discharge framework rather than creating a separate statute.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana clinical staff: any post-probation employee terminated after reporting a violation of public policy (clinical safety, fraud, constitutional violations, illegal conduct) has a WDEA claim. The 'public policy' element is broad — Montana courts have applied it to clinical safety reports, billing fraud reports, OSHA violation reports, and others. Practical implication: document any clinical-safety or compliance report carefully, treat the reporter as protected, and pause any post-report adverse action for legal review. The combination of WDEA whistleblower protection PLUS § 39-2-313 off-duty conduct protection PLUS MHRA makes Montana terminations require more thorough analysis than ID or WA.
dos: Treat any compliance, safety, or public-policy report as triggering WDEA whistleblower protection
Document the pre-existing non-retaliatory rationale for any adverse action against a reporter
Pause adverse actions within 60-90 days of a report for legal review
Apply WDEA's broader public-policy lens — not limited to formal regulatory reports
donts: Don't treat WDEA whistleblower protection as narrower than the case law makes it
Don't terminate a Montana employee post-report without thorough documentation
Don't underestimate the stacking — WDEA + § 39-2-313 + MHRA in Montana is serious exposure

name: Montana Independent Contractor Exemption Certificate (MCA § 39-71-417)
holding: Montana is one of few states with a formal Independent Contractor Exemption Certificate process. Workers seeking to operate as independent contractors must apply to the Department of Labor & Industry for a certificate that establishes their IC status for workers' comp and unemployment purposes. Without a certificate, the worker is generally presumed an employee.
facts: MCA § 39-71-417 creates the certificate process. The exemption certificate provides a safe harbor for both the worker and the contracting party. Montana's approach is unusually formal compared to other states' totality-of-circumstances tests.
reasoning: Montana chose a certificate-based system to reduce misclassification disputes and provide certainty for both sides.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana operations: any clinical staff classified as 1099 must have an Independent Contractor Exemption Certificate. Without the certificate, the presumption is employee status for WC and UI purposes — meaning premiums are owed and Ivy could be liable for unpaid amounts. Practical: most Ivy clinical staff are W-2 (correctly), so this is rarely a direct issue. BUT — if Ivy ever uses a 1099 contractor for short-term coverage, locum work, or any clinical role in Montana, verify the certificate is in place before the engagement starts. The certificate is the worker's responsibility but Ivy should verify.
dos: Verify Independent Contractor Exemption Certificate for any 1099 engagement in Montana
Default to W-2 classification for clinical staff in correctional settings (correct framework anyway)
Document verification of the certificate in the engagement file
Coordinate with the IC's tax/business setup to ensure proper certificate maintenance
donts: Don't classify clinical staff as 1099 in Montana without a certificate — presumed employee
Don't rely on the worker's self-classification — verify the certificate
Don't apply WA-style ABC test reasoning to Montana — the certificate framework is different

name: Montana Drug Testing (MCA § 39-2-205 to § 39-2-211)
holding: Montana's workplace drug testing statute is among the most restrictive in the country. PRE-EMPLOYMENT drug testing is permitted only for 'safety-sensitive' positions, hazardous-work positions, and a few enumerated categories. Random drug testing of current employees is generally prohibited except in specific contexts. Reasonable suspicion testing requires documented behavioral signs. Montana also has medical cannabis (MCA § 50-46-301) creating layered analysis for cannabis testing.
facts: MCA § 39-2-205 et seq. restricts employer drug testing in ways that surprise most multi-state employers. The 'safety-sensitive' definition is statutory and narrow.
reasoning: Montana's policy preference for employee privacy in drug testing matters, with carve-outs for genuine safety contexts.
application: **Major operational issue for Ivy Medical.** For Montana clinical staff: pre-employment drug testing requires safety-sensitive classification. Correctional medical staff — strong case for safety-sensitive (patient safety, controlled substance handling, working with vulnerable population). DOCUMENT this analysis for each position. Random testing of current employees is essentially off the table outside narrow exceptions. Reasonable suspicion testing requires documented behavioral signs — not just hunches. Medical cannabis use creates layered analysis: legal under state law, federally illegal, employer can still test for cannabis but should coordinate with safety-sensitive analysis. The contrast with Idaho (broad employer testing under safe harbor) and WA (restricted pre-employment cannabis but otherwise permitted) makes Montana the strictest of the three.
dos: Document the safety-sensitive analysis for every Montana position subject to drug testing
Apply correctional medical safety-sensitive classification with specific job-duty justification
Use reasonable-suspicion testing with documented behavioral signs only
Coordinate with medical cannabis use questions through ADA accommodation analysis
donts: Don't apply Idaho-style broad pre-employment testing to Montana — major exposure
Don't conduct random testing of Montana clinical staff without statutory authorization
Don't test for reasonable suspicion without documented behavioral evidence
Don't treat Montana drug testing as similar to WA's — it's substantially stricter

name: Montana Background Check Framework (FCRA + MCA § 31-3)
holding: Montana does NOT have a state ban-the-box statute for private employers. Federal FCRA governs third-party background check procedures (stand-alone disclosure, written consent, pre-adverse action notice, dispute window). Montana consumer credit chapter (MCA § 31-3) adds state-level consumer protection but doesn't directly restrict private-employer hiring inquiries. Montana state government has internal ban-the-box policies but they don't bind private employers.
facts: Montana's private-employer hiring framework parallels Idaho's — comparatively unrestricted, with FCRA applying nationwide.
reasoning: Montana preserved private-employer flexibility while federal FCRA standardizes procedural protections.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana hiring: can inquire about criminal history on the initial application (no state ban-the-box for private employers). MUST comply with federal FCRA for third-party checks (stand-alone disclosure form, written consent, pre-adverse action notice, dispute window). For correctional medical staff, the jail's facility-access clearance is SEPARATE — Ivy's hiring decision and the sheriff's clearance are independent processes. PREA 115.17 screening applies in addition to FCRA.
dos: Use stand-alone FCRA disclosure form for any third-party background check
Provide pre-adverse action notice with copy of report and dispute window
Apply PREA 115.17 screening in parallel with FCRA
Coordinate Ivy's hiring decision with the jail's separate facility-access process
donts: Don't embed FCRA disclosure in the application — must be stand-alone
Don't skip pre-adverse action notice and dispute window — FCRA enforcement is active
Don't conflate Ivy's hiring with the jail's clearance — separate processes

name: Montana Wage Notice at Hire (MCA § 39-3-204 implicit; ARM 24.16)
holding: Montana does NOT have a free-standing wage-notice-at-hire statute like Idaho's I.C. § 45-617. Wage rate notification at hire is implied by general wage-payment requirements (MCA § 39-3-204) and best practice. The Wage Protection Act requires posted notice of rates and paydays.
facts: Montana's wage payment framework relies on posted notice and the implicit obligation to communicate wage rates rather than a specific at-hire disclosure statute.
reasoning: Montana's framework focuses on posted notice and pay-cycle clarity rather than at-hire documentation.
application: For Ivy Medical's Montana hires: even without a statute, document wage rates in offer letters or signed hire paperwork. Best practice mirrors Idaho's I.C. § 45-617: include hourly rate, differentials (call, weekend, holiday), pay frequency, paydays. The documentation matters less for compliance (Montana doesn't penalize the omission) and more for dispute resolution. Combined with the Wage Protection Act and immediate-pay rule at termination, Montana's wage framework rewards documentation discipline.
dos: Include all wage info in Montana offer letters or hire paperwork even though not required
Post wage rate and payday notices at workplaces
Use consistent wage agreement templates across states
Document any rate changes in writing with employee acknowledgment
donts: Don't rely on Montana's softer notice framework as license to be sloppy — the WPA + immediate-pay rule make documentation important
Don't change rates without written notice

name: WA Salary History Ban (RCW 49.58.100)
holding: WA prohibits employers from seeking applicants' wage or salary history during the hiring process. Cannot ask the applicant or their current/former employers about salary history. Employer may confirm the applicant's wage/salary expectations and may discuss applicant's anticipated salary. Penalty up to $5,000 per violation plus actual damages and attorney's fees.
facts: RCW 49.58.100 is part of WA's broader pay equity framework. Closes the loop with EPOA's pay transparency provisions — employers can't use salary history to perpetuate pay disparities.
reasoning: Salary history questions disproportionately disadvantage women and minorities by anchoring offers to prior (potentially discriminatory) pay rates.
application: For Ivy Medical's WA hiring: NEVER ask about salary history. Train recruiters and managers — this is one of the most common interview slip-ups. Acceptable alternatives: 'What are your salary expectations?' or 'What range are you targeting?' Cannot ask the applicant's current or former employer either. If an applicant volunteers salary history, document that the information was volunteered (not solicited) and don't use it as the basis for the offer. Combined with EPOA's pay-range disclosure requirement, the WA framework is: post a good-faith range, ask about expectations, make an offer in or above the range.
dos: Train recruiters and managers to never ask about salary history
Use 'What are your salary expectations?' as the standard alternative
Document when an applicant volunteers salary history (not solicited)
Make offers based on posted ranges and applicant expectations, not history
donts: Don't ask 'what's your current salary' or 'what did you make at your last job' — direct violations
Don't ask former employers about salary history — equally prohibited
Don't use volunteered salary history to anchor the offer downward

name: WA Independent Contractor Framework — 2024 Updates (RCW 51.08.195, 50.04.140)
holding: Cross-reference to existing entry 'wa-abc-test-independent-contractor.' WA's six-part test for IC classification under WC and UI is among the strictest in the country. 2024 saw continued litigation and enforcement activity but no major statutory amendments. The combined effect: WA correctly classifies most healthcare workers as employees because the six-part test is hard to satisfy in clinical settings.
facts: Existing entry covers the substantive framework. This entry exists for index completeness in Pass 1C.
reasoning: Cross-reference.
application: See 'wa-abc-test-independent-contractor' for full analysis. For Ivy Medical's WA operations: continue defaulting clinical staff to W-2 classification. 1099 engagements in WA require specific six-part test analysis with documentation.
dos: See related entry for full do/don't list
donts: Don't apply Idaho-style 1099 flexibility to WA — six-part test is much stricter

name: Idaho Personnel File Maintenance — Best Practice (no statute)
cite: No state statute; federal recordkeeping requirements apply
holding: Idaho has no state personnel-records access statute (see 'idaho-no-personnel-records-statute'). Federal recordkeeping requirements still apply: FLSA (29 CFR 516) — pay records for 3 years; ADA — medical records separate confidential file; OSHA — injury records 5 years; I-9 — 3 years post-hire or 1 year post-termination, whichever later; FMLA — 3 years.
facts: Despite Idaho's silence on employee access, federal recordkeeping obligations create the de facto framework. The records exist regardless of access rules.
reasoning: Federal compliance creates the baseline; state silence doesn't excuse federal obligations.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho operations: maintain personnel files compliant with federal recordkeeping rules even though Idaho doesn't require employee access. Specific practice: (1) main personnel file (application, evaluations, discipline, separation); (2) separate ADA medical file (confidential, restricted access); (3) separate I-9 file (best practice — easier to produce for audit); (4) separate investigation file when applicable; (5) maintain records for at least 7 years post-separation as a baseline (covers most state statutes of limitations). Coordinate with the internal access policy from 'idaho-no-personnel-records-statute.'
dos: Maintain personnel files compliant with federal FLSA, ADA, OSHA, I-9, FMLA recordkeeping rules
Keep ADA medical records in a separate confidential file
Keep I-9 files separate for easier audit production
Hold records 7+ years post-separation as a baseline
donts: Don't co-mingle ADA medical records with the general personnel file
Don't destroy records to thwart access — federal retention rules apply
Don't treat Idaho's silence as 'no obligations' — federal rules still apply

name: Idaho Pregnancy Accommodation — Federal PWFA Applies (no state law)
court: Idaho — no state statute; federal PWFA applies
holding: Idaho does NOT have a state pregnancy accommodation statute. The federal Pregnant Workers Fairness Act (PWFA, effective June 27, 2023) provides the substantive framework. PWFA covers employers with 15+ employees and requires reasonable accommodation for pregnancy, childbirth, and related medical conditions absent undue hardship.
facts: Idaho's silence on pregnancy accommodation is consistent with its general approach of not exceeding federal floors. PWFA fills the gap.
reasoning: Idaho's policy preference for federal-only frameworks in employment protection.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho clinical staff: apply federal PWFA at 15+ employees. Reasonable accommodation for pregnancy may include: schedule adjustments, light-duty assignments, additional bathroom breaks, sit/stand options, leave for prenatal appointments, leave for recovery, lactation accommodations. The interactive process applies similar to ADA. No state-law overlay in Idaho (unlike WA's pregnancy-specific provisions in WLAD and other states with stronger frameworks).
dos: Apply federal PWFA at 15+ employees regardless of Idaho silence
Conduct interactive process for pregnancy accommodation requests
Document accommodation determinations
Apply WA's broader pregnancy framework when staff work across state lines (rare for jail-based staff)
donts: Don't read Idaho's silence as 'no obligation' — PWFA fills the gap
Don't apply ADA standards to pregnancy without recognizing PWFA's lower threshold (lower than ADA's 'disability')
Don't decline accommodation without genuine undue hardship analysis

name: Idaho Final Pay — 48-Hour Acceleration Detail (I.C. § 45-606)
holding: Existing entry 'idaho-45-606-wage-payment' covers the general rule. Specific detail worth flagging: an employee who QUITS may request final pay within 48 hours by written demand. Without the demand, pay is due by the earlier of the next payday or 10 working days. An employee who is DISCHARGED has no 48-hour acceleration option — pay is due by the earlier of next payday or 10 working days. The 48-hour rule applies only to voluntary separations with written demand.
facts: The 48-hour written-demand acceleration is a frequently-misunderstood provision. The rule is specific to quits.
reasoning: Idaho allows quitting employees to accelerate final pay through written demand while preserving the default 10-day window for unsolicited final-pay scenarios.
application: For Ivy Medical's Idaho separations: train Paylocity and HR on the distinction. A quit + written 48-hour demand = 48-hour processing. A quit without demand OR a discharge = 10 working days or next payday. Build a written-demand form so employees who want acceleration have a standard path, and the company has documentation. The demand triggers a real obligation — missing the 48-hour window after a proper demand creates penalties.
dos: Distinguish quit (48-hour demand available) from discharge (no acceleration)
Build a written-demand form for quits seeking acceleration
Honor 48-hour demands when properly made
Train payroll on the trigger and timeline
donts: Don't honor 48-hour acceleration for discharge cases — not available
Don't ignore a properly-made 48-hour demand — penalties accrue
Don't process all final pay on the same timeline — quit-with-demand is faster

name: Montana Final Pay Written Policy Requirement (MCA § 39-3-205)
holding: Cross-reference to existing entries 'mt-39-3-201-wage-payment' and 'mt-wage-claim.' Critical nuance: Montana's immediate-pay rule for involuntary termination (MCA § 39-3-205) defaults to immediate. The employer can extend to the next regular payday OR within 15 days by establishing a WRITTEN POLICY. Without the written policy, the immediate-pay default applies. Many employers learn this the hard way after a termination.
facts: Existing entries capture the framework. This entry exists to emphasize the written-policy requirement, which is the #1 operational risk in Montana wage payment.
reasoning: Cross-reference and emphasis.
application: **HIGHEST-PRIORITY ACTION for Ivy Medical's Montana operations.** Verify that Ivy's employee handbook contains a written policy extending final pay timing under § 39-3-205. If not, ADD IT IMMEDIATELY. Without the written policy, every involuntary termination in Montana triggers same-day pay obligation — meaning Paylocity must cut a manual check, the manager must hand-deliver, and any delay creates a Wage Protection Act claim with penalties. The written policy can extend to the next regular payday OR 15 days, whichever is earlier. Treat this as a critical compliance check.
dos: Verify written final-pay policy is in Ivy's Montana handbook — TODAY
Set the policy to next-payday-or-15-days, whichever is earlier
Audit handbook annually to ensure the policy persists through revisions
Train HR on the immediate-pay default if policy is absent
donts: Don't terminate a Montana employee without confirming the written policy is in place
Don't assume the policy persists through every handbook revision — verify
Don't conflate this with Idaho's 10-day or WA's end-of-pay-period rules — Montana is different
